recipes.name: LB (Lysogeny Broth), Miller
recipes.summary: General-purpose rich medium for routine growth of E. coli and other non-fastidious bacteria; the default cloning/plasmid workhorse.

components.name: Tryptone
components.note: Pancreatic digest of casein (Bacto Tryptone or equivalent).

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 10 g/L = 171 mM. This high-salt level is what defines the Miller formulation.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (approx. 0.2 mL/L); HCl to lower
recipes.storage: room temperature after autoclaving, or 4 C; keep sealed and dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as dehydrated powder; sterile broth several months at room temperature if unopened and not cloudy
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 25 g/L (Sigma L3522).
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L). For top/overlay agar use 0.7% w/v (7 g/L).
Miller = 10 g/L NaCl; Lennox = 5 g/L; Luria/low-salt = 0.5 g/L. Only the NaCl differs.
pH adjustment is optional for most routine E. coli work - unadjusted LB sits near pH 6.8-7.0.
Add heat-labile antibiotics only after the medium has cooled below ~50 C.
Do not use high-salt Miller LB when selecting with salt-sensitive antibiotics (zeocin, hygromycin, phleomycin) - use the Luria/low-salt version.

sources.title: Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning: A Laboratory Manual, 3rd ed. - LB (Luria-Bertani) medium recipe (Cold Spring Harbor Protocols)
Sigma-Aldrich L3522, LB Broth (Miller) product page - composition and preparation
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth (Miller/Lennox/Luria variants)

recipes.name: LB (Lysogeny Broth), Lennox
recipes.summary: Reduced-salt LB; standard for phage P1 work, salt-sensitive antibiotics, and many protein-expression protocols.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 5 g/L = 86 mM - half the Miller level.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C, dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder; several months sterile at room temperature
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 20 g/L (Sigma L3022).
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L); for overlay/top agar 0.7% w/v (7 g/L).

Lennox's original 1955 formulation also contained 1 g/L glucose; virtually all modern commercial 'LB Lennox' powders omit it. Add 1 g/L D-glucose only if you specifically want the historical formulation.
Preferred over Miller for zeocin, hygromycin B and phleomycin selection, which lose activity at high ionic strength.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich L3022, LB Broth (Lennox) product page - composition and preparation
Lennox ES (1955) Transduction of linked genetic characters of the host by bacteriophage P1. Virology 1:190-206
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth (Miller/Lennox/Luria variants)

recipes.name: LB (Lysogeny Broth), Luria / low-salt
recipes.summary: Very low salt LB used when the selection agent (zeocin, phleomycin, hygromycin) is inhibited by NaCl.
recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C, dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder
notes: CORRECTED ATTRIBUTION: the 0.5 g/L NaCl figure above is the classic 'LB Luria' formulation, NOT Invitrogen/Thermo's 'Low Salt LB'. Invitrogen's Low Salt LB for Zeocin is 10 g/L tryptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, 5 g/L NaCl (i.e. Lennox-level salt), pH adjusted to 7.5 with NaOH. An earlier version of this entry cited Thermo as the source for the 0.5 g/L value, which it is not.

The actual Zeocin requirement is a threshold, not a specific recipe: salt < 110 mM AND pH 7.5. Miller LB (171 mM) fails it; Lennox LB (86 mM) already passes it; this Luria formulation (8.6 mM) passes it comfortably. Any of the low-salt options works provided you set the pH.

For zeocin selection, pH matters as much as salt: adjust to pH 7.5 (not the 7.0 listed in finalPH above, which is the generic Luria-LB value) before autoclaving, since zeocin activity falls sharply below pH 7. Cool to below 55 C before adding Zeocin to 25 ug/mL final.
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L).
Growth yields are somewhat lower than Miller LB for some strains.

sources.title: Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher - Zeocin Selection Reagent User Guide (MAN0000019): Low Salt LB = 10 g tryptone, 5 g yeast extract, 5 g NaCl per L, pH 7.5; requires salt < 110 mM and pH 7.5; Zeocin 25 ug/mL
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth (Miller/Lennox/Luria variants)

recipes.name: LB Agar (Miller) - plates
recipes.summary: Standard solid LB for pouring selective and non-selective plates.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 15 g/L. Use 1.5% for standard plates; 1.2% gives a softer plate that is easier to replica-plate; 2% for plates incubated at 42 C or for swarming-prone strains.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl (adjust before adding agar)
recipes.storage: poured plates inverted at 4 C in a sealed bag; bring to room temperature before use
recipes.shelfLife: poured plates 1-3 months at 4 C (antibiotic plates shorter: ampicillin ~1 month, others 2-3 months)
recipes.hazards: molten agar is a scald hazard; wear heat-resistant gloves
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 40 g/L (Miller LB agar).
Cool to 50-55 C before adding antibiotics, IPTG or X-gal, then pour.

A 1 L batch pours roughly 40 standard 90-100 mm plates at ~25 mL each, or up to ~50 at 20 mL each. (Corrected: the previous '40-45 plates at ~25 mL' was self-inconsistent - 45 x 25 mL = 1125 mL, more than the batch.)
Swirl gently when mixing after autoclaving - vigorous mixing traps bubbles that solidify on the plate surface.
Do not re-autoclave agar repeatedly; hydrolysis weakens the gel.

sources.title: Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning, 3rd ed., Appendix 2 (media) - LB agar
American Society for Microbiology - Luria Broth (LB) and Luria Agar (LA) Media and Their Uses protocol

recipes.name: LB Agar (Lennox) - plates
recipes.summary: Solid Lennox LB for plating when reduced NaCl is preferred (phage work, salt-sensitive selection).

components.name: Agar
components.note: 15 g/L for standard plates.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.storage: poured plates inverted at 4 C, sealed
recipes.shelfLife: 1-3 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: molten agar scald hazard
notes: Total dehydrated powder equivalent is 35 g/L.
Use as the bottom layer for phage plaque assays with a 0.7% top agar overlay of the same base.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich L2897, LB Agar (Lennox) product page
Protocols Online - Lysogeny broth

recipes.name: LB Top Agar (soft agar overlay, 0.7%)
recipes.summary: Semi-solid LB overlay poured over LB plates for phage plaque assays, transposon/library plating and lawn formation.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: Lennox-level salt is the usual base for phage overlays; use 10 g/L if you want a Miller base.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 7 g/L. This is the key difference from plates (1.5%). Published overlays range 0.4-0.8%; 0.7% is the most common.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH / HCl
recipes.storage: solidified bottles at room temperature; remelt in a microwave or boiling water bath and hold at 45-50 C in a water bath before use
recipes.shelfLife: several months as sealed sterile bottles; remelt no more than a few times
recipes.hazards: molten agar scald hazard
notes: Hold at 45-48 C before mixing with cells - hotter than ~50 C kills the indicator bacteria, cooler and it sets in the tube.
Use ~3 mL overlay per 90-100 mm plate, 7-8 mL per 150 mm plate.
For lambda and many coliphages add MgSO4 to 10 mM and/or CaCl2 to 2-5 mM after autoclaving to aid adsorption.
Agarose (0.6-0.7%) or Noble agar can substitute where agar impurities inhibit the phage.
For a nutritionally leaner overlay that gives larger plaques, omit the yeast extract (tryptone top agar).

sources.title: Kropinski AM et al. (2009) Enumeration of Bacteriophages by Double Agar Overlay Plaque Assay. Methods Mol Biol 501:69-76
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning, 3rd ed. - top agar / plating bacteria for lambda
Barrick Lab protocols - media recipes (top agar 7-8 g/L)

recipes.name: Tryptone Soft Agar (motility / swim agar, 0.3%)
recipes.summary: Very low-percentage tryptone agar in which motile bacteria swim through the gel, producing expanding chemotactic rings.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: Some chemotaxis protocols use 8 g/L NaCl; 5 g/L is the Adler-style standard.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 3 g/L. Swim agar 0.25-0.35%; swarm agar 0.5-0.7%; twitching assays 1.0%. The percentage is the single most important variable in this assay.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl
recipes.storage: pour fresh; use plates the same day or next day
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 days - drying changes the effective agar concentration and ruins reproducibility
recipes.hazards: molten agar scald hazard
notes: Pour 20-25 mL per 90 mm plate and let set on a level bench, uncovered for no more than a few minutes.
Inoculate by stabbing into the centre of the agar with a toothpick or needle, do not spread on the surface.
Deliberately contains no yeast extract - a shallow amino acid gradient is what drives the visible chemotactic ring.
Batch-to-batch agar variation strongly affects ring diameter; compare only strains run on plates from the same pour.

sources.title: Adler J (1966) Chemotaxis in Bacteria. Science 153:708-716
Ha DG, Kuchma SL, O'Toole GA (2014) Plate-based assay for swimming motility in Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Methods Mol Biol 1149:59-65

recipes.name: SOB (Super Optimal Broth)
recipes.summary: Magnesium-rich rich broth used to grow cells for competent-cell preparation; the base for SOC.

components.name: Tryptone
components.note: 2% w/v

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 0.5 g/L = 8.6 mM (often quoted as '10 mM NaCl')

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: = 2.5 mM. Equivalent to adding 2.5 mL of 1 M KCl per litre. MW KCl = 74.55.

components.name: Magnesium chloride (MgCl2)
components.note: Add 5 mL of sterile 2 M MgCl2 per litre AFTER autoclaving. Make the 2 M stock from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30 (40.66 g per 100 mL), or from anhydrous MgCl2, MW 95.21 (19.04 g per 100 mL). Sambrook's '19 g in 100 mL' refers to the anhydrous salt.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH (some protocols specify pH 6.8-7.5)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min; add the MgCl2 as a separately sterilized stock afterwards
recipes.storage: 4 C after the Mg addition
recipes.shelfLife: base medium several months sterile; complete SOB best used within 1-2 months
notes: Add Mg only after autoclaving - autoclaving Mg with phosphate/peptone causes precipitation.
A common alternative Mg regime is 10 mM MgCl2 PLUS 10 mM MgSO4 (add 10 mL of a sterile 1 M MgCl2 / 1 M MgSO4 mix per litre); this is what NEB uses.
SOB + 20 mM glucose = SOC.
Do not substitute LB for SOB in chemically competent cell prep - transformation efficiency drops substantially.

sources.title: Hanahan D (1983) Studies on transformation of Escherichia coli with plasmids. J Mol Biol 166:557-580
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOB medium recipe (Sambrook & Russell)
NovoPro - SOB (Super Optimal Broth) preparation and recipe

recipes.name: SOC (Super Optimal broth with Catabolite repression)
recipes.summary: SOB plus 20 mM glucose; the standard recovery/outgrowth medium after bacterial transformation or electroporation.

components.name: Tryptone
components.note: 2% w/v

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: 8.6 mM

components.name: Magnesium chloride (MgCl2)
components.note: 5 mL of sterile 2 M MgCl2 per litre, added after autoclaving. MgCl2*6H2O MW = 203.30; anhydrous MgCl2 MW = 95.21.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose, anhydrous)
components.note: 20 mL of a 0.22 um filter-sterilized 1 M glucose stock per litre, added after autoclaving and cooling. Glucose MW (anhydrous) = 180.16; if using D-glucose monohydrate use MW 198.17.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the base 121 C, 15-20 min; add filter-sterilized glucose and sterile MgCl2 afterwards
recipes.storage: 4 C; aliquot into small sterile tubes to avoid repeated opening
recipes.shelfLife: up to 1 year at 4 C if kept sterile; discard immediately if cloudy
notes: Never autoclave the glucose with the peptones - Maillard browning destroys sugar and produces growth-inhibitory products. Filter it in separately.
NEB's commercial SOC Outgrowth Medium is 2% peptone, 0.5% yeast extract, 10 mM NaCl, 2.5 mM KCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 10 mM MgSO4, 20 mM glucose - i.e. it carries both magnesium salts.
Warm to 37 C before adding to transformed cells; cold SOC lowers recovery.
Typical use: 250-950 uL per transformation, shaking 37 C for 1 h before plating.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOC recipe (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning 4th ed.)
New England Biolabs - SOC Outgrowth Medium (B9020) composition
Hanahan D (1983) J Mol Biol 166:557-580

recipes.name: 2xYT (Double Yeast-Tryptone)
recipes.summary: Enriched broth with roughly double the nutrients of LB; standard for M13/filamentous phage propagation, phage display and higher-density E. coli culture.
recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C, dark
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder; several months sterile
notes: Total dehydrated equivalent 31 g/L.
For plates add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L); for M13 top agar use 0.7% w/v.
Preferred over LB for M13, fd and phagemid work, and generally gives higher biomass than LB before the culture stalls.
Not phosphate-buffered - cultures still acidify at high density. Use Terrific Broth if you need buffering.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - YT medium recipe (Sambrook & Russell)
Sambrook J et al., Molecular Cloning: A Laboratory Manual, 2nd ed., p. A.3, CSHL Press
QIAGEN FAQ 213 - What is the recipe for 2x YT?

recipes.name: Terrific Broth (TB)
recipes.summary: Phosphate-buffered, glycerol-containing rich medium for very high cell density - the standard for plasmid preps and recombinant protein expression.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 4 mL per litre. Primary carbon source; non-fermentable, so it avoids acetate overflow.

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4)
components.note: 2.31 g/L. Prepared as part of a separately autoclaved 10x phosphate stock (0.17 M KH2PO4).

components.name: Potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4)
components.note: 12.54 g/L. Part of the 10x stock (0.72 M K2HPO4). If you only have K2HPO4*3H2O, MW = 228.22 (16.43 g/L).

recipes.adjustPHWith: none normally required - the phosphate pair sets pH ~7.2
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the tryptone/yeast extract/glycerol in 90% of the final volume at 121 C for 20 min; autoclave the 10x phosphate stock separately and add after both have cooled below 60 C
recipes.storage: 4 C; room temperature acceptable for short periods
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months as powder; complete medium best within 1-2 months at 4 C
notes: Make the 10x phosphate stock as 2.31 g KH2PO4 + 12.54 g K2HPO4 in 90 mL water, adjust to 100 mL, autoclave. Add 100 mL per litre of finished medium.
Autoclaving phosphate together with the peptones causes precipitation - keep them separate. This is the single most common TB preparation error.

Commercial 'Terrific Broth, modified' powders (e.g. Sigma T9179) use a lower phosphate load: 12 g/L tryptone, 24 g/L yeast extract, 9.4 g/L K2HPO4, 2.2 g/L KH2PO4, plus 4-8 mL/L glycerol added by the user. Both are widely used; the Tartof-Hobbs values above are the original.
TB routinely reaches OD600 of 10-20 in shake flasks - use baffled flasks and no more than 20-25% fill volume or the culture becomes oxygen-limited.
Autoinduction media (e.g. ZYM-5052) are built on a TB-like base.

sources.title: Tartof KD & Hobbs CA (1987) Improved media for growing plasmid and cosmid clones. Bethesda Research Labs Focus 9:12
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning, 3rd ed. - Terrific Broth
Sigma-Aldrich T9179, Terrific Broth (modified) powder - composition

recipes.name: Nutrient Broth
recipes.summary: Simple general-purpose broth for cultivating non-fastidious organisms; the reference medium in many standard methods and teaching labs.

components.name: Peptone
components.note: Enzymatic digest of gelatin/meat; Difco 'Peptone'.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl; specification is pH 6.8 +/- 0.2 at 25 C
recipes.storage: dehydrated powder in a tightly closed container at 15-30 C; prepared medium 2-8 C
recipes.shelfLife: powder until the labelled expiry; prepared broth a few weeks at 2-8 C
recipes.hazards: beef extract is animal-derived; check institutional rules if TSE/BSE-free media are required
notes: Total dehydrated powder is 8 g/L.
For nutrient agar add agar to 1.5% w/v (15 g/L) - see the separate nutrient agar recipe.
Note the pH is deliberately slightly acidic (6.8), unlike LB at 7.0.
Much less rich than LB; do not expect comparable cell densities.
Some regional standards (e.g. ISO/European pharmacopoeia 'nutrient broth') use different peptone sources - check which one your method calls for.

sources.title: BD Difco & BBL Manual, 2nd ed. - Nutrient Broth
BD Difco Nutrient Broth 234000 product information

recipes.name: Nutrient Agar
recipes.summary: Solid general-purpose medium for isolation, colony counts and maintenance of non-fastidious bacteria.

components.name: Agar
components.note: 15 g/L for plates. For nutrient soft agar / overlays use 0.7% w/v; for slants 1.5-2%.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl; specification pH 6.8 +/- 0.2 at 25 C (2.3% solution)
recipes.storage: poured plates inverted at 2-8 C, sealed; dehydrated powder 15-30 C, tightly closed
recipes.shelfLife: poured plates 1-2 months at 2-8 C
recipes.hazards: molten agar scald hazard; beef extract is animal-derived
notes: Total dehydrated powder is 23 g/L.
Heat with frequent agitation and boil for 1 minute to dissolve the agar completely before autoclaving.
Widely used for standard plate counts and as a base for blood agar (add 5% defibrinated blood at 45-50 C).

sources.title: BD Difco & BBL Manual, 2nd ed. - Nutrient Agar
BD Difco Nutrient Agar 213000 product page

recipes.name: M9 Salts, 5x concentrate
recipes.summary: Concentrated buffered salt/nitrogen base that is diluted to make M9 minimal medium.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), anhydrous
components.note: = 0.239 M in the 5x, 47.7 mM at 1x. Equivalents: Na2HPO4*2H2O (MW 177.99) 42.5 g/L; Na2HPO4*7H2O (MW 268.07) 64.0 g/L; Na2HPO4*12H2O (MW 358.14) 85.5 g/L. Using the wrong hydrate is the classic M9 mistake.

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4), anhydrous
components.note: = 0.110 M in the 5x, 22 mM at 1x.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: = 42.8 mM in the 5x, 8.56 mM at 1x.

components.name: Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl)
components.note: = 93.5 mM in the 5x, 18.7 mM at 1x. Sole nitrogen source; substitute 15NH4Cl for isotopic labelling.

recipes.adjustPHWith: not normally adjusted; the phosphate pair gives ~pH 7.0-7.4 at 1x
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min (15 psi), liquid cycle - dispense into 200 mL aliquots first
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C; discard if any precipitate or turbidity appears
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months sterile
notes: Dissolve fully before autoclaving; phosphates dissolve slowly in cold water, so warm and stir.
This concentrate contains NO carbon source, magnesium, calcium or trace elements - all are added at the 1x stage.
200 mL of this 5x makes 1 L of 1x M9.
A slightly less concentrated variant (30 g/L anhydrous Na2HPO4, 211 mM) circulates in some lab handbooks; the 33.9 g/L value above matches Sambrook and Sigma M6030 and is the one to prefer.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - M9 minimal medium (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning)
Sigma-Aldrich M6030, M9 Minimal Salts 5x - composition (Na2HPO4 33.9, KH2PO4 15, NaCl 2.5, NH4Cl 5 g/L)

recipes.name: M9 Minimal Medium (1x, complete with glucose)
recipes.summary: Defined minimal medium for E. coli: used for auxotroph selection, isotope labelling, metabolic and physiology experiments.

components.name: M9 salts
components.note: 200 mL of sterile 5x M9 salts per litre. See the m9-salts-5x recipe.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4)
components.note: 2 mL of sterile 1 M MgSO4 per litre. Make the 1 M stock from MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.47 (24.65 g per 100 mL); anhydrous MgSO4 is MW 120.37.

components.name: Calcium chloride (CaCl2)
components.note: 0.1 mL of sterile 1 M CaCl2 per litre. Make the 1 M stock from CaCl2*2H2O, MW 147.01 (14.70 g per 100 mL); anhydrous CaCl2 is MW 110.98.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose, anhydrous)
components.note: 20 mL of 0.22 um filter-sterilized 20% (w/v) glucose per litre = 4 g/L = 22 mM. Glucose MW (anhydrous) 180.16; monohydrate 198.17. Glycerol (0.2-0.4% v/v) or other carbon sources can be substituted.

components.name: Thiamine hydrochloride (vitamin B1)
components.note: OPTIONAL. 1 mL of a filter-sterilized 1 mg/mL stock per litre. Required for thiamine auxotrophs (e.g. many BL21 and B-strain derivatives) and commonly added as insurance. Thiamine HCl MW 337.27.

components.name: Casamino acids
components.note: OPTIONAL - only for 'M9 rich' / supplemented M9. 2 g/L. Adding this makes the medium no longer strictly minimal and defeats amino acid auxotroph selection. Vitamin-free acid-hydrolysed casein; contains no tryptophan.

recipes.solvent: sterile deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: usually not adjusted; the phosphate buffer holds ~pH 7.0-7.4
recipes.sterilization: assemble aseptically from separately sterilized components - autoclave the 5x salts, MgSO4 and CaCl2; 0.22 um filter the glucose and thiamine
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: assemble fresh where possible; complete medium 2-4 weeks at 4 C
notes: Add components in this order to avoid precipitation: water, then 5x salts, then glucose, then MgSO4, then CaCl2. Adding concentrated CaCl2 or MgSO4 directly into concentrated phosphate produces an insoluble phosphate precipitate.
Never autoclave glucose with the phosphate salts.
For M9 minimal plates, autoclave agar (15 g/L) in the water separately, cool to ~50 C, then add the sterile 5x salts and supplements.
Doubling time on M9/glucose is roughly 50-60 min for E. coli K-12 at 37 C, versus ~20-25 min in LB. Plan overnight cultures accordingly.
For 15N or 13C labelling replace NH4Cl and/or glucose with the labelled forms in the 5x salts and carbon stock respectively.
Some protocols add trace metals (Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, Co, Mo) for prolonged or high-density growth; base M9 has none beyond what is carried in as contaminants.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - M9 minimal medium (Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning)
Miller JH, Experiments in Molecular Genetics, CSHL Press - A/M9 minimal media
Sigma-Aldrich M6030, M9 Minimal Salts 5x

recipes.name: MacConkey Agar
aka: MacConkey agar with crystal violet and neutral red
recipes.summary: Selective and differential agar: bile salts and crystal violet suppress Gram-positives while lactose plus neutral red distinguishes lactose fermenters (pink) from non-fermenters (colourless).

components.name: Lactose, monohydrate
components.note: The differential substrate. Lactose monohydrate MW 360.31; anhydrous lactose MW 342.30.

components.name: Bile salts mixture
components.note: Selective agent against Gram-positives. Oxoid 'Bile Salts No. 3' or Difco bile salts mixture.

components.name: Neutral red
components.note: 30 mg/L. pH indicator - turns red/pink below pH 6.8 as acid accumulates from lactose fermentation.
components.hazard: dye; avoid inhalation of the powder

components.name: Crystal violet
components.note: 1 mg/L. Inhibits Gram-positive organisms, especially enterococci and staphylococci. Weigh from a dilute stock - 1 mg per litre cannot be weighed accurately on a normal balance.
components.hazard: harmful if swallowed; suspected carcinogen; stains skin and clothing

components.name: Agar
components.note: 13.5 g/L in the Difco formulation. Oxoid MacConkey No. 3 uses 1.5% (15 g/L).

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl; specification pH 7.1 +/- 0.2 at 25 C
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min - do NOT over-autoclave
recipes.storage: poured plates inverted at 2-8 C in a sealed bag, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: poured plates 1-2 months at 2-8 C; dehydrated powder until labelled expiry
recipes.hazards: contains crystal violet (harmful, suspected carcinogen, staining) and neutral red; wear gloves and avoid raising dust when weighing. Buying the dehydrated blend is safer and far more reproducible than weighing the dyes yourself.
notes: Total dehydrated powder is 50 g/L (components sum to 50.03 g).
In practice, use the commercial dehydrated blend: the 1 mg/L crystal violet cannot be weighed reliably from scratch.
Boil with frequent agitation for 1 minute to fully dissolve before autoclaving.
Exceeding 121 C / 15 min degrades the bile salts and lactose and softens the gel.
Do not remelt and repour - the selective agents deteriorate.

Result interpretation: pink to red colonies, often with a bile-precipitate halo = strong lactose fermenter (e.g. E. coli); colourless/transparent = non-fermenter (e.g. Salmonella, Shigella, Pseudomonas).

Variants exist: MacConkey Agar Base (no lactose, add your own sugar); MacConkey Agar without crystal violet (permits enterococci and staphylococci); MacConkey with sorbitol (SMAC) for E. coli O157:H7.

CORRECTED: Oxoid MacConkey Agar No. 3 (CM0115) is 20 g/L peptone, 10 g/L lactose, 1.5 g/L bile salts No. 3, 5 g/L NaCl, 0.03 g/L neutral red, 0.001 g/L crystal violet, 15 g/L agar, pH 7.1 +/- 0.2 - i.e. the same 0.03 g/L neutral red as the Difco formula above. An earlier version of this entry listed 0.075 g/L neutral red for No. 3, which is wrong: 0.075 g/L is the neutral red level in the DIFFERENT Oxoid product 'MacConkey Agar' CM0007 (20 g/L peptone, 5 g/L bile salts, 12 g/L agar, NO crystal violet, pH 7.4 +/- 0.2). Do not carry the 0.075 g/L figure over to a No. 3 preparation - it is 2.5x the correct dye level.
Not intended for E. coli K-12 lab strain maintenance - use LB. This is a clinical/environmental isolation medium.

sources.title: BD Difco & BBL Manual, 2nd ed. - MacConkey Agar formula (peptone 17, proteose peptone 3, lactose 10, bile salts 1.5, NaCl 5, agar 13.5, neutral red 0.03, crystal violet 0.001 g/L; pH 7.1 +/- 0.2)
American Society for Microbiology - MacConkey Agar Plates Protocol
Thermo Scientific Oxoid MacConkey Agar No. 3 (CM0115) product specification

recipes.name: SM Buffer with Gelatin
recipes.summary: Standard bacteriophage dilution and storage buffer; gelatin coats surfaces and stabilises lambda particles during storage.

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 5.8 g/L in the published recipe

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 2 g/L in the published recipe. If using anhydrous MgSO4 (MW 120.37) use 0.96 g/L instead.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 7.5
components.note: 50 mL of 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5 per litre

components.name: Gelatin
components.note: Added as 5 mL of a 2% (w/v) gelatin stock per litre

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve NaCl and MgSO4*7H2O in 800 mL, add Tris-Cl and gelatin, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: pH is set by the 1 M Tris-Cl pH 7.5 stock; no further adjustment normally needed
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 15 psi (121 C), liquid cycle
recipes.storage: room temperature, in 50 mL sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: indefinite at room temperature if sterile; discard each aliquot after use
notes: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols / Sambrook & Russell recipe: 5.8 g NaCl, 2 g MgSO4*7H2O, 50 mL 1 M Tris-Cl (pH 7.5), 5 mL 2% (w/v) gelatin, H2O to 1 L.
Discard each aliquot after use to minimise the chance of contaminating the whole batch.
Gelatin can be omitted for most tailed phages; it is most useful for lambda and for very dilute phage suspensions where adsorption to plastic/glass causes titre loss.
Do not store phage in SM buffer at -20 C without a cryoprotectant; use 4 C for months-to-years, or add glycerol for -80 C.

recipes.name: LB Top Agar, 0.7% (soft agar overlay)
recipes.summary: Molten 0.7% agar overlay for double-agar-overlay (DAL) plaque assays; low agar lets progeny phage diffuse and form visible plaques.

components.name: Agar (bacteriological)
components.note: 7 g/L

recipes.adjustPHWith: 5 N NaOH
recipes.storage: room temperature in a tightly closed bottle; stable for months. Remelt and hold at 45-50 C before use.
recipes.shelfLife: several months as a sealed solid; do not repeatedly remelt more than a few times
recipes.hazards: Molten agar at 50 C and freshly autoclaved bottles cause burns; superheated agar can boil over on removal from the autoclave.
notes: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols recipe (per 100 mL): 0.7 g agar, 1 g tryptone, 0.5 g yeast extract, 0.5 g NaCl, H2O to 100 mL.
HOLD TEMPERATURE IS CRITICAL: equilibrate to 45-50 C before adding host cells. Above ~55 C you kill the host lawn; below ~42 C the agar sets in the tube.
Typical assay: 3 mL molten top agar + 100 uL overnight host culture + 100 uL phage dilution, vortex briefly, pour onto a pre-warmed, dry bottom agar plate.
Many phages need divalent cations - supplement the top agar with 1-10 mM CaCl2 and/or 10 mM MgSO4 from sterile 1 M stocks added after cooling to 50 C.
0.6% agar gives larger, easier-to-count plaques for slow-diffusing phages; 0.4-0.5% agarose is used for very large phages.
Use agarose instead of agar if agar sulfation/impurities inhibit your phage (common with some marine and Pseudomonas phages).

PLATES MUST BE DRY before overlaying, or the top agar slides and plaques smear. Dry lids-off in a laminar flow hood 15-30 min, or overnight at room temperature.
Pre-warm plates to 37 C before pouring the overlay to stop the top agar setting unevenly.
Supplement with CaCl2/MgSO4 from sterile 1 M stocks (added after cooling to ~50 C) if your phage requires divalent cations.

recipes.summary: Rich, Mg2+-containing medium that is the standard for growing E. coli hosts and propagating bacteriophage lambda to high titre.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate
components.note: 2 g/L of the heptahydrate = 8.1 mM Mg2+ (MW 246.47). Anhydrous MgSO4 equivalent is 0.98 g/L (MW 120.37).

'NZY' medium is the same without casamino acids; 'NZM' is without yeast extract and casamino acids.
For plates add 15 g/L agar; for top agarose add 7 g/L agarose (see the NZCYM top agarose entry).

Hold at 45-50 C. Use ~3 mL per 90 mm plate, ~7 mL per 150 mm plate.
Standard lambda plating: adsorb phage to host cells resuspended in 10 mM MgSO4 for 15-20 min at 37 C, then add molten top agarose and pour.

recipes.name: CaCl2 Stock, 1 M
recipes.summary: Sterile divalent cation stock; Ca2+ is required for adsorption by many phages and is added to media, top agar and phage buffer.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: 147.01 g/L. If using anhydrous CaCl2 (MW 110.98) use 110.98 g/L; if using the hexahydrate CaCl2*6H2O (MW 219.08) use 219.08 g/L.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (preferred) or autoclave 121 C, 15 min
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C, in small sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if any precipitate forms
recipes.hazards: CaCl2 dissolution is strongly exothermic - the solution gets hot. Add salt to water slowly with stirring; use a heat-tolerant vessel.
notes: HYDRATE FORM MATTERS: anhydrous CaCl2 = 110.98, dihydrate = 147.01, hexahydrate = 219.08 g/mol. Check the bottle label before weighing.
Anhydrous CaCl2 is hygroscopic and absorbs water on the shelf; the dihydrate is the more reliable choice for accurate molarity.
ADD Ca2+ AFTER AUTOCLAVING media and buffers. Autoclaving with phosphate or carbonate gives calcium precipitates.
Typical working concentrations for phage: 1-10 mM final in top agar, plates, lysate cultures and phage buffer.
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols publishes an equivalent 2.5 M stock as 11 g CaCl2*6H2O in 20 mL, 0.22 um filtered, stored in 1 mL aliquots at 4 C.

recipes.name: MgSO4 Stock, 1 M
recipes.summary: Sterile Mg2+ stock used to supplement media, top agar, phage buffers and to resuspend host cells before lambda adsorption.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate, anhydrous
components.note: 120.37 g/L (CSH recipe: 12 g in 100 mL). If using MgSO4*7H2O (MW 246.47) use 246.47 g/L instead - roughly double the mass.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if cloudy
notes: HYDRATE FORM MATTERS: anhydrous MgSO4 = 120.37 g/mol; heptahydrate MgSO4*7H2O (Epsom salt, the common lab form) = 246.47 g/mol. The common failure is weighing the ANHYDROUS mass (120.37 g/L) out of a bottle that is actually the HEPTAHYDRATE: that delivers only 120.37/246.47 = 0.49 M, i.e. HALF the intended concentration. The converse error (weighing 246.47 g/L of genuinely anhydrous salt) gives ~2 M, i.e. double.

CORRECTION 2026-07-20: this note previously read 'using the heptahydrate mass for the anhydrous salt halves your actual concentration', which states the error backwards - that combination doubles the concentration. Rewritten above.

The Cold Spring Harbor recipe is 12 g per 100 mL. CSH does not print the hydrate form on that page, but 12 g/100 mL only equals 1 M for the ANHYDROUS salt (12/120.37/0.1 = 1.00 M), so anhydrous is what is meant. Most lab bottles are the heptahydrate - check the label before weighing.
1 M MgSO4 near saturation can precipitate on cooling after autoclaving; warm and mix before use.
Standard use: resuspend host cells in 10 mM MgSO4 before adsorbing lambda; supplement top agar to 10 mM.

recipes.name: Tris-Cl Stock, 1 M, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Buffer stock used to make SM buffer, phage buffer, TM/TMG and most phage handling solutions.

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 121.14 g/L, then titrate to pH 7.5 with concentrated HCl (~65 mL of 12 N HCl per litre). Titrate to a meter - this volume is a starting estimate, not a fixed amount.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, adjust pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and gives off fumes - titrate in a fume hood.
notes: MW is for Tris FREE BASE = 121.14. Do not use 157.60 (Tris-HCl salt) unless you are actually weighing out the hydrochloride.

CORRECTION 2026-07-20: the HCl volume was listed as ~70 mL/L for pH 7.5. The Sambrook/CSH titration table gives 70 mL for pH 7.4, 60 mL for pH 7.6 and 42 mL for pH 8.0, so pH 7.5 needs ~65 mL. 70 mL overshoots pH 7.5. Corrected to ~65 mL.

TRIS pH IS STRONGLY TEMPERATURE DEPENDENT (approx. -0.028 pH units per degree C). Adjust the pH at the temperature the buffer will be used at, and let the solution cool to room temperature before final adjustment.
For SM buffer and phage buffer the target is pH 7.5; TMG buffer specifies pH 7.2 and some TM variants pH 7.4.
Autoclaved Tris often yellows slightly - this is normal and does not affect phage work.

recipes.name: Gelatin Stock, 2% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Concentrated gelatin stock used to supply the 0.01% gelatin in SM and TMG buffers.

components.name: Gelatin (Type A or B, bacteriological)
components.note: 20 g/L

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (this also dissolves the gelatin)
recipes.storage: 4 C (will set to a gel) or room temperature in small sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard at any sign of microbial growth
notes: Gelatin does not dissolve well in cold water - sprinkle onto water, let it swell for 15-30 min, then autoclave to dissolve fully.
Use 5 mL per litre to give the 0.01% (w/v) final gelatin in SM buffer and TMG.
Gelatin is an animal product - it is a nutrient source, so this stock is a prime contamination target. Aliquot small and discard aggressively.
Omit gelatin entirely for phage preps destined for protein gels, TEM or mass spectrometry.

recipes.name: PEG/NaCl Phage Precipitation Solution, 5x (20% PEG-8000 / 2.5 M NaCl)
recipes.summary: Concentrates phage from lysates by polyethylene glycol precipitation; added at one quarter volume to give 1x (4% PEG / 0.5 M NaCl) final.

components.name: Polyethylene glycol 8000
components.note: 200 g/L (i.e. 100 g per 500 mL)

components.name: NaCl
components.note: 146.1 g/L. The commonly published recipe of 75 g per 500 mL is 2.57 M - close enough for precipitation.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve NaCl first in ~80% of final volume, then add PEG in small portions with vigorous stirring, then bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (stir/swirl after autoclaving to redissolve), or 0.22 um filter while warm
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: PEG dust is an inhalation nuisance; weigh carefully. The solution is very viscous - pipette slowly and account for hold-up volume.
notes: PEG DISSOLVES SLOWLY. Add it in small portions to the NaCl solution with vigorous stirring, or stir several hours at room temperature; gentle warming to 37-50 C helps.

Standard use: add 1 volume of this 5x solution to 4 volumes of clarified phage lysate (final 4% PEG / 0.5 M NaCl), mix, hold 1 h to overnight at 4 C, then centrifuge 10,000-11,000 x g for 20-30 min at 4 C.
CLARIFY THE LYSATE FIRST (centrifuge and/or 0.45 um filter) - PEG precipitates cell debris just as well as phage.
Drain the pellet thoroughly and respin briefly to remove residual PEG; carried-over PEG inhibits restriction enzymes and PCR downstream.
PEG-6000 is used interchangeably at the same 20% w/v; PEG-8000 is the more common choice for phage.
Resuspend the pellet gently in SM buffer or phage buffer - vortexing shears tailed phage.

recipes.name: Phage Glycerol Freezer Stock (25% v/v final)
recipes.summary: Cryoprotected phage stock for long-term archiving at -80 C; equal volumes of phage suspension and sterile 50% glycerol give 25% glycerol final.

components.name: Phage lysate or purified phage in SM buffer / phage buffer
components.note: Use a high-titre stock: at least 1e8 pfu/mL, ideally 1e10

components.name: Glycerol, sterile aqueous
components.note: Equal volume of sterile 50% (v/v) glycerol. To make the 50% stock, mix 50 mL glycerol + 50 mL water and autoclave.

recipes.solvent: none - the phage suspension is the diluent
recipes.sterilization: the glycerol solution is autoclaved 121 C, 20 min; do not autoclave after adding phage
recipes.storage: -80 C in screw-cap cryovials
recipes.shelfLife: years at -80 C; re-titre every 1-2 years
notes: FINAL GLYCEROL CONCENTRATION: 25% (v/v) here. Published phage practice spans roughly 10-50% v/v final - SEA-PHAGES uses 10%, many labs use 20-25%, and 50% is a common cryopreservation control condition.
USE SCREW-CAP CRYOVIALS. Snap-cap tubes can pop open at -80 C.
ALWAYS KEEP A NON-FROZEN BACKUP: most tailed phages survive for years at 4 C in SM buffer, and for some phages that is more reliable than freezing.

GLYCEROL IS NOT UNIVERSALLY PROTECTIVE FOR PHAGE. It has been shown to reduce titre for some phages (e.g. T4 in certain buffers). Titre before and after freezing to check your specific phage tolerates it.
Avoid freeze-thaw cycles - aliquot into single-use volumes.
-20 C storage is markedly worse than -80 C for phage; use -80 C or liquid nitrogen vapour phase.
Mix gently by inversion; do not vortex tailed phage.

recipes.name: Chloroform Treatment of Phage Lysates (use note)
recipes.summary: Adding a small volume of chloroform to a phage lysate kills residual host bacteria and releases phage from unlysed cells - but it destroys lipid-containing phages.

components.name: Chloroform
components.hazard: Chloroform is a suspected human carcinogen, a CNS depressant and hepatotoxin. Handle only in a fume hood with nitrile-over-nitrile or laminated gloves; standard nitrile gloves are permeated within minutes. Dispose of as halogenated organic waste.
components.note: Typical range 0.5-2% (v/v). Some plate-lysate protocols use several drops per tube, or a 1:10 v/v wash.

recipes.solvent: not applicable - added directly to the phage lysate
recipes.sterilization: none - chloroform IS the sterilising agent here
recipes.storage: Lysates stored over a chloroform layer keep at 4 C for months. Store in glass or polypropylene, never polystyrene.
recipes.shelfLife: chloroform-treated lysates: months to years at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Chloroform is a suspected carcinogen (IARC Group 2B), hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic, and a CNS depressant. Use in a fume hood only. It dissolves polystyrene and many plastics - use glass or polypropylene. Collect as halogenated organic waste; never pour down the drain.
notes: CRITICAL: DO NOT USE CHLOROFORM ON LIPID-CONTAINING PHAGES. Phages with lipid envelopes or internal lipid membranes (e.g. Cystoviridae such as phi6, Corticoviridae such as PM2, Tectiviridae such as PRD1) are inactivated. Lambda and most tailed phages (Caudoviricetes) are resistant.

ALWAYS TEST FIRST on a small aliquot: titre before and after chloroform. If the titre drops, skip the step and use 0.22 um filtration instead. Even non-lipid phages can lose titre through capsid protein denaturation.
Typical use: add chloroform to 0.5-2% (v/v), vortex or shake, hold at 4 C for 10-30 min, then centrifuge briefly and take the upper aqueous phase.
Chloroform is denser than water and forms the LOWER phase. Take the phage from the upper aqueous layer.
Residual chloroform inhibits some downstream enzymes and is toxic to host cells at plating - let traces evaporate, or back-extract, before assays sensitive to it.
DO NOT use chloroform for phage preparations intended for therapy, animal work or any clinical application - residual solvent is unacceptable there. Use filtration and chromatography instead.
Chloroform is a reasonable alternative to filtration when you need to avoid the phage losses that 0.22 um membranes cause with large or filamentous phages.

recipes.summary: Sugar supplement added to host cultures to induce LamB, the outer membrane maltoporin that is the receptor for bacteriophage lambda.

components.name: Maltose monohydrate
components.note: 200 g/L. Maltose monohydrate MW = 360.31; anhydrous maltose MW = 342.30. The 20% figure is a mass/volume spec, so the hydrate form does not change the mass weighed - but it does change the molarity (20% monohydrate = 0.555 M; 20% anhydrous = 0.584 M).

recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 3-6 months; discard if cloudy or if crystals appear

Standard use: add to 0.2% (w/v) final in the host growth medium (10 mL of 20% stock per litre) to induce LamB expression before lambda infection.
Only relevant for lambda and other LamB-dependent phages. Most phages use a different receptor and need no maltose.
Commonly paired with 10 mM MgSO4 in the growth medium for lambda plating hosts.

recipes.name: Ampicillin stock solution, 100 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x selection stock for beta-lactamase (bla / AmpR) plasmids in E. coli; working concentration 100 ug/mL.

components.name: Ampicillin sodium salt
components.form: Ampicillin sodium salt (sodium ampicillin), CAS 69-52-3, C16H18N3NaO4S, MW 371.39 g/mol (e.g. Sigma-Aldrich A9518)
components.note: 100 mg/mL = 100 g/L. Equivalent to 0.269 M. Use the SODIUM SALT, which is freely water-soluble; ampicillin free acid (anhydrous, MW 349.41) dissolves poorly in water and needs base or a different solvent.
components.hazard: Beta-lactam. Potent contact sensitiser / allergen - wear gloves and avoid inhaling powder. Do not handle if penicillin-allergic.

recipes.storage: -20 C in single-use aliquots (protect from repeated freeze-thaw); 4 C acceptable short term
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C; 3 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Beta-lactam antibiotic; potent allergen/sensitiser. Avoid skin contact and dust inhalation.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 100 ug/mL (1:1000 dilution of this stock). 50 ug/mL is sometimes used for low-copy plasmids.
Add to molten agar only after it has cooled to ~55 C - ampicillin is heat-labile and will be destroyed by autoclaving.
Ampicillin is degraded by secreted beta-lactamase, so satellite colonies appear around large AmpR colonies on plates older than ~24 h. Use carbenicillin instead if this is a problem.
Cold Spring Harbor equivalent recipe: dissolve 1 g sodium ampicillin in H2O to a final volume of 10 mL.
Prewash the 0.22 or 0.45 um filter with 50-100 mL H2O before filtering to remove filter extractables.

recipes.name: Carbenicillin stock solution, 100 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: More stable beta-lactam substitute for ampicillin on AmpR plasmids; greatly reduces satellite colonies.

components.name: Carbenicillin disodium salt
components.form: Carbenicillin disodium salt (alpha-carboxybenzylpenicillin disodium), CAS 4800-94-6, C17H16N2Na2O6S, MW 422.36 g/mol
components.note: 100 mg/mL = 100 g/L = 0.237 M. Freely soluble in water.
components.hazard: Beta-lactam; allergen/sensitiser as for ampicillin.

recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C; ~1 month at 4 C
recipes.hazards: Beta-lactam antibiotic; potent allergen/sensitiser.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 100 ug/mL (1:1000). Some labs use 50 ug/mL.
Interchangeable with ampicillin for selecting bla (AmpR) plasmids - carbenicillin is a poorer beta-lactamase substrate, so plates stay selective longer and produce far fewer satellite colonies.
Heat-labile: add to molten agar at ~55 C, never before autoclaving.

recipes.name: Kanamycin stock solution, 50 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x selection stock for aminoglycoside phosphotransferase (nptII / KanR) plasmids in E. coli; working concentration 50 ug/mL.

components.name: Kanamycin sulfate
components.form: Kanamycin A sulfate, CAS 25389-94-0, C18H38N4O15S, MW 582.58 g/mol (e.g. Sigma-Aldrich K4000)
components.note: 50 mg/mL = 50 g/L = 0.0858 M of the sulfate salt. Note that commercial kanamycin sulfate is sold on a potency basis (K4000 is >=750 ug kanamycin base per mg); for routine plasmid selection the salt mass is used directly without potency correction.
components.hazard: Aminoglycoside. Avoid inhalation; ototoxic/nephrotoxic on systemic exposure.

recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots (4 C also acceptable)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Aminoglycoside antibiotic; avoid dust inhalation.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 50 ug/mL (1:1000 dilution).
Kanamycin is relatively heat-stable but is still added to molten agar at ~55 C by convention.
Kanamycin selection is inhibited by high salt; use low-salt LB (e.g. Lennox or 5 g/L NaCl) if selection looks weak.
Some labs use 30 ug/mL for kanamycin on plates and 50 ug/mL in liquid culture.

recipes.name: Chloramphenicol stock solution, 25 mg/mL (1000x, ethanol)
recipes.summary: 1000x ethanolic selection stock for chloramphenicol acetyltransferase (cat / CmR) plasmids; working concentration 25 ug/mL.

components.name: Chloramphenicol
components.form: Chloramphenicol free base, CAS 56-75-7, C11H12Cl2N2O5, MW 323.13 g/mol (e.g. Sigma-Aldrich C0378)
components.note: 25 mg/mL = 25 g/L = 77.4 mM. Chloramphenicol is poorly water-soluble (~2.5 mg/mL) - it MUST be dissolved in ethanol, not water.
components.hazard: Suspected carcinogen; associated with aplastic anaemia. Handle powder in a fume hood, wear gloves.

recipes.solvent: 100% ethanol (bring to final volume); 70% or 95% ethanol also works
recipes.sterilization: none (ethanol is self-sterilising; do not filter through cellulose-ester membranes, which ethanol dissolves)
recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months to 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Chloramphenicol is a suspected carcinogen and is linked to idiosyncratic aplastic anaemia. Weigh in a fume hood; wear gloves. Ethanol is flammable.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 25 ug/mL (1:1000 dilution of this stock). Widely used alternatives are 34 ug/mL (from a 34 mg/mL stock) and 20 ug/mL; Cold Spring Harbor's 1000x recipe uses 10 mg/mL in 95% ethanol giving 10 ug/mL. Check your plasmid/strain's convention - the accepted range spans 10-34 ug/mL.
Because the stock is ethanolic, keep the added volume below ~1% of the medium volume (1 mL per litre at 1000x is fine).
Add to molten agar at ~55 C. Chloramphenicol is comparatively heat-stable but ethanol will flash off in very hot agar.
If a 34 mg/mL stock is preferred, dissolve 340 mg in ethanol to 10 mL final volume and use at 1:1000 for 34 ug/mL.

recipes.name: Tetracycline stock solution, 10 mg/mL (1000x, ethanol)
recipes.summary: 1000x selection stock for tetracycline-resistance (tetA / TcR) plasmids; working concentration 10 ug/mL.

components.name: Tetracycline hydrochloride
components.form: Tetracycline hydrochloride, CAS 64-75-5, C22H24N2O8 - HCl, MW 480.90 g/mol (e.g. Sigma-Aldrich T7660)
components.note: 10 mg/mL = 10 g/L = 20.8 mM. Tetracycline free base (MW 444.43) is much less water-soluble; the HCl salt is the standard reagent.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; light-sensitive; stains. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.solvent: 70% ethanol (bring to final volume); 50% ethanol or 100% ethanol also used, water acceptable for short-term stocks
recipes.sterilization: none if made in ethanol (self-sterilising); 0.22 um filter if made in water
recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots, wrapped in foil - tetracycline is strongly light-sensitive
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at -20 C in ethanol, protected from light. Aqueous stocks: only ~2 weeks at 4 C in the dark.
recipes.hazards: Light-sensitive; degradation products (including anhydrotetracycline) are toxic. Harmful if swallowed.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 10 ug/mL (1:1000 dilution). 12.5 ug/mL and 15 ug/mL are also common.
Tetracycline chelates divalent cations - Mg2+ and Ca2+ in rich media reduce its potency. Avoid magnesium-supplemented media when selecting with tetracycline.
Protect plates and cultures from light; keep tetracycline plates wrapped in foil at 4 C and use within ~2-4 weeks.
Add to molten agar at ~55 C.
Ethanolic stocks are markedly more stable than aqueous ones - prefer 70% ethanol for anything kept longer than a couple of weeks.

recipes.name: Streptomycin stock solution, 50 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x aqueous stock for streptomycin selection (aadA / strA-strB markers, or rpsL counter-selection); working concentration 50 ug/mL.

components.name: Streptomycin sulfate
components.form: Streptomycin sulfate salt, CAS 3810-74-0, C21H39N7O12 - 1.5 H2SO4, MW 728.69 g/mol
components.note: 50 mg/mL = 50 g/L = 68.6 mM. CAUTION: some vendors quote MW 1457.38 for the doubled formula 2(C21H39N7O12) - 3 H2SO4; that is the same substance written as a dimer. Sigma-Aldrich lists 728.69 for the 1:1.5 formula. Use 728.69 unless your bottle's label says otherwise.
components.hazard: Aminoglycoside; ototoxic/nephrotoxic on systemic exposure. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Aminoglycoside antibiotic; avoid dust inhalation.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 50 ug/mL is the common laboratory default; published values range from 25 to 100 ug/mL depending on strain and marker. 100 ug/mL is often used when selecting spontaneous rpsL (StrR) mutants.
Streptomycin is heat-labile - add to molten agar at ~55 C.
Aminoglycoside uptake is proton-motive-force dependent, so streptomycin is much less effective under anaerobic conditions or on very rich/high-salt media.
Frequently paired with spectinomycin, since the aadA gene confers resistance to both.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich - Streptomycin sulfate salt substance record (C21H39N7O12 - 1.5 H2SO4, MW 728.69, CAS 3810-74-0)

recipes.name: Spectinomycin stock solution, 50 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x aqueous stock for spectinomycin selection (aadA / SpecR); working concentration 50 ug/mL.

components.name: Spectinomycin dihydrochloride pentahydrate
components.form: Spectinomycin dihydrochloride pentahydrate, CAS 22189-32-8, C14H24N2O7 - 2 HCl - 5 H2O, MW 495.35 g/mol
components.note: 50 mg/mL = 50 g/L = 101 mM. IMPORTANT: this is the pentahydrate dihydrochloride (MW 495.35) - spectinomycin free base is C14H24N2O7, MW 332.35. Roughly 67% of the pentahydrate salt's mass is spectinomycin base, so if a protocol specifies base equivalents, scale accordingly.
components.hazard: Aminocyclitol antibiotic. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Avoid dust inhalation; standard antibiotic handling precautions.
notes: Working concentration in E. coli: 50 ug/mL (1:1000). 100 ug/mL is common where background growth is a problem, and 25 ug/mL when combined with a second antibiotic.
Add to molten agar at ~55 C.
aadA confers resistance to both spectinomycin and streptomycin; the two are often used together (e.g. 25 ug/mL spec + 50 ug/mL strep).
Spectinomycin selection is noticeably weaker on high-salt media - low-salt LB gives cleaner selection.

recipes.name: Gentamicin stock solution, 10 mg/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: 1000x aqueous stock for gentamicin selection (aacC1 / GmR); working concentration 10 ug/mL in E. coli.

components.name: Gentamicin sulfate
components.form: Gentamicin sulfate, CAS 1405-41-0 - a MIXTURE of the sulfate salts of gentamicins C1, C1a, C2, C2a and C2b. No single defined molecular weight.
components.note: 10 mg/mL = 10 g/L. Because gentamicin sulfate is a multi-component mixture, molarity is not meaningful and vendors sell it on a potency basis (typically 590-700 ug gentamicin base per mg). Weigh by salt mass for routine selection; if your protocol specifies potency units, correct using the potency printed on the bottle.
components.hazard: Aminoglycoside; ototoxic/nephrotoxic on systemic exposure. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots (4 C acceptable; gentamicin is comparatively stable)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Aminoglycoside antibiotic; avoid dust inhalation.
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN for two reasons: (1) gentamicin sulfate has no single molecular weight - it is a defined mixture, and vendor potency (ug base per mg salt) varies between lots, so molar calculations are not possible; (2) the published E. coli working concentration varies substantially between sources.

Working concentration in E. coli: GoldBio's table gives 10 ug/mL. Many labs and Addgene-style tables use 15-25 ug/mL, and Gram-negative work outside E. coli commonly uses 20-30 ug/mL. Confirm against your own plasmid/strain before relying on 10 ug/mL.
Add to molten agar at ~55 C.
Aminoglycoside uptake is proton-motive-force dependent - selection is weak under anaerobic conditions.

sources.title: GoldBio - Antibiotic stock/working concentration table (gentamycin 10 mg/mL stock, 10 ug/mL working, E. coli)

recipes.name: Erythromycin stock solution, 100 mg/mL (ethanol)
recipes.summary: Ethanolic macrolide stock, mainly for erm-marked Gram-positive work; E. coli requires much higher concentrations because of outer-membrane exclusion.

components.name: Erythromycin
components.form: Erythromycin (erythromycin A free base), CAS 114-07-8, C37H67NO13, MW 733.93 g/mol
components.note: 100 mg/mL = 100 g/L = 136 mM. Erythromycin free base is essentially insoluble in water (~2 mg/mL) - it must be dissolved in ethanol, methanol or DMSO. Erythromycin estolate/stearate/ethylsuccinate salts have different MWs and are NOT interchangeable for lab selection.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable.

recipes.solvent: 100% ethanol (bring to final volume); DMSO or methanol also acceptable
recipes.sterilization: none (ethanolic solution is self-sterilising; do not filter through cellulose-ester membranes)
recipes.storage: -20 C in aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months to 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Macrolide antibiotic; harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable.
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN: the working concentration of erythromycin in E. coli is genuinely not standardised. E. coli is intrinsically resistant because the outer membrane excludes macrolides and AcrAB-TolC effluxes them, so selection concentrations reported in the literature span roughly 100-500 ug/mL (150 ug/mL is a frequently cited value for erm plasmid selection). Verify empirically for your strain with a kill curve.

By contrast, for Gram-positive hosts (Bacillus, Staphylococcus, Lactococcus, Streptococcus) erm selection is typically 5-10 ug/mL, and MLS-resistance selection often uses 1 ug/mL erythromycin + 25 ug/mL lincomycin.
At 150 ug/mL in E. coli you would add 1.5 mL of this 100 mg/mL stock per litre - that is 0.15% ethanol, which is tolerated. Do not exceed ~1% ethanol in the medium.
Add to molten agar at ~55 C.
Erythromycin is acid-labile; do not use in acidified media.

Many erm resistance genes are INDUCIBLE rather than constitutive. Plating untreated cells directly onto a high erythromycin concentration can kill them before resistance is expressed. If transformants fail to appear, allow a longer outgrowth, or pre-induce with a sub-inhibitory erythromycin concentration, before applying full selection.

recipes.name: IPTG stock solution, 1 M
recipes.summary: Gratuitous, non-metabolisable lac operon inducer for T7/lac and tac/trc expression systems and for blue-white screening.

components.name: IPTG
components.form: Isopropyl beta-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside, CAS 367-93-1, C9H18O5S, MW 238.30 g/mol (dioxane-free grade preferred)
components.note: 1 M = 238.30 g/L, i.e. 2.383 g in a final volume of 10 mL. Highly water-soluble.
components.hazard: Low toxicity but expensive; avoid dust.

recipes.storage: -20 C in 1 mL single-use aliquots; 4 C for up to ~1 month in active use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Not classified as hazardous at bench scale; standard gloves/eye protection.
notes: Working concentrations from this 1 M stock (1:1000 gives 1 mM): protein induction in BL21(DE3) typically 0.1-1.0 mM, with 0.4-0.5 mM the most common default. Lower (0.05-0.1 mM) plus lower temperature favours soluble protein.
Blue-white screening: 0.1 mM final in the plate, or spread 100 uL of 0.1 M IPTG on the plate surface and let it absorb for >=30 min before plating.

Cold Spring Harbor also specifies a 20% (w/v) = 0.84 M stock: dissolve 2 g IPTG in 8 mL H2O, adjust to 10 mL, filter through 0.22 um, aliquot 1 mL, store at -20 C. [CORRECTED: previously stated as 0.8 M; 200 g/L / 238.30 g/mol = 0.839 M. Prepare by mass as written rather than back-calculating from the molarity.]
IPTG is not metabolised by E. coli, so induction is sustained - unlike lactose or arabinose.
Do not autoclave. Filter-sterilise only.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - IPTG solution, pdb.rec8091 (20% w/v = 0.8 M; 0.22 um filter; 1 mL aliquots at -20 C)
Sigma-Aldrich I5502 / I6758 - Isopropyl beta-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside (MW 238.30, CAS 367-93-1)
GoldBio - How to Make an IPTG Stock Solution (1 M = 2.38 g per 10 mL)

recipes.name: X-gal stock solution, 20 mg/mL (2% w/v in DMF)
recipes.summary: Chromogenic beta-galactosidase substrate for blue-white screening of lacZ-alpha recombinants; hydrolysis yields an insoluble blue indigo dye.

components.name: X-gal
components.form: 5-Bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl beta-D-galactopyranoside, CAS 7240-90-6, C14H15BrClNO6, MW 408.63 g/mol (e.g. Sigma-Aldrich B4252)
components.note: 20 mg/mL = 20 g/L = 2% (w/v) = 48.9 mM. X-gal is insoluble in water - it must be dissolved in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) or DMSO.
components.hazard: Handle in glass or polypropylene only - DMF dissolves polystyrene.

recipes.sterilization: none (DMF solution is self-sterilising; do not filter - DMF destroys most filter membranes)
recipes.storage: -20 C, wrapped in aluminium foil (light-sensitive), in a glass or polypropylene tube
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C protected from light
recipes.hazards: DMF is a reproductive toxin (suspected of damaging the unborn child) and is readily absorbed through skin. Prepare in a fume hood wearing nitrile gloves. DMF dissolves polystyrene - never use standard polystyrene tubes or pipettes.
notes: Working concentration: 40 ug/mL final in LB agar (2 mL of this 20 mg/mL stock per litre of medium).
Surface-spread alternative (more economical and most common): spread 40 uL of the 20 mg/mL stock over a standard 90 mm plate and let it absorb for >=30 min before plating cells.
Cold Spring Harbor also lists a 25 mg/mL stock in DMF; 40 mg/mL stocks are also sold. Adjust the volume added to hit 40 ug/mL final.
Use with IPTG (0.1 mM final) to induce lacZ. Blue colour needs 16-20 h at 37 C, and develops further if plates are left at 4 C for a few hours.
DMSO can substitute for DMF and is less toxic, but X-gal is somewhat less soluble in it.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - X-gal solution, pdb.rec8092 (20 mg/mL in DMF, foil-wrapped, glass or polypropylene)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - X-gal stock solution (25 mg/mL), pdb.rec10948 (in DMF, -20 C protected from light)
Sigma-Aldrich B4252 - X-Gal (MW 408.63, CAS 7240-90-6, C14H15BrClNO6)
Thermo Fisher - Preparation of X-Gal/IPTG LB Agar Plates for Blue/White Colony Screening

recipes.name: L-arabinose stock solution, 20% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Inducer of the araBAD (pBAD) promoter; supports graded, titratable induction in araBAD-deficient hosts.

components.name: L-(+)-Arabinose
components.note: 20% (w/v) = 200 g/L = 1.33 M. Use the L-enantiomer - D-arabinose does not induce araBAD. Very freely water-soluble.
components.hazard: Non-hazardous sugar.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do not autoclave - sugars caramelise and can form growth-inhibitory Maillard products)
recipes.storage: 4 C, or -20 C in aliquots for long-term
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C; 1 year at -20 C
notes: Working concentration: 0.2% (w/v) final = 13.3 mM gives full induction; add 10 mL of this 20% stock per litre of medium (1:100).

For titrating expression level, Invitrogen's pBAD manual recommends testing 0.00002%, 0.0002%, 0.002%, 0.02% and 0.2% - prepare the intermediate points by serial 10-fold dilution of the 20% stock in sterile water.

Graded (rather than all-or-none) induction requires a host defective in arabinose catabolism and transport, e.g. TOP10 or LMG194. In wild-type E. coli, arabinose is consumed and the population induces heterogeneously.
Glucose represses pBAD via catabolite repression - use glycerol (0.2%) rather than glucose as the carbon source when inducing.
A 10% or 2% stock is often more convenient for small-scale titrations; scale the mass accordingly.

sources.title: Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher - pBAD/His and pBAD/Myc-His User Manual (20% arabinose stock; 0.00002%-0.2% induction series; TOP10/LMG194 hosts)
Thermo Fisher - pBAD Expression System brochure (tightly controlled, titratable arabinose induction)
Schleif R. FEMS Microbiol Rev 34:779 (2010) - AraC and regulation of the L-arabinose operon in E. coli

recipes.name: Anhydrotetracycline (aTc) stock solution, 400 ug/mL (1000x)
recipes.summary: Non-antibiotic tetracycline analogue that induces TetR-repressed promoters (pTet/tetO) at concentrations far below any growth effect.

components.name: Anhydrotetracycline hydrochloride
components.form: Anhydrotetracycline hydrochloride, CAS 13803-65-1, C22H22N2O7 - HCl, MW 462.88 g/mol
components.note: 400 ug/mL = 0.4 g/L = 864 uM (0.864 mM). This is close to saturation in water; do not try to make a more concentrated aqueous stock. [CORRECTED: this line previously read '864 nM (0.864 uM)', a 1000-fold unit error. 0.4 g/L / 462.88 g/mol = 8.64e-4 M = 864 uM. The 216 nM figure quoted below for the 100 ng/mL working concentration was and remains correct.]
components.hazard: Light-sensitive; tetracycline-family compound. Avoid dust inhalation.

recipes.storage: -20 C in 1 mL single-use aliquots, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C if not subjected to repeated freeze-thaw
recipes.hazards: Strongly light-sensitive. Avoid dust inhalation; handle as a tetracycline-class compound.
notes: Working concentration: 400 ng/mL from a 1:1000 dilution of this stock, per the Cold Spring Harbor 1000x recipe.

Most TetR/pTet systems are fully induced at ~100 ng/mL (216 nM); above ~100 ng/mL the induction factor changes little, and 100 ng/mL is commonly cited as the highest concentration causing no growth retardation. Titrate over 0-200 ng/mL for graded expression.

Cold Spring Harbor specifically recommends WATER as the solvent even though ethanol and DMSO are more usual for tetracyclines, because aTc decays more slowly in water at 400 ug/mL (a nearly saturated solution).
aTc has ~100-fold higher affinity for TetR than tetracycline itself but essentially no antibacterial activity, which is why it is preferred as an inducer.
Protect cultures and plates from light; aTc photodegrades quickly. Add fresh at the time of induction.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Anhydrotetracycline Hydrochloride Stock Solution (1000x), pdb.rec108104 (400 ug/mL in H2O, filter-sterilised, 1 mL aliquots at -20 C, protected from light)
Sigma-Aldrich - Anhydrotetracycline hydrochloride substance record (C22H22N2O7 - HCl, MW 462.88, CAS 13803-65-1)

Rodriguez-Garcia A. et al., Nucleic Acids Res 33:e87 (2005) - 'Natural and synthetic tetracycline-inducible promoters for use in the antibiotic-producing bacteria Streptomyces'; induction factor barely changes above 100 ng/mL aTc. NOTE: this plateau was measured in Streptomyces, not E. coli - it is consistent with E. coli TetR/pTet behaviour but is not direct E. coli evidence.

recipes.name: Antibiotic supplementation of LB medium - working concentrations (stock to final)
recipes.summary: Calculator sheet: how much of each 1000x stock to add per volume of LB broth or molten LB agar for standard E. coli selection.

components.name: Ampicillin
components.note: 1:1000. Most common AmpR selection.

components.name: Carbenicillin
components.note: 1:1000. Substitute for ampicillin; fewer satellite colonies.

components.name: Chloramphenicol
components.note: 1:1000. Ethanolic stock; 10-34 ug/mL all in common use.

components.name: Tetracycline
components.note: 1:1000. Keep plates in the dark.

components.name: Gentamicin
components.note: 1:1000. Range 10-25 ug/mL - verify for your system.

components.name: Erythromycin
components.note: 1:667 for E. coli (E. coli is intrinsically resistant). Gram-positive hosts need only 5-10 ug/mL. Highly variable - verify empirically.

recipes.solvent: LB broth or molten LB agar (the antibiotic stock volume is negligible and is not subtracted from the medium volume)
recipes.sterilization: none - the medium is autoclaved first, then the sterile antibiotic stock is added aseptically
recipes.storage: LB + antibiotic broth: 4 C, use within ~1 month. Plates: 4 C, inverted, in a sealed bag.
recipes.shelfLife: Amp/carb plates ~1 month; kan, cam, spec, strep plates ~2-3 months; tetracycline plates ~2-4 weeks in the dark
recipes.hazards: See individual antibiotic recipes.
notes: CRITICAL: cool autoclaved agar to 50-55 C before adding antibiotic. Ampicillin, carbenicillin and streptomycin are heat-labile and will be substantially destroyed above ~60 C.
At 1000x, add 1 mL of stock per litre of medium (1 uL per mL). Swirl thoroughly but gently to avoid bubbles before pouring.
Standard 90 mm plate takes ~25 mL, so 1 L of agar pours ~40 plates.
When selecting with two antibiotics simultaneously, many labs halve each concentration to reduce combined toxicity.
These are E. coli values. Other organisms differ substantially - do not transfer them to Agrobacterium, Pseudomonas, Bacillus etc. without checking.

Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - LB-kanamycin agar plates, pdb.rec11685
GoldBio - How to Make Stock Solutions for Antibiotics and Cell Selection Agents (Table 1, E. coli working concentrations)

recipes.name: Blue-white screening supplement for LB agar (IPTG + X-gal)
recipes.summary: IPTG and X-gal additions to antibiotic LB agar for lacZ-alpha complementation screening of recombinant clones.

components.name: IPTG
components.note: 1:10000 dilution of the 1 M stock, i.e. 100 uL per litre of medium. Induces lacZ expression.

components.name: X-gal
components.note: 1:500 dilution, i.e. 2 mL of the 20 mg/mL DMF stock per litre of medium.
components.hazard: Contains DMF - add in a fume hood.

components.name: Ampicillin (or the appropriate plasmid marker)
components.note: Blue-white screening is always done on top of normal plasmid selection.

recipes.solvent: molten LB agar cooled to 50-55 C
recipes.sterilization: none - added aseptically to already-autoclaved medium
recipes.storage: 4 C, inverted, in the dark (foil-wrapped or in a light-proof bag)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 month at 4 C protected from light
recipes.hazards: X-gal stock is dissolved in DMF, a reproductive toxin - handle in a fume hood with nitrile gloves.
notes: Surface-spread alternative (uses far less X-gal and is the more common bench practice): onto a pre-poured antibiotic LB plate, spread 40 uL of 20 mg/mL X-gal plus 100 uL of 0.1 M IPTG, and let it absorb for at least 30 min at 37 C before plating cells.
Requires a lacZ-deltaM15 host (DH5-alpha, JM109, TOP10, XL1-Blue) and a vector carrying lacZ-alpha with the MCS inside it.
Blue colour needs 16-20 h at 37 C. If colonies are pale, move plates to 4 C for 2-4 h - the indigo dye intensifies in the cold.
Faint-blue colonies can still be recombinant (small in-frame inserts); pick clearly white colonies and confirm by colony PCR or restriction digest.
IPTG can be omitted with lacI-negative hosts, but including it gives more reliable colour.

recipes.name: TAE Electrophoresis Buffer, 50x
recipes.summary: Concentrated stock for agarose gel running buffer; dilute 1:50 to 1x (40 mM Tris-acetate, 1 mM EDTA).

components.name: Tris base
components.form: Tris base (free base, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane), MW 121.14, CAS 77-86-1
components.note: 242 g/L = 2.00 M in the 50x stock, giving 40 mM Tris in 1x.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: 57.1 mL per litre of 50x. Density 1.049 g/mL, MW 60.05, so this is ~1.0 M acetate in 50x = 20 mM in 1x.
components.hazard: Corrosive, flammable. Add in a fume hood; always acid to water.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA, pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: = 100 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre of 50x; yields 1 mM EDTA in 1x.

recipes.sterilization: none (autoclaving optional; buffer is not required to be sterile)
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard if precipitate or microbial growth appears
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and flammable.
notes: Canonical Sambrook/CSH recipe: 242 g Tris base + 57.1 mL glacial acetic acid + 100 mL 0.5 M EDTA (pH 8.0), water to 1 L.
pH is NOT adjusted - it comes out at ~8.5 (1x TAE measures ~8.3-8.5). Do not titrate.
50x TAE is close to saturation; if it precipitates on cold storage, warm and stir before diluting, or make 25x instead.
TAE has lower buffering capacity than TBE and will exhaust on long/high-voltage runs (anode acidifies). Recirculate or replace buffer for runs >2 h.
TAE is preferred over TBE when DNA will be gel-extracted, because borate inhibits many downstream enzymes and interferes with silica column binding.

recipes.name: TAE Electrophoresis Buffer, 1x (working)
recipes.summary: Working agarose gel running buffer and gel-casting buffer; the standard buffer when DNA will be recovered from the gel.

components.name: 50x TAE stock
components.note: 20 mL of 50x per litre of 1x.

recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: prepare fresh or use within ~1 month; discard if cloudy
notes: Final composition: 40 mM Tris, 20 mM acetate, 1 mM EDTA, pH ~8.3-8.5.
Use the SAME buffer to cast the gel and to fill the tank - mismatched buffer causes distorted, smiling bands.
Low ionic strength: DNA migrates faster than in TBE, and large fragments (>12 kb) resolve better, but buffering capacity is exhausted sooner.

recipes.name: TBE Electrophoresis Buffer, 10x
recipes.summary: Concentrated stock for agarose/polyacrylamide running buffer; dilute 1:10 to 1x (89 mM Tris-borate, 2 mM EDTA).

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 0.89 M = 107.8 g/L; the canonical recipe rounds this to 108 g/L.

components.name: Boric acid
components.note: 0.89 M = 55.0 g/L; canonical recipe states 55 g/L.
components.hazard: Boric acid is classified as a reproductive toxicant (EU H360FD). Weigh in a fume hood or with dust control; avoid inhaling powder.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA, pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: = 40 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre of 10x; yields 2 mM EDTA in 1x. Equivalent from powder: 7.44 g/L Na2EDTA*2H2O (MW 372.24).

recipes.storage: room temperature, in glass or PETG; keep out of the cold room
recipes.shelfLife: 3-6 months; discard once crystals form and will not redissolve
recipes.hazards: Boric acid - reproductive toxicant; handle powder with care.
notes: Canonical recipe: 108 g Tris base + 55 g boric acid + 40 mL 0.5 M EDTA (pH 8.0), water to 1 L. pH lands at ~8.3 with no titration.
10x TBE precipitates on storage (borate-EDTA crystals). Many labs make 5x TBE instead, which is more stable - see the separate 5x recipe.
Do NOT autoclave in a tightly closed bottle; loosen the cap.

Prefer TBE for small fragments (<1 kb), sharp bands, and long or high-voltage runs. Avoid TBE if the DNA will be gel-purified or used directly in enzymatic reactions - borate inhibits ligase, polymerases, and silica-column binding.

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-HCl, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Workhorse buffer stock for TE, restriction/ligation buffers, and lysis buffers at near-neutral pH.

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 121.14 g per litre. Do NOT substitute Tris-HCl salt (MW 157.60) at this mass - see notes.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 M / 37%)
components.note: Titrant only; not a fixed amount. Approximately 65 mL per litre to reach pH 7.5 (CSH tabulates 70 mL for pH 7.4 and 60 mL for pH 7.6).
components.hazard: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and produces irritating fumes - add in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (sterile, unopened aliquots)
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive.
notes: Tris pH is strongly temperature dependent: pH falls ~0.03 units per 1 C rise. Titrate at the temperature the buffer will be USED at, and always let the solution cool to room temperature before the final pH reading.
Alternative without HCl titration: mix 1 M Tris base (121.14 g/L) with 1 M Tris-HCl (157.60 g/L) until pH 7.5, or dissolve both salts together.

Use a Tris-compatible electrode. The usual failure is at the REFERENCE JUNCTION, not the glass membrane: Ag+ leaching from an Ag/AgCl reference precipitates in the junction in Tris and protein-containing media, producing a drifting liquid-junction potential and errors of up to several tenths of a pH unit. Use a calomel or double-junction reference electrode, calibrated at the temperature you titrate at. CORRECTED: the previous note stated that standard glass electrodes 'read high in amine buffers' - the sign of the error is not reliably predictable, so do not assume a direction and mentally correct for it.
If the 1 M solution is yellow, the Tris is poor quality - discard it (CSH).
Tris is a poor buffer below pH 7.2; for pH <7.2 use phosphate or HEPES instead.

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-HCl, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Standard stock for TE buffer, DNA elution buffers, and most cloning buffers.

components.name: Tris base
components.note: 121.14 g per litre.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 M / 37%)
components.note: Titrant only. CSH tabulates ~42 mL per litre to reach pH 8.0.
components.hazard: Corrosive; add in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (sterile aliquots)
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive.
notes: CSH procedure: dissolve 121.1 g Tris base in 800 mL H2O, add ~42 mL conc. HCl, cool to room temperature, make final pH adjustment, bring to 1 L, aliquot and autoclave.

pH drops ~0.03 units per 1 C increase - a buffer titrated to 8.0 at 25 C reads ~8.6 at 4-5 C. CORRECTED from '~8.5', which was inconsistent with the ~0.03/C coefficient applied over a 21 C drop; Sigma's Trizma pH-vs-temperature table gives 8.58 at 5 C for a solution that is 8.00 at 25 C.
Equivalent all-salt route: 1 M Tris-HCl salt is MW 157.60 (CAS 1185-53-1); mixing Tris base and Tris-HCl salt avoids handling concentrated acid.

recipes.name: 0.5 M EDTA, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Chelator stock used in TE, TAE, TBE, and loading dyes; sequesters Mg2+ to inhibit nucleases.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.form: Na2EDTA*2H2O (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate), MW 372.24, CAS 6381-92-6
components.note: 186.12 g per litre. Do NOT use free-acid EDTA (MW 292.24) or the tetrasodium salt at this mass.

components.name: NaOH pellets
components.note: Titrant only - roughly 20 g of NaOH pellets per litre to bring the slurry to pH 8.0.
components.hazard: NaOH is caustic; the solution heats as pellets dissolve.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (pellets or 10 N)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle)
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
recipes.hazards: NaOH pellets are corrosive - wear eye protection; solution warms during dissolution.
notes: CRITICAL: Na2EDTA*2H2O will not dissolve until the pH is raised to ~8.0 with NaOH. The powder stays as a white slurry - keep adding NaOH with vigorous stirring and it clears suddenly near pH 8.
CSH procedure: 186.1 g Na2EDTA*2H2O into 800 mL H2O, stir vigorously, adjust to pH 8.0 with NaOH (~20 g pellets), aliquot, autoclave.
If your bottle is labelled 'EDTA free acid' (MW 292.24) use 146.12 g/L and expect to need substantially more NaOH.
Never overshoot past pH 8.0 with NaOH - back-titrating with HCl adds unwanted chloride.
