recipes.name: TE Buffer, pH 8.0 (1x)
recipes.summary: Standard buffer for dissolving and storing DNA; EDTA chelates Mg2+ to block nuclease activity.

components.name: Tris-HCl (from 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 8.0 per litre.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 2 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per litre.

recipes.adjustPHWith: already at pH from the component stocks; verify and adjust with HCl/NaOH if needed
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature; 4 C for long-term DNA storage
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sterile
notes: pH 8.0 TE is the default for genomic and plasmid DNA - alkaline pH suppresses acid-catalysed depurination.

The 1 mM EDTA can inhibit downstream reactions that need Mg2+ (PCR with small template volumes, some restriction digests). For those, use low-TE (10 mM Tris, 0.1 mM EDTA) or nuclease-free water instead.
10x TE (100 mM Tris-Cl, 10 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) can be made by scaling both stocks 10-fold; dilute before use.
For RNA, do not use DEPC-treated water to make Tris buffers (DEPC reacts with the primary amine of Tris) - use certified nuclease-free water.

recipes.summary: TE variant at pH 7.5, used where a near-neutral buffer is preferred (e.g. hybridization, some enzyme storage).

components.name: Tris-HCl (from 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 stock)
components.note: 10 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.5 per litre.

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA pH 8.0 stock)

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH if verification is off

recipes.name: 3 M Sodium Acetate, pH 5.2
recipes.summary: Salt stock for ethanol/isopropanol precipitation of DNA and RNA (use at 1/10 volume, 0.3 M final).

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.note: 408.2 g per litre. If using ANHYDROUS sodium acetate (MW 82.03, CAS 127-09-3) use 246.1 g/L instead - substituting masses between the two forms is the most common error with this recipe.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Titrant only - add dropwise to bring the solution to pH 5.2.
components.hazard: Corrosive and flammable; use in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, titrate, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive and flammable.
notes: CSH procedure: dissolve 408.3 g sodium acetate*3H2O in 800 mL H2O, adjust to pH 5.2 with glacial acetic acid (or to pH 7.0 with dilute acetic acid), bring to 1 L, aliquot, autoclave.
Standard use: add 1/10 volume of 3 M NaOAc pH 5.2 to the nucleic acid sample (0.3 M final), then 2-2.5 volumes of cold 100% ethanol (or 0.7 volume isopropanol).
pH 5.2 is preferred for DNA; the mildly acidic pH improves recovery of small fragments. Use 3 M sodium acetate pH 7.0 or ammonium acetate when carrying material into reactions sensitive to low pH.
For RNA work, prepare with nuclease-free water and dedicate the bottle to RNA only.
Ammonium acetate (2.5-7.5 M) is a common alternative that leaves less dNTP carryover, but it must NOT be used before T4 polynucleotide kinase reactions.

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 0.8% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Low-percentage analytical gel for resolving large DNA - genomic DNA checks, linearized plasmids, ~0.8-12 kb fragments.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 0.8 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TAE or 1x TBE running buffer
components.note: Shown as dilution of 50x TAE. If using TBE, dilute 10x TBE 1:10 (or 5x TBE 1:10 for 0.5x). The gel must be cast in the SAME buffer used in the tank.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE (the buffer IS the solvent - do not use plain water)
recipes.sterilization: none (microwaving to dissolve is effectively pasteurizing)
recipes.storage: cast and use the same day; poured gels can be kept overnight at 4 C wrapped in cling film or submerged in buffer
recipes.shelfLife: use within 24 h of casting
recipes.hazards: Molten agarose is >90 C and superheats in the microwave - it can boil over violently. Loosen the cap, heat in short bursts, swirl, and wear heat-resistant gloves.
notes: Resolution range at 0.8%: roughly 0.8-10 kb linear DNA (interpolated between the standard 0.7% = 0.8-12 kb and 1.0% = 0.5-10 kb rows).
Weigh agarose into the flask, add buffer, swirl, then microwave in 30-45 s bursts with swirling until no lens-shaped undissolved particles remain when held to the light.
Cool to ~55-60 C before adding stain and pouring, or the tray will warp and the stain will degrade.
Add stain after cooling: 1x SYBR Safe (1 uL of 10,000x per 10 mL) or ethidium bromide to 0.5 ug/mL final.
Run at 5-10 V/cm of inter-electrode distance; for large fragments/genomic DNA drop to 1-2 V/cm.

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 1% (w/v)
recipes.summary: The default all-purpose analytical gel; resolves roughly 0.5-10 kb linear DNA.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 1.0 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TAE or 1x TBE running buffer
components.note: Shown as dilution of 50x TAE; substitute 10x TBE at 1:10 if running TBE.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE (the buffer IS the solvent)
recipes.storage: use same day
recipes.hazards: Superheated molten agarose - risk of boil-over and burns.
notes: Standard resolution range: ~0.5-10 kb linear DNA (NEB/Sambrook).
Typical mini-gel: 0.5 g agarose in 50 mL 1x TAE.
Gel thickness of 4-6 mm is ideal; thicker gels need more stain and image poorly.
Comb wells hold roughly 4-5 uL per mm of well width at 5 mm gel depth - do not overload or bands will smile and streak.
Add stain at ~55 C: 5 uL of 10,000x SYBR Safe per 50 mL, or ethidium bromide to 0.5 ug/mL.

recipes.summary: Mid-high percentage gel for PCR products and smaller fragments; resolves roughly 0.2-3 kb.

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 1.5 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TAE or 1x TBE running buffer
components.note: TBE gives noticeably sharper bands at this percentage; use TAE if the band will be excised and purified.

recipes.storage: use same day
recipes.hazards: Superheated molten agarose - risk of boil-over and burns.
notes: Standard resolution range: ~0.2-3 kb linear DNA.
Higher-percentage gels take longer to dissolve and set faster - pour promptly once cooled to ~55-60 C.
Run slightly slower (4-6 V/cm) than a 1% gel to avoid heating and band distortion.

recipes.name: Agarose Gel, 2% (w/v)
recipes.summary: High-percentage gel for small fragments - genotyping, colony PCR, and short amplicons (~50 bp-2 kb).

components.name: Agarose (molecular biology / electrophoresis grade)
components.note: 2.0 g per 100 mL of 1x running buffer.

components.name: 1x TBE (preferred) or 1x TAE running buffer
components.note: Shown as dilution of 10x TBE. TBE is strongly preferred at 2% for resolving small fragments.

recipes.storage: use same day
recipes.hazards: Superheated molten agarose - risk of boil-over and burns.
notes: Standard resolution range: ~0.05-2 kb linear DNA.
2% gels are brittle and dissolve slowly - use extra buffer to compensate for evaporation during microwaving and reweigh/top up before pouring.
For fragments below ~100 bp, use 3-4% agarose, a high-resolution/low-melt sieving agarose, or switch to polyacrylamide.

Xylene cyanol FF co-migrates with ~4 kb DNA at 1% agarose and with ~750-800 bp at 2%; bromophenol blue runs at ~300-370 bp at 1% and ~150 bp at 2%. At 2% the xylene cyanol band sits right in the middle of the size range of interest and can mask bands, so consider a bromophenol-blue-only loading dye at high percentages.

CORRECTED: an earlier version of this note assigned the ~750 bp figure to a 1% gel. ~750-800 bp is the 2% value; at 1% xylene cyanol runs at ~4 kb (Thermo R0611 product data; consistent with the 6x loading dye recipe in this file).

recipes.name: 6x DNA Gel-Loading Dye (glycerol, Sambrook Buffer III)
aka: bromophenol blue / xylene cyanol loading dye
recipes.summary: Classic two-dye loading buffer; glycerol sinks the sample into the well and the dyes track the run front.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 30 mL per 100 mL. Glycerol is viscous - pipette slowly or weigh it (density 1.26 g/mL).

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.note: 0.25 g per 100 mL.

components.name: Xylene cyanol FF
components.note: 0.25 g per 100 mL.

recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
notes: Use at 1x final: add 1 volume of 6x dye to 5 volumes of sample (e.g. 2 uL dye + 10 uL sample).

Dye migration in a 1% agarose gel: bromophenol blue runs with ~300-500 bp DNA, xylene cyanol FF with ~4 kb. In 1x TBE bromophenol blue tracks ~370 bp and xylene cyanol ~4.9 kb. These shift with agarose percentage - check the position before assuming a band is the dye.
This dye contains NO EDTA. If your samples come from a Mg2+-containing reaction and will sit before loading, prefer the EDTA-containing 6x dye.
Sambrook Buffer I is the sucrose version (40% w/v sucrose, 0.25% bromophenol blue, 0.25% xylene cyanol FF) - denser and equally acceptable.

recipes.name: 6x DNA Gel-Loading Dye with EDTA (Thermo R0611 formulation)
recipes.summary: Commercial-equivalent 6x loading dye containing EDTA to quench Mg2+-dependent nucleases and stop enzymatic reactions.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: 60 mL per 100 mL. Very viscous - warm slightly and pipette slowly, or weigh (density 1.26 g/mL).

components.name: Tris-HCl (from 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.6 stock)
components.note: 1 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.6 per 100 mL. Make the pH 7.6 stock as for pH 7.5/8.0 (121.14 g/L Tris base, ~60 mL conc. HCl per litre per CSH).

components.name: EDTA (from 0.5 M Na2EDTA pH 8.0 stock)
components.note: 12 mL of 0.5 M EDTA pH 8.0 per 100 mL. Gives 10 mM EDTA at 1x - enough to chelate reaction Mg2+.

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.note: 30 mg per 100 mL.

components.name: Xylene cyanol FF
components.note: 30 mg per 100 mL.

recipes.adjustPHWith: set by the Tris-HCl stock; verify
recipes.sterilization: none (0.22 um filter optional; 60% glycerol filters slowly)
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C; the manufacturer ships this formulation for ambient/4 C storage
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more
notes: Published composition of Thermo Scientific 6X DNA Loading Dye (#R0611): 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.6, 0.03% bromophenol blue, 0.03% xylene cyanol FF, 60% glycerol, 60 mM EDTA.
Use at 1x: 1 volume dye to 5 volumes sample.
The 60% glycerol makes samples sink cleanly and is more forgiving than 30% when loading into deep wells under buffer.
The EDTA stops restriction digests and other Mg2+-dependent reactions on contact - useful when loading digests straight from the block.
Dye colour is much fainter than the 0.25% Sambrook formulation; that is intentional, so the dye does not obscure faint bands.

recipes.summary: Concentrated intercalating DNA stain stock; diluted 1:20,000 into gels or staining baths.

components.name: Ethidium bromide
components.note: 10 g/L is identical to 10 mg/mL (= 25.4 mM). MW 394.31, CAS 1239-45-8. Dissolve powder by stirring on a magnetic stirrer for several hours until fully dissolved.
components.hazard: POWERFUL MUTAGEN, moderately toxic. Weighing the powder is the highest-risk step - buy the ready-made 10 mg/mL solution if at all possible.

recipes.storage: 4 C, wrapped in aluminium foil or in an amber bottle; light-sensitive
recipes.shelfLife: several years if protected from light
recipes.hazards: Potent mutagen and irritant. Wear nitrile gloves (change often - EtBr penetrates latex), lab coat, and eye protection. Handle powder only in a fume hood with a dust mask. Never pour EtBr solutions down the drain - collect as regulated chemical waste or run through activated-charcoal destaining media per your institution's EHS policy.
notes: If weighing powder: work in a fume hood, decontaminate the balance afterwards, and dispose of weigh boats as hazardous waste. Most labs now buy the 10 mg/mL aqueous solution ready-made specifically to avoid this.
Do not autoclave EtBr solutions - autoclaving does not destroy it and aerosolizes the compound.
SYBR Safe or GelRed/GelGreen are lower-hazard alternatives and are preferred for teaching labs and new setups.

recipes.name: Ethidium Bromide Working Solution, 0.5 ug/mL
recipes.summary: Working dilution for casting into agarose gels or for post-electrophoresis staining baths.

components.name: Ethidium bromide (from 10 mg/mL stock)
components.note: 1:20,000 dilution = 5 uL of 10 mg/mL stock per 100 mL. For a 50 mL mini-gel that is 2.5 uL.
components.hazard: Mutagen - gloves, and treat gel, buffer, and tips as EtBr waste.

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE (for in-gel staining, add to molten agarose cooled to ~55 C) or water (for a post-run staining bath)
recipes.storage: staining baths can be reused for weeks in a covered, foil-wrapped tray at room temperature; label clearly as EtBr
recipes.shelfLife: reuse bath until sensitivity drops (typically several weeks)
recipes.hazards: Mutagen. All contact materials (gel, buffer, gloves, tips, trays) become EtBr waste. Never discard to drain.
notes: Do not exceed 0.5 ug/mL final in the gel, the running buffer, or the staining bath - higher concentrations raise background and retard DNA migration.
In-gel staining: add EtBr to molten agarose at ~55 C, swirl, then pour. The DNA stains as it runs.

Post-staining: after the run, soak the gel in 0.5 ug/mL EtBr for 15-30 min with gentle agitation, then destain in water for 15-30 min to lower background. Post-staining gives better sensitivity for small fragments because EtBr does not migrate toward the cathode during the run.

Ethidium bromide intercalates into DNA, and at 0.5 ug/mL it slows the migration of linear DNA by roughly 15%. Note this if you are sizing precisely, and never size a stained sample against a ladder run in an unstained gel.
Visualize on a 300 nm UV transilluminator (Ex ~300/520 nm, Em ~600 nm). Wear a UV face shield; prolonged UV exposure nicks DNA intended for cloning.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich - Ethidium bromide technical article (0.5 ug/mL limit, post-staining protocol)
Bio-Rad - Ethidium Bromide Solution product literature

recipes.name: SYBR Safe DNA Gel Stain, 1x (in-gel working solution)
recipes.summary: Reduced-mutagenicity alternative to ethidium bromide, cast directly into the agarose gel at 1x.

components.name: SYBR Safe DNA Gel Stain (10,000x concentrate in DMSO)
components.note: 1:10,000 dilution = 10 uL of 10,000x concentrate per 100 mL of gel (3 uL per 30 mL; 5 uL per 50 mL).

recipes.solvent: 1x TAE or 1x TBE agarose gel solution
recipes.storage: 10,000x concentrate: any temperature from 2 C to 25 C, protected from light. Do not freeze-cycle unnecessarily - DMSO freezes below ~18 C, so thaw completely and mix before use.
recipes.shelfLife: per manufacturer lot expiry (typically 1 year+); protect from light
recipes.hazards: Formulated for reduced mutagenicity versus ethidium bromide, but it is still a DNA-binding dye - wear gloves and dispose per institutional policy. The concentrate is in DMSO, which promotes skin absorption of dissolved solutes.
notes: Recommended method: dilute the 10,000x concentrate 1:10,000 into the gel buffer FIRST, mix, then add the powdered agarose and melt. Alternatively add the concentrate to molten agarose cooled to ~55 C and swirl thoroughly.
Worked example from the manufacturer guide: for 30 mL of molten agarose, add 3 uL of 10,000x concentrate to 30 mL of 1x TBE, mix, then add agarose powder.
The molten agarose may look pale orange - this is normal.

Ex/Em approximately 280/502 nm and 530 nm bound to DNA. Visualize on a blue-light transilluminator (~470-500 nm) for best sensitivity and to avoid UV damage; a 300 nm UV transilluminator also works but is less sensitive.
DNA appears as bright green bands on a dark background.
Post-electrophoresis staining is also possible: soak the gel in a 1x solution in running buffer for ~30 min, protected from light.

recipes.name: DEPC-Treated Water (RNase-free water)
recipes.summary: Water treated with diethylpyrocarbonate to inactivate RNases, then autoclaved to destroy residual DEPC; for RNA work.

components.name: Diethylpyrocarbonate (DEPC)
components.note: 1 mL of fresh DEPC per litre of water. Use a fresh bottle - DEPC hydrolyses on storage and old stock is ineffective.
components.hazard: Suspected carcinogen; lachrymator; reacts with water to release CO2 (pressure build-up). Dispense in a fume hood only.

recipes.sterilization: REQUIRED: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (15 psi, liquid cycle) after DEPC incubation, to hydrolyse residual DEPC to ethanol and CO2
recipes.storage: room temperature in a dedicated RNase-free bottle; aliquot into single-use volumes
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months in sealed sterile aliquots; discard any aliquot that has been pipetted into
recipes.hazards: DEPC is a suspected carcinogen and a strong lachrymator. Handle the neat liquid only in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection. Bottles must be loosely capped during incubation and autoclaving - CO2 evolution can burst a sealed bottle.
notes: CSH procedure: add 1 mL of fresh DEPC to 1 L of H2O, shake well to disperse, incubate at 37 C for at least 12 h and/or autoclave at 15 psi on liquid cycle for 20 min to inactivate the remaining DEPC.

CRITICAL: DEPC reacts with primary amines, so it CANNOT be used to treat Tris buffers (or HEPES, or any amine-containing solution). Prepare Tris solutions using already-DEPC-treated-and-autoclaved water, or use certified nuclease-free water.
The autoclave step is not optional. Residual DEPC carboxymethylates adenine residues in RNA and will inhibit downstream reverse transcription and in vitro translation.

Modern practice (including Thermo/Ambion guidance) is that commercially supplied certified nuclease-free water is preferable to lab-made DEPC water: it avoids DEPC exposure and eliminates residual-DEPC artefacts. Reserve DEPC treatment for glassware/solutions where no certified alternative exists.
For RNase-free technique generally: dedicate pipettes and filter tips, wear gloves and change them often, and bake glassware at 180-250 C for 4 h rather than DEPC-treating it.

recipes.name: PBS (Phosphate-Buffered Saline), 10x, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: 10x concentrated phosphate-buffered saline stock; dilute 1:10 for washes, dilutions and blocking buffers.

components.name: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
components.note: MW 58.44; gives 1.37 M in the 10x stock = 137 mM at 1x

components.name: Potassium chloride (KCl)
components.note: MW 74.55; 27 mM in 10x = 2.7 mM at 1x

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, ANHYDROUS (Na2HPO4)
components.note: Anhydrous form, MW 141.96. If you only have the heptahydrate Na2HPO4*7H2O (MW 268.07) use 27.2 g/L; for the dihydrate Na2HPO4*2H2O (MW 177.99) use 18.1 g/L. Using a hydrate at the anhydrous weight under-doses phosphate by up to 47%.

components.name: Potassium phosphate monobasic, anhydrous (KH2PO4)
components.note: MW 136.09; 18 mM in 10x = 1.8 mM at 1x

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl (adjust the 10x stock to pH 7.4; some protocols leave the 10x unadjusted and pH only after dilution)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 15 psi on liquid cycle, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months; discard if cloudy or if precipitate forms
notes: Contains no Ca2+ or Mg2+. To make the Ca/Mg-containing version add 1.33 g/L CaCl2*2H2O (MW 147.01) and 1.0 g/L MgCl2*6H2O (MW 203.30) to the 10x -- but add these AFTER autoclaving and only to phosphate-free diluent, or they will precipitate.
The 10x stock may precipitate at 4 C. Warm to room temperature and mix until clear before diluting.
pH rises slightly on dilution; check pH of the 1x working solution if the application is pH-sensitive.

recipes.name: PBS (Phosphate-Buffered Saline), 1x, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: Standard isotonic wash and dilution buffer for cells, blots and immunoassays.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, ANHYDROUS (Na2HPO4)
components.note: Anhydrous, MW 141.96; 10 mM. Heptahydrate Na2HPO4*7H2O (MW 268.07) = 2.72 g/L; dihydrate Na2HPO4*2H2O (MW 177.99) = 1.81 g/L.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl (or NaOH if overshot); pH 7.2 is used for some cell-culture applications
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 20 min at 15 psi on liquid cycle, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature; 4 C once opened for cell-culture use
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months sterile; discard immediately if turbid (microbial growth)
notes: Easiest route is a 1:10 dilution of the 10x stock; the direct weigh-out above is for when you want a single-use batch at exactly pH 7.4.
For PBS-T (wash buffer) add Tween-20 to 0.05-0.1% (v/v) after the buffer is made up to volume.

recipes.name: TBS (Tris-Buffered Saline), 10x, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: 10x Tris-buffered saline stock; the preferred wash base for phospho-specific western blots (phosphate-free).

components.name: Tris base (free base, Trizma base)
components.note: 24.2 g/L. If you weigh Tris-HCl (MW 157.60) instead you will get the wrong pH and need NaOH rather than HCl to reach 7.5.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (combine in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15-20 min (optional for blotting use)
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
notes: 1x working solution = 20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, pH 7.5.
A pH 7.6 variant (24 g Tris base + 88 g NaCl per litre) is equally common and interchangeable for western blotting.
Tris pH is strongly temperature-dependent (approx. -0.028 pH units per degree C). pH the stock at the temperature at which it will be used, or at least record the temperature.

recipes.name: TBS-T (Tris-Buffered Saline + Tween-20), 1x
recipes.summary: Standard western blot wash buffer: 20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, pH 7.5.

components.name: Tween-20 (polysorbate 20)
components.note: Viscous. Cut the pipette tip or use a positive-displacement pipette, and rinse the tip in the buffer to deliver the full volume.

recipes.adjustPHWith: none required if the 10x TBS was correctly adjusted
recipes.storage: room temperature for up to 1 week; 4 C for longer
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 weeks; make fresh weekly -- Tween-20 supports microbial growth and oxidises over time
notes: 0.05% Tween-20 is also widely used and gives less stringent washing; 0.1% is the Cell Signaling Technology standard.
Add Tween-20 AFTER diluting the TBS and bringing close to volume -- adding it early causes heavy foaming that makes volume measurement inaccurate.
Do not make a 10x TBST stock and store it long-term; peroxide impurities accumulate in concentrated Tween-20 and can quench HRP signal.
Swap 10x TBS for 10x PBS to make PBS-T; PBS-T is fine for most blots but should NOT be used with phospho-specific antibodies or when the detection chemistry is phosphate-sensitive.

recipes.name: SDS-PAGE Running Buffer (Tris-Glycine-SDS), 10x
recipes.summary: 10x stock of the Laemmli discontinuous running buffer for denaturing SDS-PAGE.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.note: MW 121.14; 248 mM in the 10x = ~25 mM at 1x

components.name: Glycine
components.note: MW 75.07; 1.92 M in the 10x = 192 mM at 1x

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate), electrophoresis grade
components.hazard: Fine powder is a serious respiratory and eye irritant -- weigh in a fume hood or behind a shield and wear a mask.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NONE -- do not adjust the pH
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months (10x stock); discard 1x buffer after 1-2 runs
recipes.hazards: SDS powder: respiratory/eye irritant. Avoid generating dust.
notes: Critical: do NOT titrate this buffer with HCl or NaOH. The correct pH of 8.3 arises from the Tris:glycine ratio; adding chloride destroys the stacking behaviour of the discontinuous system and causes smeared or distorted bands.
1x working buffer = 25 mM Tris, 192 mM glycine, 0.1% (w/v) SDS.
Dissolve the Tris and glycine fully before adding SDS, and stir slowly -- SDS foams badly.
The 10x stock may precipitate SDS at low room temperature. Warm to ~37 C and mix until clear before diluting; do not decant off the precipitate.
For native PAGE use the same recipe but omit the SDS entirely (10x Tris-glycine, pH 8.3).

VERIFIED: the 30.0 g/L Tris figure matches the cited Cold Spring Harbor recipe exactly and gives 248 mM (24.8 mM at 1x). Many other protocols -- including the 10x transfer buffer elsewhere in this file -- specify 30.3 g/L, which gives exactly 250 mM. The 0.8% difference has no effect on a run; do not be alarmed by the mismatch between the two stocks.

recipes.name: 1.5 M Tris-Cl, pH 8.8 (Resolving Gel Buffer, 4x)
recipes.summary: Stock buffer for the resolving (lower) layer of a Laemmli SDS-PAGE gel; used as one quarter of the gel volume to give 375 mM final.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base, titrated to pH 8.8 with HCl
components.note: 181.5 g/L as published by Cold Spring Harbor. Strict arithmetic gives 1.5 M x 121.14 = 181.7 g/L; the 0.1% difference is immaterial at the bench, and either weight is fine. Do not substitute Tris-HCl powder (MW 157.60).

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~850 mL, pH, cool to room temperature, re-check pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 6 M HCl
recipes.sterilization: none (0.22 um filter optional to extend shelf life)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard if cloudy or contaminated
notes: CORRECTED: an earlier version of this note said the solution 'gets cold'. That is wrong in net terms. Dissolving Tris base is mildly endothermic, but bringing 1.5 M Tris down to pH 8.8 consumes roughly 0.85 mol HCl per litre and that neutralisation is strongly exothermic -- the solution ends up WARM, by of order 10 degrees C. Cold Spring Harbor's recipe accordingly instructs: cool the solution to room temperature and readjust the pH to 8.8. Because Tris pH shifts about -0.028 units per degree C, pH-ing while warm and then cooling leaves the finished buffer too ALKALINE (pH ~9.2 if you titrated at 35 C), and the gel runs at the wrong pH.
Some laboratories include 0.4% (w/v) SDS in this buffer (a '4x' buffer that supplies both the Tris and the SDS). If you use that version, omit the separate 10% SDS from the gel mix.

CLARIFIED: use this buffer as one QUARTER of the final gel volume (2.5 mL per 10 mL of gel mix), giving 375 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.8 in the cast resolving gel. This is a 1-in-4 dilution, not a 1:4 ratio of buffer to everything else (which would be one fifth, 300 mM).

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Resolving gel buffer (4x)
Laemmli UK (1970) Nature 227:680-685

recipes.name: 0.5 M Tris-Cl, pH 6.8 (Stacking Gel Buffer, 4x)
recipes.summary: Stock buffer for the stacking (upper) layer of a Laemmli SDS-PAGE gel; used as one quarter of the gel volume to give 125 mM final.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base, titrated to pH 6.8 with HCl
components.note: 60.5-60.6 g/L (0.5 M x 121.14 = 60.57 g/L). Do not substitute Tris-HCl powder (MW 157.60). Reaching pH 6.8 from Tris base requires near-complete protonation and therefore a large volume of HCl; the solution warms strongly, so cool to room temperature before the final pH check.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~850 mL, pH, cool to room temperature, re-check pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 6 M HCl
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
notes: pH 6.8 is the single most important number in the Laemmli system -- it sets the glycine ionisation that produces band stacking. Check it carefully at room temperature.
A 1.0 M pH 6.8 stock is also common; if you use 1.0 M, halve the volume in the gel recipe to keep the stacking gel at 125 mM.

CLARIFIED: use this buffer as one QUARTER of the final gel volume (0.75 mL per 3 mL of gel mix), giving 125 mM Tris-Cl pH 6.8 in the cast stacking gel. This is a 1-in-4 dilution, not a 1:4 ratio of buffer to everything else.

recipes.name: 10% (w/v) SDS Stock Solution
recipes.summary: Working detergent stock used in gel casting, lysis buffers and running/transfer buffers.

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate), electrophoresis grade
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory and eye irritant; weigh in a fume hood wearing a dust mask.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (heat to ~68 C with gentle stirring to dissolve, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: none normally required; a few protocols adjust to pH 7.2 with HCl
recipes.sterilization: DO NOT AUTOCLAVE. 0.22 um filter if sterility is needed.
recipes.storage: room temperature -- never 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: SDS dust is harmful if inhaled and irritating to eyes and airways.
notes: Stir gently. Vigorous stirring produces a persistent foam that makes it impossible to bring the solution accurately to volume.
SDS crystallises out below ~15 C. If the stock is cloudy, warm to 37-50 C and mix until completely clear before pipetting -- otherwise the delivered concentration is wrong.
Autoclaving hydrolyses SDS and is not recommended.

recipes.name: 10% (w/v) Ammonium Persulfate (APS)
recipes.summary: Free-radical initiator for polyacrylamide gel polymerisation; used with TEMED.

components.name: Ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8)
components.hazard: Strong oxidiser; harmful if swallowed, causes skin/eye irritation and respiratory sensitisation.
components.note: MW 228.20; 0.1 g per 1 mL = 438 mM

recipes.storage: prepare fresh, or aliquot and store at -20 C; the solid powder is stored at room temperature in a desiccator
recipes.shelfLife: best made fresh on the day; up to 1 week at 4 C, or ~2-3 months as single-use frozen aliquots
recipes.hazards: Strong oxidising agent. Avoid contact with combustible material. Wear gloves and eye protection.
notes: Most gels fail to polymerise because of old APS, not bad acrylamide. If a gel is slow to set, make fresh APS first.
Typical scale is small: 0.1 g in 1 mL of water. Freeze in 100-200 uL single-use aliquots and do not refreeze.
A useful freshness test: fresh 10% APS crackles/fizzes faintly when the powder hits the water.
Used at 0.1% (w/v) final in both resolving and stacking gels -- i.e. 1 part 10% APS per 100 parts gel volume.

recipes.name: Laemmli / SDS Sample (Loading) Buffer, 4x
recipes.summary: Denaturing, reducing loading buffer; mix 1 part with 3 parts protein sample and heat before loading.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 6.8
components.note: CORRECTED: this previously specified the 0.5 M stacking-gel stock, which at 200 mM final would occupy 40% of the volume; together with 40% glycerol that leaves no room for the SDS, dye and reductant. Use a 1 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock = 20% v/v (2 mL per 10 mL of 4x buffer). If made from powder, use Tris base (MW 121.14) titrated to pH 6.8 with HCl.

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate)
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory irritant.
components.note: MW 288.38; gives 2% at 1x

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: Density 1.26 g/mL; adds sample density so it sinks into the well. Gives 10% at 1x.

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.note: MW 691.94 (sodium salt) / 669.96 (free acid). See notes -- commercial 4x buffers use far less dye.

components.name: DTT (dithiothreitol)
components.form: add as the SOLID, immediately before use
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; strong odour. Handle in a fume hood.
components.note: CORRECTED: this previously read '400 mM from a 1 M stock', which is 40% of the final volume and cannot coexist with 40% glycerol (the total would exceed 100%). Add DTT as the solid: 0.4 M x 154.25 = 61.7 g/L, i.e. 0.062 g per mL of 4x buffer (0.617 g per 10 mL). The cited CSH recipe states the 400 mM concentration but does not specify a route; the solid is the only route that fits the volume budget.

recipes.adjustPHWith: none -- pH is set by the Tris-Cl stock; do not re-titrate the finished buffer
recipes.storage: store WITHOUT reducing agent at room temperature or 4 C; store complete (reduced) buffer in single-use aliquots at -20 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year without reducing agent; ~6 months as frozen reduced aliquots (do not refreeze)
recipes.hazards: DTT and 2-mercaptoethanol are toxic and malodorous -- prepare and use in a fume hood. 2-mercaptoethanol in particular is acutely toxic.
notes: UNCERTAINTY -- read before use: the bromophenol blue concentration differs substantially between authorities. Cold Spring Harbor Protocols specifies 0.4% (w/v) in the 4x buffer (the value used above); most commercial 4x Laemmli buffers use only 0.02-0.1% (w/v). 0.4% produces a very dark blue front that can obscure low-molecular-weight bands and interfere with UV/stain-free imaging. If you are following a commercial protocol or imaging small proteins, use 0.02-0.1% instead. The dye is a tracking marker only and does not affect separation.

VOLUME BUDGET -- this is why the Tris and DTT entries above were corrected. A 4x buffer is volumetrically crowded: 40% glycerol alone takes nearly half the volume, and 8% SDS plus 400 mM DTT displace roughly another 11%. Any component supplied from a stock dilute enough to need >20% of the volume will not fit. Build it as: 2 mL 1 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 + 4 mL glycerol + 0.8 g SDS + 40 mg bromophenol blue + water to 10 mL, then add 0.617 g solid DTT to the aliquot immediately before use.

Add the reducing agent immediately before use. Alternatively add 2-mercaptoethanol to 20% (v/v) (= 5% v/v, ~715 mM at 1x). If you add BME to an already-finished aliquot rather than building it into the formulation, everything else is diluted ~20% -- either account for that or make the base buffer at 5x and let the BME bring it to 4x.
Omit the reducing agent entirely to run non-reducing gels and preserve disulfide-linked complexes.
Standard use: mix 1 volume of 4x buffer with 3 volumes of sample, then heat 5 min at 95-100 C (or 10 min at 70 C for membrane proteins, which aggregate irreversibly when boiled).
Order of addition matters: water, then Tris, then glycerol with vigorous stirring, then SDS with SLOW stirring to avoid frothing, then the dye.
Equivalent 2x recipe (also from CSH): 100 mM Tris-Cl pH 6.8, 4% SDS, 20% glycerol, 0.2% bromophenol blue, 200 mM DTT -- mix 1:1 with sample.

recipes.name: SDS Gel-Loading Buffer, 2x
recipes.summary: Denaturing reducing loading buffer mixed 1:1 with sample; convenient for dilute protein samples.

components.name: Tris-Cl, pH 6.8
components.note: From the 0.5 M Tris-Cl pH 6.8 stock (Tris base MW 121.14, titrated with HCl)

components.name: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate)
components.hazard: Powder is a respiratory irritant.

components.name: Bromophenol blue (sodium salt)
components.note: MW 691.94; 0.01-0.05% is sufficient in practice and gives a cleaner dye front

components.name: DTT (dithiothreitol)
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; use in a fume hood.
components.note: MW 154.25. Add from a 1 M stock just before use.

recipes.adjustPHWith: none

recipes.name: SDS-PAGE Resolving Gel, 8%
recipes.summary: Laemmli resolving gel for large proteins, roughly 40-200 kDa.

components.name: Acrylamide/bis-acrylamide 37.5:1
components.hazard: Unpolymerised acrylamide is a potent neurotoxin and probable human carcinogen. Always wear nitrile gloves, work in a fume hood, and never handle the powder outside a hood -- buy the ready-made 30% solution.
components.note: 29:1 acrylamide:bis is also used and gives slightly better resolution of small proteins

components.name: Ammonium persulfate (APS)
components.note: ADD LAST, immediately before pouring

components.name: TEMED (N,N,N',N'-tetramethylethylenediamine)
components.hazard: Flammable, corrosive, strong ammoniacal odour -- use in a fume hood.
components.note: ADD LAST with the APS. Increase to 0.1% to speed polymerisation.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume; add the water FIRST, then acrylamide, Tris and SDS, and only then APS/TEMED)
recipes.adjustPHWith: none -- pH comes from the Tris-Cl stock
recipes.storage: cast and use the same day; a poured gel can be kept overnight at 4 C wrapped in tissue moistened with 1x running buffer
recipes.shelfLife: use within 24 h of casting; the un-catalysed mix must be poured within minutes of adding APS/TEMED
recipes.hazards: Acrylamide monomer is a neurotoxin and probable carcinogen; TEMED is flammable and corrosive; APS is a strong oxidiser. Gloves, eye protection and a fume hood are mandatory until the gel has fully polymerised.
notes: Approximate resolving range: 40-200 kDa.

Overlay the poured gel with water-saturated butanol, isopropanol or ethanol to exclude oxygen (which inhibits polymerisation) and give a flat interface. Rinse the overlay off thoroughly before casting the stacker.
Polymerisation takes ~30 min at room temperature. Keep the leftover mix in the tube to judge when the gel has set.
Degassing the mix under vacuum for 10 min before adding APS/TEMED improves reproducibility.
Worked example for 10 mL: 2.7 mL 30% acrylamide, 2.5 mL 1.5 M Tris pH 8.8, 0.1 mL 10% SDS, 0.1 mL 10% APS, 4 uL TEMED, water to 10 mL.

recipes.name: Wet Transfer Buffer (Towbin), 1x
recipes.summary: Standard wet (tank) transfer buffer for moving proteins from a Tris-glycine gel onto nitrocellulose or PVDF.

components.name: Methanol
components.hazard: Flammable and acutely toxic by ingestion, inhalation and skin contact; causes blindness. Handle in a fume hood and collect waste as halogen-free organic solvent.
components.note: Add after the salts have dissolved

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve salts in ~500 mL water, add methanol, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NONE -- do not adjust the pH
recipes.storage: 4 C (pre-chill before use; transfers generate significant heat)
recipes.shelfLife: make fresh, or reuse a maximum of 2-3 times within a week; buffer conductivity rises with each use
recipes.hazards: Methanol is toxic and flammable. Prepare in a fume hood; do not pour down the drain.
notes: Do NOT pH this buffer. The Tris:glycine ratio sets pH ~8.3; adding acid or base introduces chloride, which massively increases current and heating.

Methanol strips SDS from proteins and improves binding to the membrane, but reduces the transfer efficiency of large (>100 kDa) proteins. For proteins >100 kDa, drop methanol to 10% and/or add SDS to 0.01-0.1% (see the SDS note below -- CORRECTED, this figure previously read 0.05-0.1% and contradicted it).
For PVDF, methanol is required (and the membrane must be pre-wetted in 100% methanol). For nitrocellulose, methanol can be reduced or omitted.

Towbin buffer with SDS: add 1-10 mL of 10% SDS per litre (0.01-0.1% final) to aid transfer of large proteins; start at the low end (0.01-0.025%) and increase only if large proteins are still retained in the gel. Note that SDS can reduce binding to nitrocellulose.
Keep the buffer and tank cold (ice pack or 4 C room). Overheating causes gel distortion and poor transfer.

recipes.name: Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250 Staining Solution
recipes.summary: Classic total-protein stain for polyacrylamide gels; detection limit roughly 50-100 ng per band.

components.name: Coomassie Brilliant Blue R-250
components.note: MW 825.96 (sodium salt), CAS 6104-59-2. R-250 (not G-250) is the correct dye for this solution; G-250 is used for colloidal/Bradford methods.

components.name: Methanol
components.hazard: Flammable and acutely toxic; causes blindness. Use in a fume hood.

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns. Always add acid to water, never the reverse.

recipes.sterilization: none (filter through Whatman No. 1 paper to remove undissolved dye)
recipes.storage: room temperature, in a tightly closed bottle away from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; the solution is reusable several times until staining becomes weak
recipes.hazards: Methanol is toxic and flammable; glacial acetic acid is corrosive. Prepare and use in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection. Dispose of as organic/aqueous chemical waste, never down the drain.
notes: MUST be filtered after the dye dissolves. Undissolved dye particles stick to the gel and produce permanent blue speckles.
Typical protocol: stain 30 min to 2 h with gentle agitation (or overnight), then destain.
The solution is reusable -- keep a dedicated staining tray and return the used stain to the bottle.
Ethanol can be substituted for methanol at the same 40% (v/v) if you want to avoid methanol; sensitivity is comparable.
For mass-spectrometry-compatible staining, use a colloidal Coomassie G-250 method instead -- methanol/acetic acid R-250 stain fixes the gel and can hamper in-gel digestion.

recipes.name: Coomassie Destaining Solution
recipes.summary: Removes unbound Coomassie dye from the gel matrix, leaving blue protein bands on a clear background.

components.name: Methanol
components.hazard: Flammable and acutely toxic; causes blindness. Use in a fume hood.

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns.

recipes.storage: room temperature in a closed container
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Methanol is toxic and flammable; glacial acetic acid is corrosive. Fume hood, gloves and eye protection. Collect used destain as chemical waste.
notes: Change the destain several times (every 30-60 min) until the background is clear -- typically 2 h to overnight.

A milder, slower variant used by many labs is 10% methanol / 10% acetic acid; it removes less protein from the gel and is gentler on faint bands. A 30% methanol / 10% acetic acid version is also common. All three work; the higher the alcohol, the faster and more aggressive the destain.
Dropping a folded Kimwipe, a piece of foam or a few Dowex beads into the tray absorbs released dye and speeds destaining considerably.
Prolonged destaining will eventually leach dye from the protein bands themselves -- stop when the background is acceptable.

recipes.name: Ponceau S Staining Solution
recipes.summary: Rapid, fully reversible total-protein stain for checking transfer efficiency on nitrocellulose or PVDF membranes.

components.name: Ponceau S (tetrasodium salt)
components.note: MW 760.55, CAS 6226-79-5, formula C22H12N4Na4O13S4

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.hazard: Corrosive; causes severe burns. Add the acid to the aqueous dye solution, never water to acid.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (CORRECTED: dissolve the dye in ~90% of the final volume of water, then add glacial acetic acid to 5% v/v and bring to final volume. The previous '~95%' left no headroom -- 95% water plus 5% acid is already 100% before the dye's own volume, so the batch would overshoot and end up dilute.)
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; reusable many times until staining becomes faint
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid is corrosive -- handle in a fume hood with gloves and eye protection.
notes: Stains in 1-5 min with gentle rocking. Destain with several rinses of deionized water until bands are visible against a pale background.
Fully reversible: wash the membrane in TBS-T or 0.1 M NaOH for a few minutes to remove all dye before blocking. It does not interfere with subsequent immunodetection.
Image or photocopy the stained membrane immediately -- the stain fades and is removed during blocking, and this image is your only record of loading and transfer quality.
The solution is reusable; pour it back into the bottle after each use.
A more sensitive variant adds trichloroacetic acid (e.g. 0.1% Ponceau S in 5% TCA), but TCA fixes proteins to the membrane and can reduce antibody accessibility.

recipes.name: RIPA Lysis Buffer
recipes.summary: Stringent whole-cell lysis buffer that solubilises cytoplasmic, membrane and nuclear proteins for western blotting.

components.name: Tris base (titrated to pH 7.4 with HCl)
components.form: Tris base (free base), NOT Tris-HCl powder
components.note: 6.06 g/L. Commonly written '50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.4', but the standard preparation weighs out Tris BASE (MW 121.14) and titrates with HCl. If you weigh Tris-HCl powder (MW 157.60) instead, use 7.88 g/L and titrate up with NaOH.

components.name: NP-40 substitute (Igepal CA-630) or Triton X-100
components.note: Genuine Nonidet P-40 is discontinued; Igepal CA-630 is the accepted equivalent. Triton X-100 is a common substitute at the same 1%.

components.name: Sodium deoxycholate
components.note: MW 414.55. Precipitates below pH ~7.5 and in the cold -- see notes.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate (Na2EDTA*2H2O)
components.note: 0.372 g/L. Free-acid EDTA (MW 292.24) will not dissolve at neutral pH without NaOH. Anhydrous disodium EDTA is MW 336.21.

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.storage: 4 C for the base buffer; aliquot and store at -20 C for long-term
recipes.shelfLife: up to 6 months at 4 C without inhibitors; add protease/phosphatase inhibitors immediately before use
recipes.hazards: SDS powder is a respiratory irritant; sodium deoxycholate is harmful if swallowed and irritating. Handle detergent powders in a fume hood.
notes: UNCERTAINTY -- there is no single canonical RIPA formulation and published versions differ in ways that matter. The pH is given variously as 7.4 (most common), 7.5, or 8.0; the non-ionic detergent may be NP-40/Igepal or Triton X-100; and EDTA (1-5 mM) and EGTA are present in some recipes and absent in others. The values above are the most widely cited combination. If you are reproducing a specific published experiment, use that paper's exact formulation rather than this one.

ALWAYS add protease inhibitors fresh to the aliquot you are about to use, never to the stock bottle. A typical addition is 1x complete protease inhibitor cocktail plus 1 mM PMSF. For phospho-proteins also add phosphatase inhibitors (e.g. 1 mM Na3VO4, 10 mM NaF, 10 mM sodium pyrophosphate) and keep everything ice-cold.

Sodium deoxycholate precipitates below about pH 7.5 and when cold. Dissolve it at room temperature at the correct pH, and if the chilled buffer goes cloudy, warm briefly and mix -- do not use it cloudy.
RIPA disrupts most protein-protein interactions. For co-immunoprecipitation use a milder buffer (e.g. 1% NP-40 or 0.5% Triton X-100 without deoxycholate and SDS).
The SDS and deoxycholate in RIPA are incompatible with some downstream assays -- use a BCA (not Bradford) protein assay on RIPA lysates.

recipes.name: Blocking Buffer, 5% Non-Fat Dry Milk in TBS-T
recipes.summary: General-purpose western blot blocking solution; the default first choice for most antibodies.

components.name: Non-fat dry milk powder (skimmed milk powder)
components.note: Use plain non-fat dry milk with no added sugar, vitamins or cocoa. 7.5 g in 150 mL is the Cell Signaling worked example.

recipes.solvent: 1x TBS-T (20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, pH 7.5) -- bring to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.storage: 4 C; use the same day
recipes.shelfLife: 24 h at 4 C -- milk solutions spoil rapidly and support bacterial growth
notes: DO NOT use milk to block blots probed with phospho-specific antibodies. Milk contains casein, itself a phosphoprotein, which produces high background and can sequester the antibody. Use 5% BSA in TBS-T instead.
Milk also contains biotin and endogenous IgG -- avoid it for biotin/streptavidin detection systems and when using anti-bovine or anti-goat secondary antibodies.
Block for 1 h at room temperature with agitation, or overnight at 4 C.
Mix thoroughly and let stand a few minutes to disperse lumps; filter through cheesecloth or a coffee filter if you see undissolved particles, which cause speckled background.
3% milk is sufficient for many blots and gives slightly better sensitivity at the cost of a little more background.

recipes.summary: Casein-free blocking buffer required for phospho-specific antibodies and biotin-based detection.

components.name: Bovine serum albumin (BSA), fraction V, protease-free
components.note: 1.0 g in 20 mL is the Cell Signaling worked example for antibody dilution buffer. Use IgG-free/protease-free grade for best results.

components.name: Sodium azide (NaN3)
components.hazard: ACUTELY TOXIC. Forms explosive metal azides in drains and lead/copper plumbing. Do not pour down the sink. Never combine with HRP-based reagents.
components.note: MW 65.01. OPTIONAL preservative, for primary-antibody dilutions only -- see notes.

recipes.solvent: 1x TBS-T (20 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween-20, pH 7.5) -- bring to final volume
recipes.adjustPHWith: none
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (recommended, and required if the solution will be reused)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 1-2 weeks at 4 C without azide; up to several months with 0.02% azide
recipes.hazards: Sodium azide is acutely toxic and forms explosive heavy-metal azides. Omit it unless you specifically need a long-lived antibody dilution, and dispose of azide-containing waste properly.
notes: This is the required blocking and dilution buffer for phospho-specific antibodies -- milk's casein is a phosphoprotein and will cause high background.

CRITICAL: sodium azide irreversibly inhibits horseradish peroxidase. Never include azide in any solution that contacts an HRP-conjugated secondary antibody or the substrate. It is only acceptable in primary-antibody dilutions, and even then the blot must be washed thoroughly before adding HRP conjugate. Omit the azide entirely if in doubt.

BSA dissolves slowly. Sprinkle it onto the surface of the buffer and let it hydrate without stirring for 10-15 min, then mix gently -- vigorous stirring denatures it and creates foam.
Swap TBS-T for PBS-T if your protocol calls for it, but not for phospho-antibody work.

recipes.name: Sodium phosphate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: General-purpose near-neutral buffer for enzyme assays, protein work and fixatives; buffers well over pH 5.8-8.0 via the H2PO4-/HPO4(2-) pair.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic, anhydrous
components.form: Na2HPO4 (anhydrous, MW 141.96). If using the dihydrate use MW 177.99; heptahydrate 268.07; dodecahydrate 358.14 - the hydrate form changes the mass by up to 2.5x.
components.note: 10.99 g/L at pH 7.4. Cold Spring Harbor gives 10.9 g/L, same value.

components.name: Sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate
components.note: 3.12 g/L at pH 7.4. Cold Spring Harbor gives 3.1 g/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M NaOH / 1 M HCl (fine adjustment only - set the pH primarily by the mono/dibasic ratio)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: 4 C (or room temperature if sterile)
recipes.shelfLife: 1 month at 4 C; discard if cloudy or if microbial growth appears
notes: pKa values of phosphoric acid at 25 C: pKa1 = 2.15, pKa2 = 7.20, pKa3 = 12.35. Only pKa2 is used for the physiological buffer.

Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 7.20 + log10([HPO4 2-]/[H2PO4 -]). Using the IDEAL pKa2 of 7.20 this predicts a ratio of only ~1.6:1 dibasic:monobasic at pH 7.4 - but DO NOT MIX 1.6:1. The amounts in this recipe and the table below use a ratio of 3.4:1 (77.4/22.6), because the apparent pKa2 falls to ~6.87 at the ionic strength of a 0.1 M buffer. The whole table below is internally consistent with an apparent pKa2 of 6.865. Follow the table, not the ideal-pKa calculation, and confirm on a calibrated pH meter.
1 M stock solutions: 138 g/L NaH2PO4*H2O (monobasic) and 142 g/L Na2HPO4 anhydrous (dibasic).
For any other target pH at 0.1 M, scale the two molarities from the table above: molarity_dibasic = 0.1 * (x/100), molarity_monobasic = 0.1 * (y/100).
Phosphate precipitates with Ca2+ and Mg2+ and inhibits many enzymes (kinases, DNA polymerases, carboxylases). Use HEPES or Tris if divalent cations are needed.
pH of phosphate buffer is only weakly temperature dependent (dpKa/dT ~ -0.0028/C), which is a key advantage over Tris.
Freezing a phosphate buffer causes a large pH drop (down to ~pH 3-4) as Na2HPO4*12H2O crystallises out - do not freeze protein samples in concentrated phosphate.

notes: pKa2 of phosphate = 7.20 at 25 C. pH = 7.20 + log10([HPO4 2-]/[H2PO4 -]).

TABLE STATUS [UPDATED]: rows at pH 5.8, 6.0, 6.2, 6.8, 7.0, 7.2, 7.4 and 8.0 have now been confirmed against the Fisher Scientific reference table (the 6.2 and 7.2 rows were re-verified on a later pass). The remaining rows (6.4, 6.6, 7.6, 7.8) could not be re-fetched directly (fishersci.co.uk returns HTTP 403), but every row sums to 100 mL and the implied apparent pKa2 varies smoothly from 6.83 at pH 5.8 to 6.79 at pH 7.6 with no outliers, which is the expected ionic-strength trend and rules out a transcription error. Always confirm the final pH on a meter.
1 M stock solutions: 174.18 g/L K2HPO4 anhydrous; 136.09 g/L KH2PO4. Equivalently 43.545 g K2HPO4 or 34.02 g KH2PO4 per 250 mL.
Potassium phosphate has higher solubility than sodium phosphate at low temperature, so 1 M stocks are less prone to crystallising at 4 C.
Do not use with perchlorate or with SDS-containing solutions at low temperature (KDS precipitates).

recipes.storage: room temperature; refrigerate after opening for long-term use
recipes.shelfLife: up to 1 year sterile at room temperature; discard if turbid
notes: ATTRIBUTION [CORRECTED]: the amounts above are the Cold Spring Harbor Protocols 2014 recipe (pdb.rec081240: 8.0 g NaCl, 0.20 g KCl, 1.14 g Na2HPO4, 0.20 g KH2PO4 = 8.0 mM dibasic / 1.5 mM monobasic) - verified against that source. This line previously called it 'the Cold Spring Harbor / Sambrook formulation', which is wrong: the Sambrook / CSH-2006 PBS (pdb.rec8247) is a DIFFERENT and more widely used formulation with 1.44 g/L Na2HPO4 (10 mM) and 0.24 g/L KH2PO4 (1.8 mM). Both are legitimate 'PBS pH 7.4'; check which one your protocol assumes.

Dulbecco's PBS (DPBS) is yet another formulation, and its phosphate content (1.15 g/L Na2HPO4, 0.20 g/L KH2PO4) is very close to the amounts above - so matching phosphate numbers alone does not tell you which recipe you have. DPBS 'with calcium and magnesium' additionally contains 0.9 mM CaCl2 and 0.5 mM MgCl2 - do not treat them as interchangeable for cell adhesion work.
Combine all salts in ~800 mL water, dissolve, adjust pH to 7.4, then bring to 1 L.

The recipe above is 9.5 mM total phosphate (8.0 mM dibasic + 1.5 mM monobasic). The common Sambrook variant is 11.8 mM total (10 mM Na2HPO4 + 1.8 mM KH2PO4). [CORRECTED: this line previously described the variant as '10 mM total phosphate' - 10 mM is the dibasic component alone, not the total.]
Autoclaving raises the pH slightly; re-check pH after sterilisation for pH-critical assays.

recipes.name: Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), 10x stock, pH 7.4
recipes.summary: Concentrated PBS stock; dilute 1:10 with water for working-strength PBS.

components.name: Sodium phosphate dibasic heptahydrate (Na2HPO4*7H2O)
components.note: MW 268.07 - HEPTAHYDRATE. Anhydrous equivalent would be 13.6 g/L (MW 141.96).

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: up to 1 year at room temperature
notes: Adjust the pH of the 10x stock to 7.4; after 1:10 dilution the pH will be close to 7.4 but should be confirmed for pH-critical work.
The 10x stock may precipitate at 4 C. Warm to room temperature and mix until fully dissolved before diluting.
Note this stock uses the HEPTAHYDRATE of Na2HPO4 while the 1x CSH recipe uses the ANHYDROUS salt - a common source of error when substituting one for the other.

THIS 10x IS NOT EXACTLY 10x THE 1x RECIPE IN THIS FILE. [ADDED] 25.6 g/L Na2HPO4*7H2O = 95.5 mM, so a 1:10 dilution gives ~9.6 mM dibasic phosphate, whereas the 1x recipe here is 8.0 mM. NaCl, KCl and KH2PO4 do match at 1:10. Both recipes are faithful to their respective Cold Spring Harbor sources (pdb.rec102681 and pdb.rec081240), but they are ~19% apart in dibasic phosphate - the difference is a genuine difference in buffer strength, not only the hydrate form. Pick one lineage and stay on it if phosphate concentration matters.

recipes.name: Tris-HCl buffer, 1 M stock (pH 7.4 / 7.6 / 8.0)
recipes.summary: Workhorse buffer for pH 7.0-9.0; the standard stock for TE, TAE, TBS and most molecular biology buffers.

components.name: Tris base (free base)
components.form: Tris base / Trizma base, free base, MW 121.14. Do NOT substitute Trizma HCl (MW 157.60) at the same mass - it is a different compound and will give the wrong molarity and a much lower pH.
components.note: 121.14 g/L for a 1 M solution (Cold Spring Harbor rounds this to 121.1 g/L).

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl (~12 M): ~70 mL/L for pH 7.4, ~60 mL/L for pH 7.6, ~42 mL/L for pH 8.0
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more; discard if it develops a yellow colour
recipes.hazards: Concentrated HCl is corrosive and gives off fumes - add in a fume hood with eye protection.
notes: pKa of Tris = 8.06 at 25 C (suppliers also quote 8.08-8.1). Useful buffering range pH 7.0-9.0.

STRONGLY temperature dependent: pH falls ~0.03 units per 1 C rise (dpKa/dT ~ -0.028/C). A 0.05 M Tris solution reads pH 9.5 at 5 C, 8.9 at 25 C and 8.6 at 37 C. Always pH at the temperature of use, or state the pH-ing temperature.

pH also drops on dilution: diluting a Tris stock 10-fold LOWERS the pH by roughly 0.1 unit. [CORRECTED: this line previously read 'raises the pH', which contradicted its own opening clause. Tris is a cationic buffer and its pH falls on dilution - Promega 'Buffers for Biochemical Reactions' gives approximately -0.1 pH unit per tenfold dilution.]
Alternative preparation without concentrated HCl: mix 1 M Tris base and 1 M Tris-HCl (157.60 g/L) to the target pH; pH = 8.06 + log10([Tris base]/[Tris-HCl]).
Let the solution cool to room temperature before the final pH adjustment - dissolution of Tris base is endothermic and the solution will be cold.
Discard yellow solutions and use a higher grade of Tris base.

Tris is a primary amine: it reacts with aldehydes (do not use with glutaraldehyde/formaldehyde fixation), interferes with Bradford and BCA protein assays at high concentration, and can inhibit some enzymes.

recipes.summary: Zwitterionic Good's buffer for pH 6.8-8.2; the standard choice for cell culture and enzyme work where phosphate or Tris interfere.

components.name: HEPES, free acid
components.form: HEPES free acid, C8H18N2O4S, MW 238.30, CAS 7365-45-9. The sodium (monosodium) salt is a different compound: MW 260.29, CAS 75277-39-3 - a solution of it is strongly ALKALINE (~pH 10-10.5) and you titrate DOWN with HCl instead of up with NaOH. [CORRECTED: this previously said the sodium salt 'starts near pH 7.5'. That is true of HEPES HEMIsodium salt, not the monosodium salt; the monosodium salt is the fully deprotonated base and, as expected from pKa 7.5, gives ~pH 10.5. The 'titrate down with HCl' advice was already correct.]
components.note: 238.30 g/L for a 1 M solution. A 1 M solution of the free acid is around pH 5.0-6.5 before adjustment.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~800 mL, pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (use 5-10 M NaOH for 1 M stocks; KOH if a K+ buffer is wanted)

recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C; discard if yellow or turbid
notes: pKa (pKa2) = 7.55 at 20 C; 7.48 at 25 C. Sigma also quotes 7.85 at 0 C and 7.31 at 37 C.
Temperature coefficient dpKa/dT = -0.014 per C - much flatter than Tris, one of the main reasons to choose HEPES.
Useful buffering range pH 6.8-8.2. Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 7.48 + log10([HEPES-]/[HEPES free acid]) at 25 C.
HEPES exposed to ambient light in cell culture medium can generate hydrogen peroxide and cytotoxic products - store dark and do not leave HEPES-buffered media under illumination.
HEPES chelates some metal ions weakly and can interfere with the Lowry protein assay and with some redox chemistry.
Common working strengths: 10-25 mM in cell culture medium, 20-50 mM in enzyme assay buffers.

For heat-induced epitope retrieval in IHC the standard is a much more dilute buffer: 10 mM sodium citrate, pH 6.0 (2.94 g/L trisodium citrate dihydrate, adjust to pH 6.0 with HCl), often with 0.05% Tween-20.

recipes.summary: Simple, cheap acidic buffer for pH 3.6-5.6; standard for many hydrolase assays, ion exchange, and nucleic acid precipitation buffers.

components.form: CH3COONa*3H2O, MW 136.08. Anhydrous sodium acetate is MW 82.03 - using the wrong form gives a 66% mass error.
components.note: 9.58 g/L at pH 5.0 (5.78 g/L if using the anhydrous salt).

components.name: Acetic acid, glacial
components.form: Glacial acetic acid, ~99.7%, density 1.049 g/mL, ~17.4 M.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid / 1 M NaOH (fine adjustment only)

recipes.storage: room temperature (4 C for long-term)
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
recipes.hazards: Glacial acetic acid: corrosive, flammable, strong odour. Use a fume hood and PPE.
notes: pKa of acetic acid = 4.76 at 25 C (some sources 4.75-4.77). Useful buffering range pH 3.6-5.6.

Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 4.76 + log10([acetate-]/[acetic acid]). Note the apparent pKa falls to ~4.6-4.65 at 0.1 M ionic strength, which is why the empirical table below does not match the ideal HH calculation exactly.

MIXING TABLE (Gomori). Combine x mL of 0.2 M acetic acid (11.55 mL glacial acetic acid per litre) with y mL of 0.2 M sodium acetate (27.22 g/L of the trihydrate), then dilute to 100 mL for a 0.1 M buffer: pH 3.6 = 46.3/3.7; 3.8 = 44.0/6.0; 4.0 = 41.0/9.0; 4.2 = 36.8/13.2; 4.4 = 30.5/19.5; 4.6 = 25.5/24.5; 4.8 = 20.0/30.0; 5.0 = 14.8/35.2; 5.2 = 10.5/39.5; 5.4 = 8.8/41.2; 5.6 = 4.8/45.2.

TABLE CAVEAT: the pH 5.0 row (14.8/35.2) was cross-checked; the remaining rows are the standard Gomori values. Every row sums to 50 mL of 0.2 M stock (= 0.1 M final in 100 mL), and the implied apparent pKa across the table spans ~4.59-4.73 (lowest at the pH 4.4 row, highest at pH 5.4), clustering near 4.62. Always confirm the final pH on a calibrated meter.
3 M sodium acetate pH 5.2 (408.24 g/L trihydrate, adjusted with glacial acetic acid) is the standard stock for ethanol precipitation of nucleic acids.
Acetate is volatile at low pH - do not lyophilise or rotary-evaporate an acetate buffer and expect the pH to be retained.

recipes.name: Carbonate-bicarbonate buffer, pH 9.6 (ELISA coating buffer)
recipes.summary: Alkaline buffer used almost universally for passive adsorption of proteins onto polystyrene ELISA plates.

components.name: Sodium carbonate, anhydrous
components.form: Na2CO3 anhydrous, MW 105.99. Sodium carbonate decahydrate (washing soda) is MW 286.14 - do not substitute.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~950 mL, check pH, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M HCl or 1 M NaOH, added slowly with stirring
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do NOT autoclave - CO2 is driven off and the pH shifts)
recipes.storage: 4 C, tightly capped
recipes.shelfLife: no more than 2 weeks at 4 C; many labs make it fresh daily
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN - see the molarity note below. The gram amounts (1.59 g/L Na2CO3 + 2.93 g/L NaHCO3) and the pH 9.6 target are consistent across many sources, but the MOLARITY LABEL is inconsistent in the literature: by arithmetic this mixture is 0.050 M total carbonate (0.015 M + 0.0349 M), yet numerous websites label the identical recipe '0.1 M'. Sigma capsule C3041 dissolved per instructions is specified as 0.05 M, pH 9.6, which supports the 0.05 M reading. Treat the total carbonate concentration here as 0.05 M and verify against whatever your protocol specifies.
pKa values of carbonic acid at 25 C: pKa1 = 6.35, pKa2 = 10.33. The pH 9.2-10.6 range uses pKa2.

Ideal Henderson-Hasselbalch predicts pH ~9.9-10.0 for this ratio (pH = 10.33 + log10(0.015/0.0349)). The measured pH of the real solution is lower (~9.5-9.6) because of ionic-strength effects and CO2 uptake. ALWAYS set the final pH on a calibrated meter rather than trusting the calculation.
The buffer absorbs atmospheric CO2 and drifts acidic - keep it capped and remake often.
Do not autoclave: heating decomposes bicarbonate and shifts the pH substantially.
Typical use: coat ELISA plates with 1-10 ug/mL antigen in this buffer, 100 uL/well, overnight at 4 C.

Common alternative for the same purpose: 0.1 M carbonate-bicarbonate, pH 9.6 (3.18 g/L Na2CO3 + 5.86 g/L NaHCO3 - i.e. double all amounts above). [CORRECTED: this line previously said doubling the amounts gives '0.2 M'. Doubling a 0.05 M buffer gives 0.1 M. The 0.2 M label almost certainly came from the same mislabelling described in the uncertainty note above.]

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: up to 2 years sterile and sealed; discard if turbid or if particulates are visible
notes: This solution is UNBUFFERED. Freshly prepared saline is around pH 5.0-6.5 and has essentially no buffering capacity - it is not a substitute for PBS in pH-sensitive work.
0.9% is called 'normal' saline for historical reasons; it is not 1 N in the chemical sense (a 1 N NaCl solution would be 58.44 g/L).
Slightly hypertonic relative to plasma (308 vs ~285-295 mOsmol/L) and gives a supraphysiological chloride load; large-volume clinical use can cause hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis.
For any human or animal administration you must use pharmacopoeial (USP/EP) grade material and validated sterile, non-pyrogenic manufacturing. A bench-prepared solution is for laboratory use only.
Related strengths: 0.45% (half-normal, 77 mM), 3% (hypertonic, 513 mM), 5% (hypertonic, 856 mM).

recipes.name: 5 M NaCl
recipes.summary: Universal salt stock for buffers, hybridization, lysis and chromatography.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: 5 M = 292.2 g/L; CSH Protocols specifies 292 g/L.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~0.8 vol, then bring to final volume)
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: indefinite if sterile and not contaminated
notes: 5 M is near the practical ceiling; saturation of NaCl at 25 C is ~6.1 M. Dissolution is endothermic - the solution gets cold and the last grams dissolve slowly. Warm gently and stir.
Always add salt to ~80% of final volume first, dissolve fully, then top up. Adding water to a full measure of salt overshoots the volume.
May precipitate if stored in a cold room; re-warm and mix before use.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - NaCl (5 M) recipe

recipes.name: 1 M MgCl2 (from hexahydrate)
recipes.summary: Divalent cation source for PCR, restriction digests, polymerase and ligase buffers.
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if cloudy
notes: HYDRATE WARNING: using anhydrous MW (95.21) with hexahydrate solid gives a solution ~2.1x too dilute. Almost all lab-stock MgCl2 is the hexahydrate.
MgCl2 * 6H2O is strongly hygroscopic and deliquescent. Weigh quickly, keep the bottle tightly closed, and do not use a jar that has caked or gone wet - its true water content is no longer 6H2O.
MgCl2 solutions absorb water from air; keep aliquots capped. Autoclaved stocks can lose water by evaporation and drift high in concentration - filter sterilization avoids this.
For PCR, buy or aliquot small single-use volumes: repeated pipetting into a shared tube is a classic source of drifting Mg2+ and failed reactions.

sources.title: CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions
Sigma-Aldrich - Magnesium chloride hexahydrate, ACS reagent (FW 203.30)

recipes.name: 1 M MgSO4 (from heptahydrate)
recipes.summary: Mg2+ source for SOB/SOC medium, phage buffers and minimal media.
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
notes: HYDRATE WARNING: anhydrous MgSO4 (120.37) vs heptahydrate (246.47) differ by ~2.05x. Bench bottles labelled simply 'magnesium sulfate' are usually the heptahydrate - check the label.
The heptahydrate effloresces (loses water) in dry air, which slowly raises the effective Mg content of an old bottle. Buy fresh for quantitative work.
SOB medium uses 10 mM MgSO4 + 10 mM MgCl2; SOC adds 20 mM glucose after autoclaving.
Do not autoclave MgSO4 together with phosphate-containing media - magnesium phosphate precipitates. Add as a sterile stock after autoclaving.

sources.title: CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOB / SOC medium recipe

recipes.name: 1 M CaCl2 (from dihydrate)
recipes.summary: Ca2+ source for chemical competent-cell prep, phage buffers, nuclease and protease buffers.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: 1 M = 147.01 g/L using the dihydrate.

recipes.storage: 4 C in aliquots; room temperature acceptable for non-sterile use
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
notes: HYDRATE WARNING: three common forms - anhydrous 110.98, dihydrate 147.01, hexahydrate 219.08. Getting this wrong is the single most frequent CaCl2 error. Read the bottle.

Anhydrous CaCl2 is an aggressive desiccant: dissolution is strongly exothermic and the solid absorbs water fast, so a part-used anhydrous bottle no longer weighs out accurately. The dihydrate is the better routine choice.
Filter sterilization is preferred over autoclaving - CaCl2 forms insoluble carbonate/phosphate precipitates on heating, especially in hard water.
Never add CaCl2 stock to a phosphate-containing buffer at high concentration - calcium phosphate precipitates immediately.

For the classic CaCl2 competent-cell method, cells are made in ice-cold 100 mM (0.1 M) CaCl2. CSH also lists a 2.5 M stock made from 11 g CaCl2 * 6H2O in a final 20 mL, 0.22 um filtered, stored in 1 mL aliquots at 4 C.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - CaCl2 (2.5 M) stock solution recipe
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Preparation and Transformation of Competent E. coli Using Calcium Chloride

recipes.name: 2 M KCl
recipes.summary: Monovalent salt stock for PCR buffers, ionic-strength adjustment and protein purification.
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: indefinite if sterile
notes: KCl is much less soluble than NaCl: saturation is ~4.6 M at 25 C but only ~3.7 M at 4 C. A 2 M stock is safely below that and will not crash out in a cold room.
Standard Taq PCR buffer is 50 mM KCl - dilute this stock 1:40.
Do not substitute KCl for NaCl in buffers used with SDS: potassium dodecyl sulfate is insoluble and will precipitate.

sources.title: CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions
Sigma-Aldrich - Potassium chloride, molecular biology grade (FW 74.55)

recipes.name: 1 M DTT (dithiothreitol)
recipes.summary: Reducing agent stock for keeping protein cysteines reduced and for SDS-PAGE sample buffer.

components.name: DL-Dithiothreitol
components.note: 1 M = 154.25 g/L; CSH specifies 3.09 g in 20 mL.

components.name: Sodium acetate buffer, pH 5.2
components.note: Final 10 mM sodium acetate pH 5.2 - the mildly acidic pH is what makes the stock stable.

recipes.adjustPHWith: supplied by the 0.01 M sodium acetate; do not re-titrate
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter - NEVER autoclave (DTT is heat-labile)
recipes.storage: -20 C in single-use aliquots (e.g. 0.5-1 mL); keep on ice while in use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year at -20 C unopened aliquots; use a thawed aliquot the same day and discard
recipes.hazards: Irritant; strong unpleasant thiol odour - weigh in a fume hood. Harmful if swallowed.
notes: Prepare in 0.01 M sodium acetate pH 5.2, not plain water. At neutral/alkaline pH DTT is rapidly oxidized by dissolved O2; the acidic buffer suppresses this. CSH: 'Under these conditions, dithiothreitol is stable to oxidation by air.'
Do NOT autoclave and do not leave at room temperature. Refreezing and rethawing an aliquot repeatedly is the main reason DTT stocks go dead.
A dead giveaway of oxidized DTT is loss of the sharp sulfur smell. If it does not stink, do not trust it.
Typical working concentrations: 1-10 mM in protein buffers, 50-100 mM in SDS-PAGE sample buffer.
DTT is largely ineffective below pH ~7 as a reductant (the thiolate is the active species) even though the stock is stored at pH 5.2 - it reduces fine once diluted into a neutral working buffer.
TCEP is a more stable, odourless alternative that tolerates low pH and does not need to be aliquoted at -20 C.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Dithiothreitol Solution (DTT, 1 M)
