recipes.name: 0.5 M EDTA, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Divalent-cation chelator; component of TE, TAE and TBE, and a general nuclease inhibitor.

components.name: EDTA disodium salt dihydrate
components.form: Na2EDTA * 2H2O (FW 372.24) - NOT free-acid EDTA (292.24) and NOT the tetrasodium salt
components.note: 0.5 M = 186.12 g/L; CSH specifies 186.1 g/L.

components.name: NaOH pellets
components.note: ~20 g of NaOH pellets per litre are needed to reach pH 8.0. Not a stoichiometric component - add until the solid dissolves and pH reads 8.0.
components.hazard: Corrosive - wear eye protection.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~0.8 vol, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH (pellets or 10 N)
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: indefinite if sterile
notes: The disodium salt WILL NOT DISSOLVE until the pH is raised to ~8.0 with NaOH. If your EDTA is sitting as a white slurry, you have not added enough NaOH - keep adding and stirring vigorously, do not add more water.
HYDRATE/FORM WARNING: free-acid EDTA (292.24) is a different reagent and needs far more NaOH. The tetrasodium salt dissolves readily but arrives at pH ~11 and must be titrated DOWN with HCl.
Use a magnetic stirrer and expect 20-40 min for a litre. Gentle warming to 40-50 C helps.
Working concentrations: 1 mM in TE, 2 mM in TAE, 10 mM in many lysis buffers.
EDTA at high concentration will strip Mg2+ from PCR and restriction reactions - keep carryover low when eluting DNA into TE before an enzymatic step.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - EDTA (0.5 M, pH 8.0) recipe
CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions

recipes.name: 20% (w/v) SDS
recipes.summary: Anionic detergent stock for lysis buffers, SDS-PAGE and hybridization washes.

components.name: Sodium dodecyl sulfate, electrophoresis grade
components.hazard: Fine powder is a serious respiratory and eye irritant - weigh in a fume hood or balance enclosure and wear a mask.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in ~0.9 vol at 68 C, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: a few drops of concentrated HCl, only if necessary
recipes.sterilization: none - sterilization is not necessary. DO NOT AUTOCLAVE.
recipes.storage: room temperature; do NOT refrigerate
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
recipes.hazards: Dry SDS powder is harmful if inhaled and irritating to eyes and airways. Weigh it in a hood; do not brush spilled powder off the bench dry - wipe with a damp cloth.
notes: CSH procedure: dissolve 200 g electrophoresis-grade SDS in 900 mL H2O, heat to 68 C and stir to assist dissolution, adjust to pH 7.2 with a few drops of conc. HCl if needed, adjust volume to 1 L. Sterilization is not necessary; do not autoclave.
SDS crystallizes out of solution below ~15-20 C. Store at room temperature and re-dissolve by warming to 37-68 C if it goes cloudy or crystalline. NEVER store SDS stocks in a cold room or fridge.

SDS precipitates with potassium ions as insoluble potassium dodecyl sulfate. Do not combine SDS with KCl or potassium acetate buffers - this is exploited deliberately in alkaline lysis miniprep Solution III.

Many labs keep 10% (w/v) SDS instead; 10% is the same recipe at 100 g/L and both are common. Halve the mass for 10%. [CORRECTED: this line previously read '20% is the same recipe at 100 g/L' - 20% w/v is 200 g/L, 10% w/v is 100 g/L.]
The apparent 'MW' of SDS matters only for molarity calculations; recipes are always by mass percent.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SDS (20% w/v) recipe
Sigma-Aldrich - Sodium dodecyl sulfate, BioReagent L3771 (FW 288.38)

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-Cl, pH 7.5
recipes.summary: Workhorse buffer stock for DNA/protein work at near-neutral pH (TE, restriction buffers, lysis buffers).

components.name: Tris base (tromethamine, free base)
components.form: Tris base / Trizma base, C4H11NO3 (FW 121.14) - NOT Tris-HCl salt (FW 157.60)
components.note: 1 M = 121.14 g/L; CSH specifies 121.1 g/L. Titrate with HCl to the target pH.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: ~60-65 mL per litre to reach pH 7.5 (CSH tabulates ~70 mL for pH 7.4 and ~42 mL for pH 8.0). Titrate to the meter reading - do not add a fixed volume blind.
components.hazard: Corrosive, fuming - add in a fume hood, wear eye protection.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 0.8 vol, titrate, cool, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; discard if it yellows or grows anything
notes: TEMPERATURE: Tris pH is strongly temperature-dependent, about -0.028 pH units per degree C. A buffer at pH 7.5 at 25 C reads ~pH 8.1 at 4 C and ~pH 7.15 at 37 C. Always calibrate the meter and titrate AT THE TEMPERATURE THE BUFFER WILL BE USED, or at least record the titration temperature.
Let the solution cool to room temperature before the final pH reading - dissolving Tris base is endothermic and a warm solution reads falsely.

FORM WARNING: Tris base (121.14) is titrated down with HCl; Tris-HCl salt (157.60) is titrated UP with NaOH and starts near pH 4.7. Using the wrong MW is a classic error. Starting from Tris base is standard.
Use a Tris-compatible (non-clogging, e.g. glass-body or Ag/AgCl double-junction) pH electrode - some electrodes read Tris poorly.
Tris is a primary amine: it reacts with aldehydes and with NHS-esters/isothiocyanates. Do not use Tris buffers for amine-reactive labelling or for formaldehyde/glutaraldehyde fixation.
Tris buffering range is pH 7.0-9.0 (pKa 8.06 at 25 C). At pH 7.5 it is toward the low end but still widely used and standard.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (1 M) recipe
Sigma-Aldrich - Trizma base technical data (FW 121.14, pKa and temperature coefficient)

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-Cl, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Standard buffer stock for TE, plasmid preps, PCR and most cloning buffers.

components.name: Tris base (tromethamine, free base)
components.form: Tris base / Trizma base, C4H11NO3 (FW 121.14) - NOT Tris-HCl salt (FW 157.60)
components.note: 1 M = 121.14 g/L; CSH specifies 121.1 g/L.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: ~42 mL per litre to reach pH 8.0 (CSH tabulated value). Titrate to the meter, do not add blind.
components.hazard: Corrosive, fuming - add in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 0.8 vol, titrate, cool, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
notes: pH 8.0 sits essentially on the Tris pKa (8.06 at 25 C) - this is where Tris buffers best and where the stock is most forgiving.
Same temperature caveat as all Tris buffers: ~-0.028 pH units per degree C. Titrate at the use temperature where it matters (e.g. cold-room chromatography).
TE buffer = 10 mM Tris-Cl pH 8.0 + 1 mM EDTA pH 8.0 (dilute this stock 1:100).
Autoclaved Tris can turn faintly yellow; a pale tint is cosmetic, a strong colour means degradation - remake it.
Do not use Tris buffers when you need a nitrogen-free buffer, or with amine-reactive chemistry.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (1 M) recipe
CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions

recipes.name: 1 M Tris-Cl, pH 9.0
recipes.summary: Alkaline Tris stock for alkaline phosphatase reactions, some ligations and high-pH elutions.

components.name: Tris base (tromethamine, free base)
components.note: 1 M = 121.14 g/L. Mass is certain; the HCl volume is not tabulated in the standard reference.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: UNVERIFIED VOLUME - roughly 8-11 mL per litre. Add HCl dropwise to the meter reading; do not use a fixed volume. [REVISED from 10-13 mL: Henderson-Hasselbalch with pKa 8.06, anchored to the tabulated 42 mL/L at pH 8.0, gives ~8 mL/L at pH 9.0. The same model returns 65 mL at pH 7.4 vs the tabulated 70 mL, so it runs slightly low - hence a range topping out near 11 mL.]
components.hazard: Corrosive, fuming.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve in 0.8 vol, titrate, cool, then bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: concentrated HCl
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 6-12 months
notes: MARKED UNCERTAIN: the Tris base mass (121.14 g/L) and the target pH are solid, but the standard Sambrook/CSH Tris table only tabulates HCl volumes for pH 7.4, 7.6 and 8.0. The HCl volume given here for pH 9.0 is an extrapolation, not a published figure. Titrate to the meter and ignore the stated volume.

pH 9.0 is ~1 unit above the Tris pKa (8.06), so buffering capacity here is weak - about a third of what you get at the pKa. [CORRECTED from 'about a tenth': buffer capacity one pH unit off the pKa falls to ~33% of maximum, not 10%.] Expect the pH to drift more easily. If you need strong buffering above pH 9, consider CHES (pKa 9.3) or glycine-NaOH.
Temperature dependence bites hardest here: -0.028 pH/degree C means a pH 9.0 buffer titrated at 25 C is ~pH 9.6 in a 4 C cold room. Titrate at the working temperature.
Because so little HCl is required, it is easy to overshoot. Dilute your HCl to 1 N for the final approach.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Tris-Cl (1 M) recipe (tabulates pH 7.4, 7.6, 8.0 only)
Sigma-Aldrich - Trizma base: buffering range pH 7.0-9.0, pKa 8.06 at 25 C, dpKa/dT ~ -0.028/C

recipes.name: 3 M Potassium acetate, pH 5.5
recipes.summary: Acidic salt stock for nucleic-acid precipitation and neutralization steps.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Roughly 20-30 mL per litre are needed - not a dropwise addition. Titrate to the meter. [CORRECTED: previously stated 'a few mL per litre' (0.5% v/v), which is far too low. Bringing 3 M acetate to pH 5.5 needs ~0.35-0.55 mol acetic acid per litre; glacial acetic acid is 17.5 M, giving ~20-30 mL/L.]
components.hazard: Corrosive, pungent - use a fume hood.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid (to lower pH) / KOH (to raise)
recipes.storage: room temperature; 4 C for use in plasmid preps
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
notes: Note the distinction from the Sambrook alkaline-lysis 'Solution III', which is a different formulation (3 M with respect to potassium, 5 M with respect to acetate) - see the separate recipe in this file.
Potassium acetate precipitates SDS as insoluble potassium dodecyl sulfate; this is exactly what neutralizes and clears an alkaline lysate.
For ethanol precipitation of nucleic acids, sodium acetate pH 5.2 is the more common choice; potassium acetate is preferred where K+ is wanted or Na+ must be avoided.
Potassium acetate is hygroscopic - keep the bottle sealed and weigh promptly.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Alkaline Lysis Solution III
CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions

recipes.name: Alkaline Lysis Solution III (3 M potassium, 5 M acetate)
recipes.summary: Neutralization/precipitation buffer for plasmid miniprep by alkaline lysis (Sambrook).

components.name: Potassium acetate
components.note: 60 mL of 5 M potassium acetate per 100 mL final. The 5 M stock itself is 490.7 g/L (98.14 g/mol).

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: 11.5 mL per 100 mL final volume.
components.hazard: Corrosive, pungent - dispense in a fume hood.

recipes.solvent: deionized water to final volume (28.5 mL water per 100 mL)
recipes.adjustPHWith: no titration needed - the acetic acid fixes the pH
recipes.storage: room temperature; chill to 4 C before use
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year
recipes.hazards: Contains glacial acetic acid - corrosive, prepare in a fume hood.
notes: Sambrook composition, verbatim intent: 60 mL of 5 M potassium acetate + 11.5 mL glacial acetic acid + 28.5 mL H2O per 100 mL. The resulting solution is 3 M with respect to potassium and 5 M with respect to acetate.
This is NOT the same as '3 M potassium acetate pH 5.5' - the acetate is in large excess here. Labs conflate the two constantly; use the one your protocol names.
Use ice-cold. The cold plus the K+ is what drives quantitative precipitation of SDS, protein and chromosomal DNA while leaving plasmid in solution.
Scale note for the calculator: the 'fromStock' entry gives the 5 M potassium acetate volume; the acetic acid is a straight 11.5% v/v; water makes up the rest.

MARKED UNCERTAIN (pH only): the composition is exact and verified, but the stated pH is not. This solution is never pH-adjusted - you mix it and use it - and Sambrook does not print a pH for it. The mixture is 0.3 mol acetate from KOAc plus ~0.20 mol acetic acid (11.5 mL x 17.5 M) per 100 mL, which by Henderson-Hasselbalch calculates to pH ~4.7-4.9. [CORRECTED: finalPH was listed as 5.2, which is not derivable from this composition. It was likely carried over from 3 M sodium acetate pH 5.2, or confused with Qiagen buffer P3 (3 M KOAc, pH 5.5) - a genuinely different reagent.] Do not titrate to hit any printed number; mix the three volumes as given.

recipes.name: 1 M Imidazole, pH 8.0
recipes.summary: Competitive eluent stock for Ni-NTA / IMAC purification of His-tagged proteins.

components.name: Imidazole
components.note: 1 M = 68.08 g/L. Use high-purity/low-A280 grade for IMAC.

components.name: Concentrated HCl (~12 N)
components.note: Added to bring the free base from pH ~10 down to 8.0 - about 7 mL of conc. HCl per litre. Titrate to the meter. [CORRECTED from 0.5% v/v: with imidazole pKa 6.95, reaching pH 8.0 protonates ~8% of a 1 M solution = 0.082 mol, i.e. ~6.8 mL of 12 N HCl per litre.]
components.hazard: Corrosive, fuming.

recipes.solvent: deionized water, or directly into the running buffer (e.g. 50 mM sodium phosphate + 300 mM NaCl) so the eluent matches the column buffer
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl (to lower) / NaOH (to raise)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (also degasses and removes particulates before loading on a column)
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1-3 months; discard when it yellows
recipes.hazards: Imidazole is a reproductive toxin (suspected) - avoid inhalation of dust and skin contact; wear gloves.
notes: Imidazole free base dissolves to about pH 10 - it MUST be titrated down. A common failure is preparing 'pH 8.0 imidazole' without checking, then stripping nickel off the column with an alkaline eluent.
Buy low-absorbance / high-purity imidazole. Cheap grades absorb strongly at 280 nm and wreck protein quantification and A280 chromatograms.
Prepare the stock IN the purification buffer (phosphate or Tris + NaCl) rather than water, so that elution does not dilute the salt and drop your protein out of solution.
Imidazole pKa is ~6.95, so at pH 8.0 it has little buffering capacity of its own - the phosphate or Tris in the running buffer is doing the buffering. That is expected and fine.
Typical usage: 10-20 mM in binding/wash buffer, 250-500 mM to elute.
Imidazole chelates and will slowly strip Ni2+ from a column at high concentration; do not store columns in imidazole.

recipes.name: 80% (v/v) Glycerol
recipes.summary: Sterile cryoprotectant stock for making bacterial glycerol stocks and for enzyme storage buffers.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: MW 92.09 (C3H8O3), density 1.261 g/mL at 20 C. 80% v/v = 800 mL glycerol per litre = ~1009 g/L.

recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: indefinite if sterile
notes: Neat glycerol is extremely viscous. Measure it by weight, not by pipette, for accuracy: 800 mL of glycerol = 1009 g at 20 C. If you must pipette, use a cut tip and let it drain fully.

Prepare by dispensing the glycerol FIRST, then adding water and mixing - glycerol will otherwise sit as a layer at the bottom and never mix. Mix thoroughly; incomplete mixing is the usual reason a 'glycerol stock' fails to protect cells.
Glycerol and water mix with volume contraction, so 800 mL glycerol + 200 mL water gives slightly less than 1 L. For strict v/v, add glycerol then top up to the mark.
For bacterial glycerol stocks: mix culture with this stock to a final 15-25% glycerol (e.g. 250 uL of 80% + 750 uL culture gives 20%). Freeze at -80 C.
Many labs keep 50% (v/v) glycerol instead and mix 1:1 with culture for a final 25%. Both are standard - 80% is handier when you want the final glycerol low and the culture volume high.
Glycerol is hygroscopic; an old open bottle of 'anhydrous' glycerol may already be dilute. Keep capped.

sources.title: Addgene - Protocol: How to Create a Bacterial Glycerol Stock
Sigma-Aldrich - Glycerol, molecular biology grade (FW 92.09, density 1.25-1.26 g/mL)

recipes.name: 1 M Glucose
recipes.summary: Sterile carbon-source stock added to media after autoclaving (e.g. SOC, M9).
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter - do NOT autoclave with media
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 3-6 months; discard at the first sign of turbidity or fungal growth
notes: HYDRATE WARNING: anhydrous D-glucose is 180.16 but glucose MONOHYDRATE is 198.17 - a 10% error if confused. Most molecular-biology grade dextrose is anhydrous; check the label.

FILTER STERILIZE. Autoclaving glucose caramelizes it and, in the presence of amino acids/peptone, drives Maillard reactions that produce growth-inhibitory brown products. If you must autoclave, do it separately from the nitrogen source at reduced time.
Standard practice (CSH): autoclave SOB medium, cool to 60 C or below, then add 20 mL of sterile 1 M glucose per litre to give SOC (20 mM glucose).

1 M glucose (180.16 g/L) is well below saturation - D-glucose dissolves to roughly 900 g/L (~5 M) at 25 C, so 1 M is only about 20% of saturation. It still dissolves slowly; stir and warm gently to 37 C if needed. [CORRECTED: previously claimed 1 M was 'close to saturation', which is wrong - 50% (w/v) glucose stocks are made routinely.]
Glucose solutions are excellent microbial growth media themselves - anything that gets into the bottle will bloom. Aliquot and keep the working bottle small.
For reference, 20% (w/v) glucose = 200 g/L = 1.11 M; many protocols specify the 20% form.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOC medium recipe (add 20 mL sterile 1 M glucose after autoclaving, at <=60 C)
Sigma-Aldrich - D-(+)-Glucose, anhydrous, molecular biology grade (FW 180.16)

recipes.name: 100 mM PMSF (in isopropanol)
recipes.summary: Irreversible serine-protease inhibitor stock added to lysis buffers immediately before use.

components.name: Phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride
components.note: 100 mM = 17.42 g/L = 17.4 mg per mL of isopropanol (CSH figure).
components.hazard: ACUTELY TOXIC - a potent cholinesterase inhibitor. Corrosive and fatal if inhaled or absorbed.

recipes.storage: -20 C in tightly sealed aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: up to 1 year at -20 C in isopropanol; hours at most once diluted into aqueous buffer
recipes.hazards: PMSF is highly toxic - it inhibits acetylcholinesterase. The dry powder is a severe respiratory and contact hazard. ALWAYS weigh in a fume hood, wear nitrile gloves and eye protection. Do not weigh on an open balance.
notes: MUST be dissolved in an anhydrous organic solvent (isopropanol, ethanol or 1-propanol). PMSF hydrolyzes rapidly in water and the stock will be dead within hours.

STABILITY: the half-life of a 20 uM aqueous PMSF solution is only ~35 min at pH 8.0, and inactivation is faster at higher pH and higher temperature. [CORRECTED: previously read '20 mM' - the CSH figure is 20 micromolar. PMSF is only sparingly soluble in water and a 20 mM aqueous solution is not achievable.] Therefore: add PMSF to your buffer IMMEDIATELY BEFORE USE, never store PMSF-containing aqueous buffers, and re-dose during long preps.
Typical working concentration 0.1-1 mM (dilute this stock 1:1000 to 1:100). Adding isopropanol above ~1% v/v can denature some proteins.
PMSF may precipitate when the cold isopropanol stock hits cold aqueous buffer - add it dropwise with vigorous stirring.
PMSF does not inhibit cysteine, aspartic or metalloproteases. Use a cocktail (or EDTA + leupeptin + pepstatin) for broad coverage.
DISPOSAL: PMSF is destroyed by alkali. Aqueous solutions made alkaline to pH >8.6 can be discarded safely after several hours at room temperature (CSH).
Pefabloc SC (AEBSF) is a less toxic, aqueous-stable substitute with comparable potency and is worth considering.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - PMSF recipe (17.4 mg/mL isopropanol = 100 mM; aqueous half-life ~35 min at pH 8.0)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF)
Sigma-Aldrich / Roche - PMSF product page and safety data (FW 174.19, CAS 329-98-6)

recipes.name: beta-Mercaptoethanol (BME) - neat reagent and 1 M working stock
recipes.summary: Reducing agent supplied as a neat 14.3 M liquid; handling rules and dilution to a 1 M aqueous working stock.

components.name: 2-Mercaptoethanol (neat liquid)
components.note: Neat BME is 14.3 M (FW 78.13, density 1.114 g/mL at 25 C, >=99%). 1 M working stock = 69.9 mL neat BME per litre of water.
components.hazard: Toxic if inhaled or swallowed; corrosive; overwhelming stench. FUME HOOD ONLY.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume) - prepare and cap inside a fume hood
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter if sterility is needed - do NOT autoclave
recipes.storage: Neat reagent: 4 C, tightly sealed, in a ventilated cabinet, secondary containment. 1 M aqueous working stock: 4 C, use within weeks.
recipes.shelfLife: Neat: 1-2 years (note the vendor expiry - it oxidizes). Aqueous 1 M: 2-4 weeks at 4 C; make fresh for critical work.
recipes.hazards: HIGH HAZARD. 2-Mercaptoethanol is acutely toxic by inhalation, ingestion and skin absorption, and is corrosive. Its odour is extreme and persistent - a single open handling on the open bench will clear a floor and can trigger a building complaint. ALWAYS open the bottle, pipette and cap inside a working fume hood. Wear nitrile gloves (double-glove; BME permeates thin gloves) and eye protection.
notes: KEY NUMBERS: FW 78.13, density ~1.114 g/mL, purity >=99% -> neat concentration 14.3 M. Use 14.3 M for all C1V1 dilutions from the neat bottle.
Common working concentrations: 1-10 mM in protein buffers (dilute neat 1:14300 to 1:1430); 5% (v/v) in Laemmli SDS-PAGE sample buffer, which is ~0.7 M; 50-100 mM for full disulfide reduction.

DECONTAMINATION: keep a beaker of dilute bleach in the hood. Rinse pipette tips and used tubes into it before disposal - this oxidizes the thiol and kills the smell. Never bin BME-wetted tips in an open bench waste container.
Dispose of BME waste as hazardous chemical waste, in a sealed container, in the hood. Do not pour down the drain.
BME is volatile and oxidizes in air; buffers containing it lose reducing power over hours. Add just before use.

DTT or TCEP are preferable where the smell or toxicity is a problem; TCEP is odourless, non-volatile and stable in aqueous buffer. BME remains standard in SDS-PAGE sample buffer and some enzyme buffers.

A 1 M aqueous working stock is a practical convenience so that routine dilutions can be done at the bench rather than repeatedly opening the neat bottle - but the neat bottle is still the reference standard.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich - 2-Mercaptoethanol M3148 (FW 78.13, density 1.114 g/mL, 14.3 M)
Sigma-Aldrich - 2-Mercaptoethanol substance page and safety information
PubChem CID 1567 - 2-Mercaptoethanol properties and hazards

recipes.name: 3 M Sodium acetate, pH 5.2
recipes.summary: Standard salt for ethanol/isopropanol precipitation of DNA and RNA.

components.name: Sodium acetate trihydrate
components.note: 3 M = 408.24 g/L using the trihydrate; 246.09 g/L if using anhydrous.

components.name: Glacial acetic acid
components.note: Roughly 40-60 mL per litre are needed - this is NOT a dropwise addition. Titrate to the meter. [CORRECTED: previously stated '10-15 mL per litre' (1.2% v/v), which is 4-5x too low. Neutralizing 3 M acetate from pH ~8.9 to pH 5.2 needs ~0.7-1.1 mol of acetic acid per litre; glacial acetic acid is 17.5 M, giving ~40-60 mL/L.]
components.hazard: Corrosive, pungent - use a fume hood.

recipes.adjustPHWith: glacial acetic acid
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: indefinite if sterile
notes: Included because it is the companion reagent every lab keeps next to potassium acetate and the hydrate confusion is identical: trihydrate 136.08 vs anhydrous 82.03 - a 1.66x error.
Standard use: add 1/10 volume of 3 M sodium acetate pH 5.2, then 2-2.5 volumes of ethanol, to precipitate nucleic acids.
pH 5.2 is the conventional value for DNA; pH 5.5 is sometimes specified for RNA work.
For precipitations where sodium must be avoided, substitute ammonium acetate (but note that ammonium ions inhibit T4 polynucleotide kinase) or potassium acetate.

sources.title: CSHL Press, Molecular Cloning Lab Reference - Section 1A Stock Solutions
Sigma-Aldrich - Sodium acetate trihydrate, molecular biology grade (FW 136.08)

recipes.name: 0.1 M CaCl2 Competent Cell Buffer
recipes.summary: Ice-cold calcium chloride buffer for preparing chemically competent E. coli by the classical CaCl2 heat-shock method.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: If using ANHYDROUS CaCl2 use MW 110.98 (11.10 g/L); if using the hexahydrate CaCl2*6H2O use MW 219.08 (21.91 g/L). The dihydrate is the most commonly stocked form.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: OMIT for the two ice-cold wash steps. Include ONLY in the final resuspension buffer if you intend to freeze aliquots at -80 C. Freshly-made (unfrozen) competent cells need no glycerol.

recipes.storage: sterile buffer at 4 C for up to 6 months; must be chilled to 0-4 C before use
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months at 4 C
recipes.hazards: CaCl2 dissolution is exothermic; the solid is an eye and respiratory irritant. Low overall risk.
notes: Typical yield: 10^5 - 10^7 cfu/ug pUC19 - lower than the Inoue method but far simpler and adequate for routine subcloning and re-transformation of intact plasmid.
Keep everything ice-cold from the first spin onward. Grow cells to OD600 0.35-0.5, chill on ice 20-30 min, pellet at ~3000 x g for 10 min at 4 C.
Wash the pellet in 0.5 culture-volume of ice-cold buffer, re-pellet, then resuspend in 1/25 to 1/50 culture volume.
Competence increases markedly if cells are left in CaCl2 on ice for 12-24 h before use or freezing.
A widely used variant adds 70 mM CaCl2 + 20 mM MgCl2 (from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30), or pre-washes with 0.1 M MgCl2 before the CaCl2 step; both give modestly higher efficiency.

sources.title: Sambrook J. & Russell D.W., Molecular Cloning: A Laboratory Manual, 3rd ed., Chapter 1, Protocol 23 - 'Preparation and Transformation of Competent E. coli Using Calcium Chloride'
Cohen S.N., Chang A.C.Y., Hsu L. (1972) PNAS 69:2110 - Nonchromosomal antibiotic resistance in bacteria: genetic transformation of E. coli by R-factor DNA

recipes.name: TSS (Transformation and Storage Solution)
recipes.summary: Single-solution buffer that makes E. coli competent and doubles as the cryostorage medium - cells are made competent and frozen in the same tube.

components.name: PEG 8000 (polyethylene glycol, MW 8000)
components.note: PEG 3350 works nearly as well and is a common substitute. Dissolves slowly - stir with gentle warming.

components.name: DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide)
components.hazard: DMSO is a potent skin-penetration enhancer - it will carry dissolved solutes through gloves and skin. Use fresh, anhydrous, cell-culture-grade DMSO and nitrile gloves.

components.name: Magnesium chloride hexahydrate
components.note: Anhydrous MgCl2 is MW 95.21 (4.76 g/L) - the hexahydrate at 10.17 g/L is what is normally stocked. The original Chung 1989 paper allows 50 mM MgCl2 OR 50 mM MgSO4 (MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.47).

recipes.solvent: LB (Lysogeny Broth) medium - bring to final volume; LB makes up the remaining ~85% of the solution
recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (do NOT autoclave after adding DMSO)
recipes.storage: 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: less than 2 weeks at 4 C - make fresh for best efficiency
recipes.hazards: DMSO penetrates skin and carries solutes with it. Handle with nitrile gloves.
notes: Grow cells in LB at 37 C to OD600 ~0.5 (about 2-2.5 h), chill on ice 20 min, pellet at low speed (~1500-3000 x g, 5-10 min, 4 C), and resuspend in 1/10 culture volume of ice-cold TSS.
Typical efficiency 10^6 - 10^7 cfu/ug pUC19 - comparable to CaCl2, with the advantage that cells can be snap-frozen in TSS at -80 C directly and retain competence.

Transformation: mix DNA with 100 uL competent cells, hold on ice 30 min, then either heat-shock 42 C for 45-60 s or (per Chung) simply add SOC and outgrow at 37 C - TSS transformations work without heat shock.
Autoclave the LB/PEG/MgCl2 portion first, cool fully, then add DMSO and filter-sterilise. Adding DMSO before autoclaving destroys it.

sources.title: Chung C.T., Niemela S.L., Miller R.H. (1989) PNAS 86:2172-2175 - One-step preparation of competent E. coli: transformation and storage of bacterial cells in the same solution
protocols.io - TSS Competent Cells and Transformation

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: Rice University Bennett Lab wiki URL no longer resolves; replaced with a live protocols.io TSS protocol. Composition re-verified against Chung et al. 1989 (LB + 10% w/v PEG, 5% v/v DMSO, 50 mM Mg2+, pH 6.5) - unchanged.

recipes.name: Inoue Transformation Buffer (TB)
recipes.summary: High-manganese PIPES buffer used to make 'ultracompetent' E. coli (10^8-10^9 cfu/ug) for library construction and difficult ligations.

components.name: Manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate
components.note: = 10.88 g/L. CRITICAL: anhydrous MnCl2 is MW 125.84 - using it with the 10.88 g figure gives a 57% overdose. Always confirm the hydrate on the bottle.

components.name: Calcium chloride dihydrate
components.note: = 2.20 g/L. Anhydrous CaCl2 is MW 110.98.

components.name: Potassium chloride
components.note: = 18.64 g/L (protocols usually print 18.65 g).

components.name: PIPES
components.form: PIPES free acid, MW 302.37 - prepare a 0.5 M stock (151.19 g/L), adjust to pH 6.7 with 5 M KOH, then 0.22 um filter
components.note: Add 20 mL of the 0.5 M pH-6.7 stock per litre of buffer. PIPES free acid will not dissolve until the pH is raised toward neutrality - add KOH gradually while stirring.

recipes.adjustPHWith: KOH (pH the 0.5 M PIPES stock to 6.7 BEFORE adding the salts)
recipes.sterilization: 0.45 um filter (Nalgene, pre-rinsed) - do NOT autoclave
recipes.storage: aliquots at -20 C; thaw and chill to 0 C immediately before use
recipes.shelfLife: at least 1 year at -20 C
recipes.hazards: Manganese(II) chloride is harmful if swallowed and is a suspected reproductive/neurological toxin (CNS effects on chronic exposure). Weigh with gloves; avoid dust.
notes: DO NOT AUTOCLAVE - the buffer turns brown/orange and precipitates, and competence is lost. Filter-sterilise only.
Order of addition matters: pH the PIPES stock to 6.7 first, then add it to the water, then dissolve the salts. Adding KOH to the complete mix precipitates Mn(OH)2.
Grow cells in SOB at 18-22 C (NOT 37 C) to OD600 0.55 - the low growth temperature is the single most important variable for reaching 10^8-10^9 cfu/ug.

Chill on ice 10 min, pellet at 2500 x g for 10 min at 4 C, resuspend in 80 mL ice-cold Inoue TB per 250 mL culture, re-pellet, resuspend in 20 mL TB, then add DMSO to 7% (v/v) final and snap-freeze in liquid nitrogen.
Discard the buffer if it develops any colour or precipitate.

sources.title: Inoue H., Nojima H., Okayama H. (1990) Gene 96:23-28 - High efficiency transformation of Escherichia coli with plasmids
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning 3rd ed., Chapter 1 Protocol 24 / CSH Protocols - The Inoue Method for Preparation and Transformation of Competent E. coli: Ultra-Competent Cells
Bio-protocol Bio-101: The Inoue Method for Preparation and Transformation of Competent E. coli

recipes.name: SOB Medium (Super Optimal Broth)
recipes.summary: Magnesium-rich rich medium used to grow E. coli for competent-cell preparation; base medium for SOC.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: = 0.584 g/L. Many published SOB recipes round this to 0.5 g/L (8.6 mM); either is acceptable.

components.name: Potassium chloride
components.note: = 0.186 g/L. Usually added as 10 mL of a 250 mM KCl stock per litre.

components.name: Magnesium chloride
components.form: 1 M MgCl2 stock from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30 (203.30 g/L); anhydrous MgCl2 is MW 95.21
components.note: ADD AFTER AUTOCLAVING from a separately sterilised stock - autoclaving Mg with the medium causes precipitation.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.form: 1 M MgSO4 stock from MgSO4*7H2O, MW 246.47 (246.47 g/L); anhydrous MgSO4 is MW 120.37
components.note: ADD AFTER AUTOCLAVING. Many labs substitute a single 2 M Mg2+ stock (1 M MgCl2 + 1 M MgSO4) and add 10 mL/L.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min (base only); add Mg stocks after cooling
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months
notes: SOB differs from LB chiefly in its high Mg2+ and low Na+ - the Mg2+ is what supports the very high competence achieved by the Hanahan and Inoue methods.
For competent-cell prep, grow in SOB at 18-22 C with vigorous aeration to OD600 0.5-0.6.
Add 20 mM glucose after autoclaving to convert SOB into SOC.
For plates add 15 g/L agar.

sources.title: Hanahan D. (1983) J Mol Biol 166:557-580 - Studies on transformation of Escherichia coli with plasmids
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - SOB Medium recipe (pdb.rec102723)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: Cold Spring Harbor SOB source URL pdb.rec101196 returned HTTP 404; replaced with the real CSH SOB Medium recipe, pdb.rec102723.

recipes.name: SOC Medium (Super Optimal broth with Catabolite repression)
recipes.summary: Recovery/outgrowth medium added immediately after heat-shock transformation to maximise transformation efficiency.

components.name: Magnesium chloride
components.note: Add after autoclaving.

components.name: Magnesium sulfate
components.note: Add after autoclaving.

components.name: D-Glucose (dextrose)
components.note: = 3.60 g/L. Glucose monohydrate is MW 198.17 (3.96 g/L). ADD AFTER AUTOCLAVING as a filter-sterilised 1 M (or 20% w/v) stock - autoclaving glucose with the amino-acid-rich base causes Maillard browning and loss of efficiency.

recipes.adjustPHWith: NaOH / HCl
recipes.sterilization: autoclave the base 121 C, 20 min; add Mg and glucose stocks after cooling, then 0.22 um filter the finished medium
recipes.storage: room temperature (sterile, sealed) or 4 C; convenient as 1 mL sterile aliquots
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sealed and sterile; discard any cloudy aliquot
notes: Warm to room temperature or 37 C before adding to heat-shocked cells - cold SOC costs efficiency.
Add 250-950 uL SOC per transformation and shake at 37 C, 225 rpm for 1 h before plating.
The 1-h outgrowth is essential for kanamycin, chloramphenicol, tetracycline and gentamicin resistance (these need time to express). For ampicillin/carbenicillin it can be shortened to ~20-30 min.
SOC is simply SOB plus 20 mM glucose - if you keep SOB on hand, add sterile glucose on the day of use.

sources.title: Hanahan D. (1983) J Mol Biol 166:557-580
Thermo Fisher Scientific - S.O.C. Medium product information (15544034)
QIAGEN FAQ - What is the recipe for SOC medium?

recipes.name: 50% (v/v) Glycerol Stock Solution for Bacterial Cryostocks
recipes.summary: Sterile glycerol solution mixed 1:1 with an overnight culture to make -80 C bacterial cryostocks.

components.name: Glycerol
components.note: Glycerol MW 92.09, density 1.26 g/mL. Glycerol is extremely viscous - pipette slowly with a cut tip or weigh it (50 mL = 63.0 g). 'Molecular biology grade' / >=99% is standard.
components.hazard: Low hazard. Glycerol is hygroscopic; keep the bottle closed so the concentration does not drift.

recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min in a loosely capped screw-cap glass bottle
recipes.storage: room temperature
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (indefinite if it remains sterile and clear)
notes: Standard use: mix 500 uL of dense overnight culture with 500 uL of this 50% solution in a cryovial, vortex, and freeze immediately at -80 C. Final glycerol concentration = 25%.
If your lab prefers 15% final glycerol (also very common and arguably better for long-term viability), make a 30% (v/v) solution instead and mix 1:1, or add 150 uL of 50% glycerol to 350 uL culture.
Acceptable final range is 15-25% (v/v). Below ~10% ice crystals damage cells; above ~30% glycerol itself becomes inhibitory on thaw.
NEVER thaw a cryostock to sample it. Scrape frozen chips off the surface with a sterile tip or loop and return the vial to -80 C immediately - repeated freeze/thaw is the main cause of dead stocks.
-80 C stocks last many years. -20 C storage is NOT equivalent; viability falls off within months because -20 C freezers cycle through the glycerol/water eutectic.
Autoclaving is non-negotiable - glycerol is not self-sterilising and unautoclaved stock solution is a classic source of contaminated cryostocks.

sources.title: Addgene Protocol - How to Create a Bacterial Glycerol Stock
protocols.io - Creating Bacterial Glycerol Stocks for Long-term Storage
MIT OpenCourseWare 7.15 - Making bacterial glycerol stocks

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: Cold Spring Harbor URL pdb.prot101196 returned HTTP 404; replaced with a live protocols.io bacterial glycerol stock protocol.

recipes.name: Mammalian Cell Freezing Medium (10% DMSO in FBS)
recipes.summary: Standard cryoprotectant medium for banking adherent and suspension mammalian cell lines in liquid nitrogen.

components.name: DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide)
components.hazard: DMSO carries dissolved compounds straight through skin and most glove materials. Use fresh, unopened or well-sealed stock (it absorbs water), handle in the hood with nitrile gloves. DMSO is also cytotoxic at room temperature - do not leave cells sitting in it.

recipes.solvent: fetal bovine serum (FBS), heat-inactivated if that is your lab standard - bring to final volume
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter using a DMSO-compatible membrane - PTFE or nylon. Do NOT reach for PES or cellulose acetate, which are the usual cell-culture default but are rated for aqueous/mild-organic use only and are not DMSO-compatible. (At 10% DMSO in serum a PES membrane will usually survive the contact time; PTFE/nylon is the correct choice and is mandatory if you ever filter neat DMSO.) DMSO also attacks polystyrene filter housings and leaches plasticiser - use a polypropylene-housed unit and glass or polypropylene receiving vessels, never polystyrene.
recipes.storage: 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: prepare fresh on the day of freezing where possible; up to 1 month at 4 C
recipes.hazards: DMSO is a powerful skin-penetration enhancer. Never handle it with bare hands near other chemicals.
notes: Alternative that is equally standard: complete growth medium + 20% FBS + 10% DMSO. Use this when FBS is scarce or the line is serum-sensitive.
Serum-free lines: use a commercial serum-free cryomedium, or the base medium + 10% DMSO. Some sensitive lines do better at 5% DMSO (see Frontiers 2021 on Treg products).
Target 1 x 10^6 to 5 x 10^6 cells/mL for routine lines (ATCC/Gibco guidance goes up to 1 x 10^7 for serum-containing medium).
Add the freezing medium ICE-COLD, aliquot fast, and get vials into the controlled-rate cooler within 10 minutes - DMSO is toxic to cells at room temperature.

Cool at 1 C/min (isopropanol 'Mr Frosty' style container or a controlled-rate freezer) to -80 C overnight, then transfer to liquid-nitrogen vapour phase for long-term storage. Cells left at -80 C degrade over months.
Thaw FAST (37 C water bath, ~1-2 min) and dilute out the DMSO immediately in 9-10 volumes of pre-warmed medium; pellet and resuspend in fresh medium.

sources.title: Thermo Fisher / Gibco Cell Culture Basics - Freezing Cells protocol
Abcam - Cryopreservation of mammalian cell lines protocol
ATCC - Animal Cell Culture Guide, cryopreservation section

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: filter membrane guidance previously described PES as DMSO-compatible. PES and cellulose acetate are rated for aqueous/mild-organic use and are not DMSO-compatible; PTFE and nylon are. Guidance rewritten, with a note that PES generally survives dilute (10%) DMSO but must not be used for neat DMSO.

recipes.name: Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS), 1x
aka: Dulbecco's PBS without calcium and magnesium
recipes.summary: The default isotonic wash and dilution buffer for cell biology, immunostaining and fixation.

components.name: Disodium hydrogen phosphate
components.note: = 1.42 g/L. CRITICAL hydrate check: Na2HPO4*2H2O = 177.99 (1.78 g/L); Na2HPO4*7H2O = 268.07 (2.68 g/L); Na2HPO4*12H2O = 358.14 (3.58 g/L). The Cold Spring Harbor 10x PBS recipe specifies the HEPTAHYDRATE at 25.6 g/L for the 10x stock.

components.name: Potassium dihydrogen phosphate
components.form: KH2PO4 (anhydrous - KH2PO4 has no common hydrate)

recipes.adjustPHWith: HCl / NaOH (usually needs almost nothing)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 20 min, or 0.22 um filter
recipes.storage: room temperature (sterile) or 4 C
recipes.shelfLife: 6 months; discard if cloudy or if any precipitate or growth appears
notes: For a 10x stock, multiply every mass by 10: 80 g NaCl, 2 g KCl, 14.2 g Na2HPO4 (anhydrous), 2.45 g KH2PO4 per litre. Do NOT pH the 10x stock to 7.4 - it will sit near pH 6.8 and comes to 7.4 on dilution. pHing the concentrate then diluting overshoots.
The 10x stock precipitates at 4 C. Store it at room temperature and re-dissolve any crystals by warming before diluting.

This is CALCIUM- and MAGNESIUM-FREE (like DPBS -/-), which is what you want for trypsinisation washes. For DPBS +/+ add 0.9 mM CaCl2 (from CaCl2*2H2O, MW 147.01) and 0.5 mM MgCl2 (from MgCl2*6H2O, MW 203.30) - but never add Ca/Mg to a concentrated stock, they precipitate with phosphate.
PBS has essentially no buffering capacity above pH 8 and is a poor choice for reactions that generate acid.
Autoclaved PBS can drop ~0.2 pH units; re-check if the application is pH-critical.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - 10X PBS recipe
Sambrook & Russell, Molecular Cloning 3rd ed., Appendix 1 - Buffers and Solutions
Sigma-Aldrich - 10X Phosphate-Buffered Saline preparation

recipes.name: 4% Paraformaldehyde (PFA) in PBS
recipes.summary: Standard crosslinking fixative for immunofluorescence, immunohistochemistry and cell/tissue morphology preservation.

components.name: Paraformaldehyde
components.form: EM-grade paraformaldehyde powder, (CH2O)n - a polymer that depolymerises to formaldehyde on heating in alkali
components.hazard: IARC Group 1 human carcinogen and respiratory sensitiser. Weigh and dissolve ONLY in a chemical fume hood. Do not use methanol-stabilised formalin as a substitute where methanol interferes (e.g. many phospho-epitopes and GFP).

components.name: Sodium hydroxide
components.note: = 1 mL of 1 M NaOH per 100 mL. This is the depolymerising catalyst - the powder will not dissolve without it. Add before heating.

components.name: PBS
components.note: = 10 mL of 10x PBS per 100 mL. Add ONLY after the PFA has fully dissolved and the solution has cooled - adding PBS to hot alkaline PFA causes phosphate precipitation.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (dissolve PFA in ~half the final volume, then bring to final volume after pH adjustment)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 M HCl (roughly 1 mL per 100 mL to come back down from the NaOH)
recipes.sterilization: 0.45 um filter (removes undissolved particulate; this is clarification, not sterilisation)
recipes.storage: aliquots at -20 C, protected from light; 4 C for no more than about 1 week
recipes.shelfLife: several months at -20 C. Avoid repeated freeze/thaw - re-polymerisation gives cloudy solution and poor, patchy fixation
recipes.hazards: Formaldehyde is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen (nasopharyngeal cancer; strong evidence for leukemia) and a respiratory/skin sensitiser. Fume hood mandatory for weighing and heating. Nitrile gloves, safety glasses, lab coat. Formaldehyde-contaminated waste must go to hazardous waste - never down the drain. OSHA PEL 0.75 ppm 8-h TWA, STEL 2 ppm.
notes: Procedure for 100 mL: add 4 g PFA to ~50 mL water in a fume hood; add 1 mL 1 M NaOH; stir on a 60 C heat block/stirrer until the milky suspension goes clear (10-60 min). DO NOT EXCEED 60 C.
Then add 10 mL 10x PBS, cool to room temperature, adjust to pH 7.4 with ~1 mL 1 M HCl, bring to 100 mL with water and filter through 0.45 um.
If the solution never fully clears, add NaOH dropwise - but a stubbornly cloudy prep usually means old, oxidised PFA powder. Buy small bottles.
Typical fixation: 10-20 min at room temperature for cultured cells; longer/overnight at 4 C for tissue. Over-fixation masks epitopes and may require antigen retrieval.

Formalin (37% formaldehyde with 10-15% methanol) diluted 1:10 gives '10% neutral buffered formalin' = ~4% formaldehyde and is a legitimate substitute for histology, but the methanol disrupts some epitopes, GFP fluorescence and membrane structure - freshly depolymerised PFA is preferred for immunofluorescence.
8% PFA stock is convenient: make at double strength and dilute 1:1 with 2x buffer or medium for live-cell fixation.

sources.title: Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Paraformaldehyde Stock Solution (4% in PBS)
Cold Spring Harbor Protocols - Paraformaldehyde in PBS
R&D Systems - Protocol for Making a 4% Formaldehyde Solution in PBS
OSHA - Formaldehyde Hazard Recognition

recipes.name: 70% (v/v) Ethanol Disinfectant
recipes.summary: General-purpose surface and glove disinfectant for benches, biosafety cabinets and equipment.

components.name: Ethanol
components.note: IF YOUR STOCK IS 95% (190 proof), change stockConc to 95 - the calculator then gives 736.8 mL of 95% per litre instead of 700 mL. Reagent-alcohol/denatured stock is fine for disinfection and much cheaper.
components.hazard: Highly flammable, flash point ~13 C. Keep away from Bunsen burners and hot plates. Never spray ethanol near an open flame or into a running biosafety cabinet with an ignition source.

recipes.solvent: deionized or distilled water (bring to final volume)
recipes.sterilization: none required for surface use; 0.22 um filter if it will contact sterile material
recipes.storage: room temperature in a closed, labelled, flammables-rated container away from ignition sources
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year in a sealed container; spray bottles evaporate and drift in concentration - top up or remake every few months
recipes.hazards: Highly flammable liquid and vapour (GHS H225). Store in a flammables cabinet if you keep more than a working quantity. Ethanol vapour accumulates inside a closed biosafety cabinet - do not flame immediately after spraying.
notes: MEASURE BY VOLUME AND BRING TO VOLUME - do not add 300 mL of water to 700 mL of ethanol. Ethanol/water mixing CONTRACTS by ~3-4%, so 700 mL ethanol + 300 mL water settles at roughly 965 mL, and the same 700 mL of ethanol in that smaller volume works out to about 72-73% (v/v), not 70%. Contraction makes the additive shortcut too STRONG, not too weak. It still lands inside the effective 60-90% band, so it disinfects, but it is not the stated concentration - bring to volume in a graduated cylinder.

70% works better than 95% or absolute ethanol because the water is required: it slows evaporation (giving real contact time) and lets alcohol penetrate the cell before proteins coagulate into a protective shell. CDC recommends the 60-90% band.
Allow at least 1 minute of visible wet contact time. Ethanol that flashes off in 5 seconds has not disinfected anything.
NOT sporicidal - ethanol does not kill Bacillus or Clostridioides difficile spores, and is unreliable against non-enveloped viruses. Use fresh 1:10 bleach for spore or blood-borne spills.
Isopropanol at 70% (v/v) is an accepted equivalent for surfaces and is often preferred for electronics; it is a poorer choice where residue matters.

sources.title: CDC - Guideline for Disinfection and Sterilization in Healthcare Facilities: Chemical Disinfectants (Alcohol)
WHO Guidelines on Hand Hygiene in Health Care - alcohol-based formulations

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: the volume-contraction note had the direction of the error backwards. It previously claimed that adding 300 mL water to 700 mL ethanol yields ~68%. Contraction shrinks the final volume to ~965 mL, so the same 700 mL of ethanol is at ~72-73%, i.e. too strong, not too weak. The practical instruction (bring to volume) is unchanged.

recipes.name: 10% Bleach (1:10 Household Bleach) Disinfectant
recipes.summary: Broad-spectrum sporicidal disinfectant for biological spills, liquid waste decontamination and BSL-2 surface decon.

components.name: Sodium hypochlorite (household bleach)
components.form: unscented household bleach, 5.25% sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl, MW 74.44)
components.note: CHECK YOUR BOTTLE. Household bleach is sold at 5-9% NaOCl and concentrated versions at 8.25% are now common. Set stockConc to the label value - 1 part bleach + 9 parts water is only 'a 1:10' by volume, and the resulting ppm depends entirely on the starting strength. At 5.25% stock, a 1:10 dilution = ~5250 ppm available chlorine.
components.hazard: Corrosive to skin, eyes and metal. NEVER mix with acids (releases chlorine gas) or with ammonia/quaternary ammonium cleaners (releases chloramine gas). Never add bleach to formaldehyde- or azide-containing waste.

recipes.solvent: tap or deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.storage: opaque or closed container, room temperature, away from light and metal
recipes.shelfLife: MAKE FRESH DAILY. Diluted hypochlorite in a closed opaque container loses 40-50% of its free available chlorine in a month; in an open tray or clear bottle it decays far faster. Undiluted stock bleach itself loses ~20% potency per year.
recipes.hazards: Corrosive (GHS H314). Wear gloves and eye protection. Generates toxic chlorine gas with acids and chloramine with ammonia - never combine waste streams. Corrodes stainless steel: rinse centrifuge rotors, biosafety cabinet surfaces and instruments with water or 70% ethanol after the contact time.
notes: 1:10 (~5000 ppm) is the concentration for spills, blood, high organic load and liquid biological waste. 1:100 (~500 ppm) is adequate for routine low-soil surface wiping.
Contact time matters more than concentration: allow 10 minutes for spills and biohazard decontamination (CDC allows >=1 min for routine surface disinfection of a cleaned surface).
Organic material (serum, blood, agar, culture medium) rapidly quenches free chlorine. Absorb and remove bulk spill material first, then apply fresh bleach.
For liquid culture waste, add bleach to a final ~10% of the total volume and hold >=30 min before drain disposal (check local rules - many institutions prohibit bleach down the drain).

Bleach is one of the few routinely available reagents that reliably kills bacterial spores and non-enveloped viruses, and it destroys nucleic acid - it is the standard choice for decontaminating PCR workspaces (10% bleach, then water, then 70% ethanol).
Do not autoclave bleach-containing waste - it releases chlorine gas and destroys the autoclave chamber.

sources.title: CDC - Guideline for Disinfection and Sterilization in Healthcare Facilities: Chlorine and Chlorine Compounds
CDC - Cleaning and Disinfecting with Bleach (WASH)
CDC/NIH - Biosafety in Microbiological and Biomedical Laboratories (BMBL), 6th ed., Appendix B: Decontamination and Disinfection

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 1 - Crystal Violet (Hucker's Modification)
recipes.summary: Primary stain of the Gram stain; ammonium oxalate stabilises the dye and prevents precipitation.

components.name: Crystal violet
components.form: certified crystal violet (hexamethyl pararosaniline chloride), C25H30ClN3, MW 407.98, CI 42555
components.note: = 2.0 g per 100 mL final. Use a 'certified' / Biological Stain Commission-certified lot; uncertified dye lots vary in dye content and stain unpredictably. Dissolve this in the ethanol portion (Solution A).
components.hazard: Crystal violet is a suspected mutagen and stains skin and clothing persistently. Gloves; avoid dust.

components.name: Ammonium oxalate monohydrate
components.note: = 0.8 g per 100 mL final. Published Hucker recipes say simply 'ammonium oxalate'; the monohydrate is the normal commercial form and is what the 0.8 g figure assumes. Dissolve in the water portion (Solution B).
components.hazard: Oxalate salts are harmful if swallowed (hypocalcaemia, renal oxalate deposition).

components.name: Ethanol, 95% (v/v)
components.note: = 20 mL of 95% ethanol per 100 mL final. This is the Solution A solvent.
components.hazard: Flammable.

recipes.solvent: distilled water (bring to final volume; ~80 mL per 100 mL forms Solution B)
recipes.storage: room temperature in a tightly stoppered amber or opaque bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year (Hucker's formulation is notably stable). Filter before use if any precipitate or metallic sheen develops
recipes.hazards: Crystal violet is a suspected mutagen and an irreversible stain on skin and fabric. Ammonium oxalate is harmful if swallowed. Ethanol is flammable.
notes: Preparation for 100 mL: Solution A = 2.0 g certified crystal violet in 20 mL 95% ethanol. Solution B = 0.8 g ammonium oxalate in 80 mL distilled water. Combine A and B, then LET IT STAND 24 HOURS BEFORE USE and filter through paper.
The 24-h stand is not optional folklore - it lets undissolved dye and oxalate complex fully, and skipping it is a common cause of dye crystals on the slide that look like Gram-positive cocci.
Stain a heat-fixed smear for 1 minute, then rinse gently with water.
The ammonium oxalate is the entire point of Hucker's modification versus a plain aqueous crystal violet - it prevents dye precipitation and gives more consistent Gram-positive retention.
Store away from light; crystal violet photodegrades.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol

Hucker G.J. & Conn H.J. (1928) Stain Technology 3(2) - Further Studies on a Modification of the Gram Stain (the published follow-up to Hucker & Conn, NY State Agric Exp Stn Tech Bull 93, 1923, which is the origin of the ammonium-oxalate formulation but is not available online)

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: citation title/URL mismatch. The title cited Hucker & Conn 1923 (NY State Agric Exp Stn Tech Bull 93) but the URL resolves to their 1928 Stain Technology paper "Further Studies on a Modification of the Gram Stain". Title corrected to describe the linked article and to keep the 1923 bulletin as the named origin.

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 2 - Gram's Iodine (Mordant)
recipes.summary: Iodine/iodide mordant that forms an insoluble crystal violet-iodine complex inside the cell, trapping the primary stain.

components.name: Iodine
components.note: = 1.0 g per 300 mL, the classical Hucker/BAM proportion. Iodine is essentially insoluble in water alone - it only dissolves because the iodide forms the triiodide ion.
components.hazard: Iodine vapour is corrosive to the respiratory tract and eyes; sublimes at room temperature. Weigh in a fume hood and keep the bottle tightly closed. Stains skin brown.

components.name: Potassium iodide
components.note: = 2.0 g per 300 mL. The 1:2 I2:KI mass ratio is essential - halving the KI leaves undissolved iodine and a weak mordant.

recipes.storage: AMBER or opaque tightly-stoppered bottle, room temperature, away from light and metal
recipes.shelfLife: Discard after 30 days once in routine use, or immediately if the deep brown colour fades toward yellow - a pale solution is a failed mordant and is the single most common cause of Gram-positive organisms reading as Gram-negative
recipes.hazards: Iodine is corrosive and sublimes; harmful if inhaled or swallowed. Stains everything brown. Incompatible with ammonia (forms shock-sensitive nitrogen triiodide) and with metals.
notes: Preparation: grind the iodine crystals TOGETHER WITH the KI in a dry mortar first, then add water slowly with continuous grinding until fully dissolved, then bring to volume. Adding water first leaves iodine floating undissolved for days.
Alternative equally valid preparation: dissolve the KI in a small volume of water first, add the iodine to that concentrated iodide solution (it dissolves readily), then dilute to volume.
Apply for 1 minute after the crystal violet rinse, then rinse with water.
Many clinical labs use a stabilised PVP-iodine commercial formulation, which resists the fading problem; the free-iodine recipe above is the classical one and must be monitored for colour.
Never store in a metal-capped bottle - iodine attacks the cap and the corrosion products ruin the stain.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol
Canton City Health District - Gram Stain Reagent Preparation SOP 400-001-01-03-P

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 3 - Decolorizer (95% Ethanol)
recipes.summary: Differentiating step - removes the crystal violet-iodine complex from thin-peptidoglycan Gram-negative cells but not from Gram-positives.

components.name: Ethanol
components.form: ethanol, 95% (v/v) - the FDA BAM standard decolorizer
components.note: If you buy 95% (190 proof) ethanol you can use it neat; set stockConc to 95 and finalConc to 95, or simply dispense the stock. For the faster acetone-alcohol variant see the notes.
components.hazard: Highly flammable.

recipes.solvent: deionized water (bring to final volume; not needed if starting from 95% stock)
recipes.storage: room temperature, tightly closed, away from ignition sources
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year sealed; evaporation from a dropper bottle concentrates it over time
recipes.hazards: Highly flammable (GHS H225). Acetone-containing variants are more volatile still and are also eye irritants. Keep away from flames - Gram staining is often done next to a Bunsen burner used for heat fixing.
notes: THIS IS THE STEP THAT DECIDES THE RESULT. Over-decolorization turns Gram-positives pink; under-decolorization turns Gram-negatives purple. It is timing-critical and thickness-critical, not recipe-critical.
With 95% ethanol: apply drop-by-drop down a tilted slide until the runoff is no longer blue - typically 15-30 seconds - then rinse IMMEDIATELY with water to stop the reaction.

Common faster alternative: acetone-alcohol, equal parts 100% acetone and 95% ethanol (50:50 v/v). This decolorizes in about 5-10 seconds. It is more forgiving than pure acetone and faster than pure ethanol, and is the formulation most clinical labs prefer.
Pure acetone alone is too aggressive - it strips Gram-positives - and is not recommended.

Always run a known Gram-positive and a known Gram-negative control (e.g. Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli) alongside unknowns; the control smear is what tells you your decolorization timing is right.
Thick smears over-retain dye and read falsely Gram-positive. Make thin, evenly spread, fully air-dried smears.
Note that old (>18-24 h) Gram-positive cultures decolorize more readily and can read Gram-variable - always Gram stain fresh growth.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol
Bartholomew J.W. (1962) Stain Technology 37(3):139-155 - Variables influencing results, and the precise definition of steps in Gram staining as a means of standardizing the results obtained

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: the Bartholomew 1962 citation pointed at PMID 52916, which is actually Adams E. (1975) "Studies in gram staining", Stain Technol 50:227-31. Replaced with the correct Bartholomew 1962 article, Stain Technology 37(3):139-155, DOI 10.3109/10520296209117723.

recipes.name: Gram Stain Reagent 4 - Safranin Counterstain (Working Solution)
recipes.summary: Counterstain that renders decolorized (Gram-negative) cells pink-red; Gram-positives remain purple.

components.name: Safranin O
components.form: certified Safranin O, C20H19ClN4, MW 350.84, CI 50240
components.note: = 0.25 g per 100 mL of WORKING solution. Prepared in practice as a stock (2.5 g Safranin O in 100 mL 95% ethanol) diluted 1:10 in water, which lands at exactly this concentration.
components.hazard: Suspected mutagen; stains skin and fabric.

components.name: Ethanol, 95% (v/v)
components.note: = 10 mL of 95% ethanol per 100 mL working solution, carried over from the 1:10 dilution of the alcoholic stock.
components.hazard: Flammable.

recipes.storage: room temperature, tightly stoppered bottle, away from light
recipes.shelfLife: stock solution 1 year; working solution 6 months. Filter if precipitate appears
recipes.hazards: Safranin O is a suspected mutagen and a persistent stain. Ethanol content makes it flammable.
notes: Two-step preparation as published: STOCK = 2.5 g certified Safranin O dissolved in 100 mL 95% ethanol. WORKING = 10 mL stock + 90 mL distilled water. The component list above already describes the finished working solution, so a calculator scaling to any volume gives the working strength directly.
Apply for 30 seconds to 1 minute after the water rinse that follows decolorization, then rinse and blot (do not rub) dry.

Some labs use 0.1% basic fuchsin instead of safranin as the counterstain. Basic fuchsin stains faint Gram-negative organisms (Haemophilus, Legionella, Bacteroides, Campylobacter) far better and is preferred in clinical microbiology for those targets.
Do not over-counterstain - excessive safranin can bleed into Gram-positive cells and make them look Gram-variable.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R32: Gram Stain
American Society for Microbiology (ASM) - Gram Stain Protocol

recipes.name: Loeffler's Methylene Blue Stain
recipes.summary: Simple metachromatic stain for bacterial morphology, Corynebacterium metachromatic granules, and as a counterstain in acid-fast and capsule staining.

components.name: Methylene blue (trihydrate)
components.form: methylene blue chloride trihydrate, C16H18ClN3S*3H2O, MW 373.90, CI 52015
components.note: = 0.30 g per 130 mL of finished stain. If your bottle is ANHYDROUS methylene blue (MW 319.85), use 1.98 g/L instead of 2.31 g/L for the same molar dye content. Most Biological Stain Commission-certified product is the trihydrate.
components.hazard: Harmful if swallowed; stains skin and fabric intensely. Methylene blue is a MAO inhibitor and a serotonin-syndrome risk in clinical contexts - not a bench issue at these amounts, but treat as harmful.

components.name: Ethanol, 95% (v/v)
components.note: = 30 mL of 95% ethanol per 130 mL of finished stain. Dissolve the methylene blue in this ethanol first - it does not dissolve well directly in the dilute KOH.
components.hazard: Flammable.

components.name: Potassium hydroxide
components.note: = 0.01 g per 130 mL, i.e. the 100 mL of 0.01% (w/v) aqueous KOH called for in the classical recipe. This is a very small mass - weigh it as 10 mL of a 0.1% (1 g/L) KOH solution rather than trying to weigh 10 mg.
components.hazard: KOH solid is corrosive; at 0.01% the finished stain is not.

recipes.storage: room temperature, tightly stoppered bottle
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year or more - Loeffler's is very stable and reportedly improves with age as the dye slowly oxidises to azure B, which improves metachromasia
recipes.hazards: Harmful if swallowed. Stains skin, clothing and benchtops permanently. Ethanol content makes it flammable.
notes: Classical preparation: dissolve 0.3 g methylene blue in 30 mL 95% ethanol; separately prepare 100 mL of 0.01% (w/v) aqueous KOH; combine. Final volume 130 mL. The component list above is scaled to any final volume you like.
Use: flood a heat-fixed smear for 1 minute, rinse gently with tap water, blot dry, examine under oil immersion.
Chief clinical use is demonstrating the metachromatic (Babes-Ernst) volutin granules of Corynebacterium diphtheriae, which stain reddish-purple against the blue cell body.
Also standard as the counterstain in the Ziehl-Neelsen / Kinyoun acid-fast stains and for capsule (negative) staining backgrounds.
A plain 1% aqueous methylene blue (no alcohol, no KOH) is a different reagent - fine for simple morphology, but it does NOT give reliable metachromasia. Do not substitute.
Methylene blue at ~0.1% is also the standard stain for haemocytometer yeast viability counts (methylene blue reduction test) - dead yeast stain blue.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R45: Methylene Blue Stain (Loeffler's)
Hardy Diagnostics - Methylene Blue, Loeffler's, technical data sheet
Pro-Lab Diagnostics - Loeffler's Methylene Blue instructions for use

recipes.name: Trypan Blue Solution, 0.4% (w/v)
recipes.summary: Dye-exclusion viability stain for haemocytometer and automated cell counting - dead cells with compromised membranes stain blue.

components.name: Trypan blue
components.form: trypan blue, tetrasodium salt, C34H24N6Na4O14S4, MW 960.81, CAS 72-57-1, CI 23850
components.note: MW 960.81 is the TETRASODIUM SALT, which is what CAS 72-57-1 / Sigma T6146 / T8154 supply. The figure 872.88 that circulates in some recipe tables is the free acid (C34H28N6O14S4) and does not match the tetrasodium formula. The recipe is weighed as % (w/v), so the MW is reference-only and no mass changes.
components.hazard: Trypan blue is a suspected teratogen and possible carcinogen (it is a benzidine-derived azo dye and can release o-tolidine). Wear gloves; avoid weighing the dry powder outside a hood or balance enclosure. Dispose as chemical waste.

components.name: Sodium chloride
components.note: = 8.1 g/L, i.e. 139 mM - the solution is deliberately isotonic so that cells do not lyse or shrink during the count.

components.name: Potassium phosphate, dibasic
components.note: = 0.6 g/L. If using the trihydrate K2HPO4*3H2O (MW 228.22) use 0.786 g/L for the same molar amount.

recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter (essential - trypan blue readily forms dye aggregates that look exactly like small dead cells under the microscope)
recipes.storage: room temperature or 4 C, protected from light
recipes.shelfLife: 1 year; RE-FILTER before use if you see any particulate - dye precipitate is the number-one cause of falsely low viability readings
recipes.hazards: Suspected teratogen and possible carcinogen (benzidine-derived azo dye). Gloves required. Collect as chemical waste; do not pour down the drain.
notes: This is the Sigma T8154 / standard cell-culture formulation: 0.4% trypan blue in 0.81% NaCl and 0.06% dibasic potassium phosphate. A 0.4% solution simply made up in PBS is a widely used and acceptable substitute.

Standard count: mix cell suspension 1:1 with 0.4% trypan blue (i.e. dilution factor 2), load the haemocytometer, and count within 3-5 MINUTES. Trypan blue is itself cytotoxic - viability falls visibly if you leave the mix sitting, and a 15-minute-old mix will read artificially low.
Viability (%) = (unstained cells / total cells) x 100. Cells/mL = (mean count per 1 mm square) x dilution factor x 10^4.
Serum proteins bind trypan blue and cause high background staining. Wash cells into PBS or serum-free medium before counting if the background is heavy.

Dye exclusion measures MEMBRANE INTEGRITY, not metabolic health. Early-apoptotic cells still exclude trypan blue and will be scored as viable. For apoptosis use annexin V/PI; for metabolic viability use MTT/resazurin.
A 0.2% working strength is sometimes used for fragile primary cells to reduce dye toxicity.

sources.title: Sigma-Aldrich - Trypan Blue solution 0.4%, product T8154 specification
Strober W. (2015) Curr Protoc Immunol - Trypan Blue Exclusion Test of Cell Viability
Thermo Fisher / Gibco Cell Culture Basics - Counting Cells with a Hemocytometer

corrections: CORRECTED 2026-07-20: molecular weight of trypan blue changed from 872.88 to 960.81 g/mol. 872.88 is the free acid C34H28N6O14S4; the stated formula C34H24N6Na4O14S4 and CAS 72-57-1 are the tetrasodium salt at 960.81. No weighed mass changes (the dye is specified as % w/v).

recipes.name: MTT Stock Solution, 5 mg/mL
recipes.summary: Tetrazolium stock for the MTT colorimetric cell viability / proliferation / cytotoxicity assay.

components.name: MTT (thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide)
components.note: = 5 mg/mL = 12.07 mM. Dissolve by vortexing or brief sonication; it goes into PBS readily but can be slow.
components.hazard: MTT is acutely toxic if swallowed and is mutagenic in bacterial assays (Ames-positive). Formazan-containing waste and DMSO solubilisation waste must go to chemical hazardous waste.

recipes.solvent: sterile PBS, pH 7.4 (phenol-red-free serum-free medium or HBSS also work; avoid phenol red, which absorbs near the read wavelength)
recipes.sterilization: 0.22 um filter AFTER dissolving (removes undissolved dye and sterilises; the stock goes into live cultures)
recipes.storage: -20 C in single-use amber or foil-wrapped aliquots, PROTECTED FROM LIGHT
recipes.shelfLife: at least 6 months at -20 C. At 4 C use within a few days only. Discard if the yellow solution develops any blue/purple tinge - that is spontaneous formazan and it wrecks the assay baseline
recipes.hazards: Acutely toxic if swallowed (GHS H301) and mutagenic in vitro. Handle powder in a hood or enclosed balance; gloves. Collect all MTT and formazan/DMSO waste as hazardous chemical waste.
notes: Working use: add MTT stock to 10% of the culture volume - i.e. 10 uL of 5 mg/mL stock per 100 uL well - giving a final concentration of ~0.5 mg/mL. Incubate 2-4 h at 37 C.

Then remove the medium and dissolve the purple formazan crystals in 100-150 uL DMSO (or acidified isopropanol: 0.04 M HCl in isopropanol, or 10% SDS in 0.01 M HCl for overnight solubilisation without aspirating).
Read absorbance at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630-690 nm to subtract plate/debris background.
ALWAYS include medium-only (no cell) blanks - MTT reduces slowly and non-enzymatically in medium, and the blank subtraction matters.
Phenol red absorbs around 560 nm. Use phenol-red-free medium for the MTT incubation, or make sure blanks contain identical medium.

Known interferences: ascorbate, polyphenols, N-acetylcysteine and other reductants reduce MTT chemically and give false 'viability'. Compounds that alter mitochondrial activity without killing cells also distort the readout - MTT reports metabolic activity, not cell number.
MTT formazan is water-insoluble (unlike MTS/XTT/WST-1), which is why the solubilisation step is required; the newer tetrazolium salts avoid it but cost more.

Sigma-Aldrich - Thiazolyl Blue Tetrazolium Bromide product information sheet M2128
Mosmann T. (1983) J Immunol Methods 65:55-63 - Rapid colorimetric assay for cellular growth and survival
Abcam - MTT assay protocol

recipes.name: Butterfield's Phosphate-Buffered Dilution Water (Dilution Blanks)
recipes.summary: Standard non-nutritive diluent for 10-fold serial dilutions in viable plate counts and OD-to-cfu calibration.

components.name: Potassium dihydrogen phosphate
components.form: KH2PO4, MW 136.09 - stock is 34.0 g/L (0.25 M), adjusted to pH 7.2 with 1 N NaOH
components.note: = 1.25 mL of the 0.25 M pH-7.2 stock per litre of working dilution water. The working solution is deliberately extremely dilute: it buffers pH without providing nutrients or osmotic shock.

recipes.solvent: distilled or deionized water (bring to final volume)
recipes.adjustPHWith: 1 N NaOH (pH the 0.25 M STOCK to 7.2, not the working dilution)
recipes.sterilization: autoclave 121 C, 15 min after dispensing into bottles/tubes
recipes.storage: room temperature; refrigerate for long storage
recipes.shelfLife: Stock solution: 1 year at 4 C. Dispensed sterile blanks: 6 months, but discard any blank whose volume has changed by more than 1 mL from evaporation - the dilution factor is the whole point
notes: Stock: dissolve 34.0 g KH2PO4 in 500 mL water, adjust to pH 7.2 with 1 N NaOH, bring to 1 litre. Store at 4 C.
Working dilution water: 1.25 mL stock per litre of water. Dispense into bottles at 90 +/- 1 mL or tubes at 9 +/- 0.2 mL, then autoclave.

Standard 10-fold series: 1 mL sample into 9 mL blank = 10^-1; carry 1 mL forward each time. Alternatively 11 mL into 99 mL = 10^-2 per step. VORTEX 25 times through a 30 cm arc (or 7 s vortex) between every step - incomplete mixing is the dominant error in plate counts.
Use a FRESH sterile pipette tip for every transfer. Reusing a tip carries over cells on the outside and inflates the count by up to a full log.
Plate 0.1 mL (spread plate) or 1 mL (pour plate) and count plates bearing 25-250 colonies (FDA BAM) or 30-300 (Standard Methods). cfu/mL = (colony count) / (volume plated in mL x dilution factor).

COMMON ALTERNATIVES, all acceptable: 0.85% (w/v) NaCl saline (8.5 g/L NaCl, MW 58.44); 0.1% (w/v) buffered peptone water; 1x PBS. Butterfield's is the FDA/AOAC reference diluent for food microbiology. Plain distilled water is NOT acceptable - it osmotically lyses cells and undercounts by 20-50% within minutes.

OD-to-cfu rule of thumb for E. coli: OD600 of 1.0 is roughly 8 x 10^8 cfu/mL, but this is strain- and spectrophotometer-path-length-specific and MUST be calibrated for your own instrument by plate count. Keep readings below OD600 0.6 or dilute - the Beer-Lambert relationship goes non-linear above that.
Hold total elapsed time from first dilution to plating to under 20 minutes, and work at room temperature.

sources.title: FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) R11: Butterfield's Phosphate-Buffered Dilution Water
FDA Bacteriological Analytical Manual (BAM) Chapter 3: Aerobic Plate Count
Hardy Diagnostics - Butterfield's Phosphate Buffer technical data sheet (Cat. D590)
